Choice of Structure.front:
Priya has been running a graphic design business as a sole trader for two years. She is considering incorporating as a private limited company. Her main concern is that a large client relationship has recently gone wrong and she fears a negligence claim. She earns approximately £60,000 profit per year.

Advise Priya on the key legal and tax implications of incorporation compared with continuing as a sole trader.
Choice of Structure.answer:
Liability: As a sole trader, Priya has unlimited personal liability — if the negligence claim succeeds, her personal assets (home, savings) are at risk. Incorporating creates a separate legal person (Salomon [1897]); Priya's liability as shareholder would be limited to the amount unpaid on her shares. However, incorporation does not protect against personal negligence — if she personally committed the negligent act, she remains personally liable in tort regardless of the corporate structure. Professional indemnity insurance remains essential.

Tax: As a sole trader, Priya pays income tax (20%/40%/45% on profits) plus Class 2 and Class 4 NIC. At £60,000 profit, she will be a higher-rate taxpayer on part of her income. As a company director/shareholder, profits are subject to corporation tax (25% main rate or 19% small profits rate — ⚠️ verify current thresholds). She can extract income as a salary (up to the NIC threshold) and dividends (taxed at lower rates than salary, and not subject to NIC). This can be tax-efficient but requires careful structuring.

Administration: A company requires registration at Companies House, annual accounts filing, confirmation statement, and greater administrative obligations. Priya should weigh the compliance cost against the tax and liability benefits. ⚠️ Advise her to take specialist tax advice on the optimal salary/dividend split.
Choice of Structure.summary_line: INCORPORATION · LIMITED LIABILITY · PERSONAL TORT LIABILITY REMAINS · CT vs INCOME TAX · SALARY + DIVIDEND EXTRACTION

Choice of Structure.front:
Dan, Eve and Finn want to start a property development business together. Dan will contribute £200,000 capital and act as a sleeping investor. Eve and Finn will manage the business day-to-day. They anticipate significant profits in years 3–5 and want to be able to sell the business attractively at that point. They are concerned about personal liability and want the business's financial affairs to remain private.

Advise on the most suitable business structure, identifying the key advantages and disadvantages of the realistic options.
Choice of Structure.answer:
The realistic options are: ordinary partnership, LLP, or private limited company.

Ordinary partnership: Simple to set up, no public filing. However, all partners (including Dan as sleeping investor) have unlimited personal liability — unacceptable given his passive role and the risks of property development. Not recommended.

LLP: Separate legal personality and limited liability for all members — protects Dan. Eve and Finn as managers can still be personally liable for their own negligent acts. Taxed on a look-through basis (each member pays income tax on their share of profits). Must file accounts at Companies House — privacy concern is not fully addressed. Transferring membership interests on a sale is possible but less straightforward than a share sale. A moderate option.

Private limited company: Separate legal personality; limited liability for all shareholders. Dan can be a passive investor/shareholder without liability beyond his shares. Shares are easily transferred on a sale (desirable for exit). Subject to corporation tax on profits; dividends and salaries can be tax-efficiently structured. Must file accounts publicly — privacy is limited. Preferred for exit flexibility and investor protection.

Recommendation: A private limited company best meets their needs for limited liability, exit flexibility, and investor separation. A shareholders' agreement should be drafted to govern Dan's rights as a passive investor and to set out Eve and Finn's management authority. The privacy concern cannot be fully resolved with any UK registered structure. ⚠️ Tax advice on extraction strategy should be sought separately.
Choice of Structure.summary_line: PARTNERSHIP · LLP · PRIVATE COMPANY · LIMITED LIABILITY · EXIT FLEXIBILITY · LOOK-THROUGH TAX · SHAREHOLDERS' AGREEMENT

Corporate Personality and Veil Piercing.front: In what circumstances will a court pierce (or lift) the corporate veil established by Salomon v Salomon?
Corporate Personality and Veil Piercing.answer: The principle of separate corporate personality (Salomon v Salomon [1897]) is fundamental and courts are reluctant to pierce the veil. The Supreme Court in Prest v Petrodel Resources [2013] confirmed that true veil-piercing (treating the company's acts as those of its controller) is a narrow doctrine, applicable only where a person under an existing legal obligation or liability deliberately interposes a company to evade that obligation. Separate from true piercing, courts may 'look behind' the veil without piercing it using other principles (e.g. agency, sham, or statutory attribution). Statutory exceptions include: fraudulent trading (CA 2006 s.993 / IA 1986 s.213); wrongful trading (IA 1986 s.214); director disqualification. ⚠️ The precise scope of true veil-piercing post-Prest remains contested — recommend verifying against current academic commentary.
Corporate Personality and Veil Piercing.summary_line: SALOMON · SEPARATE PERSONALITY · PREST v PETRODEL [2013] · NARROW DOCTRINE · EVASION OF OBLIGATION · STATUTORY EXCEPTIONS

Corporate Personality and Veil Piercing.front: Distinguish between 'concealment' and 'evasion' as identified by the Supreme Court in Prest v Petrodel Resources [2013]. Why is this distinction critical for understanding when a court will (and will not) pierce the corporate veil?
Corporate Personality and Veil Piercing.answer:
In Prest v Petrodel Resources [2013] (SC), Lord Sumption drew a fundamental distinction between two categories of case in which courts appear to 'pierce' the corporate veil:

1. CONCEALMENT PRINCIPLE
The court looks behind the corporate form not to pierce it, but to discover the true facts that the corporate structure conceals. This is not true veil-piercing — the separate legal personality is not disregarded. The court simply investigates the reality behind the corporate structure. Example: tracing assets held by a company which are in truth beneficially owned by the individual. The result in Prest itself was reached on this basis (resulting trust), not on true piercing.

2. EVASION PRINCIPLE
True veil-piercing. This applies only where a person is under an existing legal obligation or liability and deliberately interposes a company to evade that obligation. The court will then pierce the veil to hold the person directly liable. This is a narrow and exceptional jurisdiction.

WHY THE DISTINCTION MATTERS
• Concealment is relatively common — courts can investigate behind the corporate structure whenever necessary to find the true facts.
• Evasion (true piercing) is rare — it requires a pre-existing legal obligation and deliberate evasion. It cannot be used merely because a result seems 'unjust' or because the parties are connected.

SQE EXAM TRAP: Many exam questions present concealment scenarios (e.g. assets held by a company that are really the individual's) and ask whether the veil has been 'pierced'. The correct answer is: the court is applying the concealment principle (not true veil-piercing). True evasion-based piercing is very rare and requires the specific conditions identified in Prest.
Corporate Personality and Veil Piercing.summary_line: PREST v PETRODEL [2013] · CONCEALMENT = LOOK BEHIND (NOT TRUE PIERCING) · EVASION = TRUE VEIL PIERCING · PRE-EXISTING OBLIGATION · NARROW · SQE EXAM TRAP

Corporate Personality and Veil Piercing.issue: Whether a company is a legal person separate from its shareholder/director, even where one individual owns almost all the shares.
Corporate Personality and Veil Piercing.rule: A company registered under the Companies Acts is a legal entity entirely distinct from its members. On incorporation it acquires a corporate personality independent of, and separate from, the personalities of its members. The 'veil of incorporation' separates the company from those who own and run it.
Corporate Personality and Veil Piercing.application: Salomon incorporated a leather business, taking back debentures secured by a floating charge as part payment. The company later failed. Unsecured creditors argued Salomon and the company were one and the same and that his debentures should be postponed. The House of Lords rejected this: the company had been validly incorporated and was a separate legal person. Salomon's secured debt ranked ahead of unsecured creditors.
Corporate Personality and Veil Piercing.conclusion: The company is not an alias or agent for its members. This principle applies even to a one-man company. It forms the foundation of all company law and the concept of limited liability. Every subsequent company law development rests on this case.
Corporate Personality and Veil Piercing.summary_line: SEPARATE LEGAL PERSONALITY · VEIL OF INCORPORATION · LIMITED LIABILITY · COMPANY ≠ MEMBERS · FOUNDATIONAL

Corporate Personality and Veil Piercing.front: Lee v Lee's Air Farming Ltd [1961]
Corporate Personality and Veil Piercing.issue: Whether the sole director and controlling shareholder of a company could simultaneously be an employee of that company, and thus covered by workers' compensation legislation.
Corporate Personality and Veil Piercing.rule: The separate legal personality of a company means that its controlling shareholder can enter into a valid contract of employment with the company. The shareholder and the company are distinct legal persons capable of contracting with one another.
Corporate Personality and Veil Piercing.application: Lee incorporated a company, held virtually all the shares, was governing director, and was also employed as chief pilot. He was killed in a crash. His widow claimed workers' compensation. The company's insurers argued Lee could not be both employer and employee. The Privy Council held that Lee and the company were separate persons — the company, as a distinct legal entity, could employ Lee on terms agreed between them.
Corporate Personality and Veil Piercing.conclusion: A sole controlling shareholder-director can be a 'worker' employed by their own company. This extends the Salomon principle: not only can a company own property and incur debts independently, it can also employ its own controller.
Corporate Personality and Veil Piercing.summary_line: SEPARATE PERSONALITY · CONTROLLING SHAREHOLDER = EMPLOYEE · SALOMON EXTENDED · PRIVY COUNCIL

Corporate Personality and Veil Piercing.issue: Whether the court would pierce the corporate veil to treat a parent company and its subsidiaries as a single economic unit, making the parent liable for the subsidiaries' tortious debts.
Corporate Personality and Veil Piercing.rule: The courts will not disregard the separate legal personality of each company in a group simply because they are under common ownership or control. There is no general 'group enterprise' doctrine in English law. The veil will only be pierced in very limited circumstances — not merely because justice might seem to require it.
Corporate Personality and Veil Piercing.application: Cape Industries sold asbestos in the US through subsidiaries. US victims obtained judgments against the subsidiaries and sought to enforce them against Cape in England, arguing the group was a single entity. The Court of Appeal refused: each company in the group was a separate legal person. The subsidiaries were not agents of Cape; Cape had deliberately structured the group to limit liability, and that was a legitimate use of the corporate form.
Corporate Personality and Veil Piercing.conclusion: The corporate veil protects each entity in a group from the other's liabilities. A parent is not automatically liable for a subsidiary's debts. Deliberate use of subsidiaries to limit exposure is not, of itself, grounds for piercing the veil.
Corporate Personality and Veil Piercing.summary_line: NO GROUP ENTERPRISE DOCTRINE · EACH COMPANY SEPARATE · VEIL NOT PIERCED · DELIBERATE STRUCTURE LEGITIMATE · COURT OF APPEAL

Corporate Personality and Veil Piercing.issue: Whether the court could pierce the corporate veil to treat company-owned properties as matrimonial assets belonging to the controlling husband in ancillary relief proceedings.
Corporate Personality and Veil Piercing.rule: True veil-piercing — treating the acts or property of a company as those of its controller — is only available where a person is under an existing legal obligation or liability and deliberately interposes a company to evade it. General unfairness or the interests of justice are not sufficient grounds. However, courts may achieve similar results through other legal principles (here, resulting trust) without piercing the veil.
Corporate Personality and Veil Piercing.application: In divorce proceedings, the husband controlled companies that held properties. The wife sought an order that the properties were his. The Supreme Court unanimously rejected veil-piercing on the facts (the husband had not used the companies to evade a pre-existing obligation). However, the Court found the properties were held on resulting trust for the husband, so ordered them transferred — reaching the same result by a different route.
Corporate Personality and Veil Piercing.conclusion: The doctrine of veil-piercing is a narrow residual doctrine. Courts should exhaust other legal mechanisms before resorting to it. The case is the leading Supreme Court authority on the limits of veil-piercing in English law. ⚠️ The precise scope of the evasion principle remains debated.
Corporate Personality and Veil Piercing.summary_line: VEIL-PIERCING NARROW · EVASION OF EXISTING OBLIGATION ONLY · RESULTING TRUST ALTERNATIVE · SUPREME COURT [2013]

Corporate Personality and Veil Piercing.front:
Anya is the sole director and 100% shareholder of Anya's Catering Ltd. The company enters into a contract to supply food for a wedding. The company fails to perform and the clients suffer loss. The company has no assets. The clients wish to sue Anya personally.

Can the clients recover from Anya personally?
Corporate Personality and Veil Piercing.answer:
Almost certainly not, on these facts. The company is a separate legal person (Salomon v Salomon [1897]). The contract was between the clients and Anya's Catering Ltd — not Anya personally. The fact that Anya owns all the shares and is the sole director does not make her personally liable for the company's contractual obligations.

The clients' remedy is against the company. The fact that the company has no assets means the clients may be unable to recover — this is a risk that the corporate form legitimately creates and which courts will not overcome simply because it is unfair on the creditors.

Veil-piercing is only available in narrow circumstances (Prest v Petrodel [2013]) — where Anya has deliberately used the company to evade a pre-existing personal obligation. On these facts, there is nothing to suggest that. If Anya personally made fraudulent misrepresentations to induce the contract, she could be personally liable in deceit — but that requires separate facts. The clients should have required a personal guarantee from Anya before contracting.
Corporate Personality and Veil Piercing.summary_line: SEPARATE LEGAL PERSONALITY · SALOMON · NO PERSONAL LIABILITY · VEIL-PIERCING NARROW · PERSONAL GUARANTEE ADVISED

Corporate Personality and Veil Piercing.front: Going deeper: Is the doctrine of corporate personality in English law too absolute? Should the courts pierce the veil more readily to prevent injustice?
Corporate Personality and Veil Piercing.answer:
THE CURRENT POSITION
Salomon v Salomon [1897] established separate corporate personality as an almost inviolable principle. Prest v Petrodel [2013] (SC) confirmed that true veil-piercing is confined to a narrow 'evasion principle': the court will only disregard the corporate form where a person is under an existing legal obligation and deliberately interposes a company to evade it. Mere unfairness, the interests of justice, or the desire to reach a 'just result' are insufficient grounds.

THE ACADEMIC DEBATE
Critics of rigidity (e.g. Ottolenghi, 'From Peeping Behind the Corporate Veil to Ignoring It Completely' [1990]) argue that a flexible, context-sensitive approach is more principled — courts should weigh the policy behind incorporation against the specific harm in each case. The rigid Salomon rule can produce deeply unjust results (e.g. tort victims of insolvent subsidiaries, as in Adams v Cape Industries [1990]). Advocates of a 'sham' exception, a 'single economic unit' doctrine, or an abuse-of-rights approach (common in EU legal systems) argue English law is an outlier.

Defenders (e.g. Mayson, French & Ryan) counter that certainty and predictability are essential commercial values: if courts pierce the veil whenever they consider it 'just', the entire edifice of limited liability investment is undermined. The legislature has already provided targeted exceptions (fraudulent and wrongful trading, CDDA 1986 disqualification) where policy requires personal liability.

POST-PREST TENSIONS
Lord Neuberger in Prest acknowledged the evasion principle is difficult to apply consistently. Subsequent cases have largely ignored true veil-piercing and instead reached results through agency, sham, trust, or statutory attribution. Some scholars (e.g. Lim, 'Salomon Reigns' [2014]) argue the doctrine of veil-piercing is effectively dead as an independent principle. Others note that concealment cases (where courts 'look behind' without technically piercing) do much of the same work.

COMPARATIVE NOTE
In Germany, the Durchgriffshaftung (piercing liability) is recognised in cases of under-capitalisation and commingling of assets. US courts apply a multi-factor 'alter ego' test more liberally than English courts. The UNIDROIT Principles do not address corporate personality directly. EU law has developed group liability concepts in competition law that English law resists in private law.

OPEN QUESTION
Should England adopt a statutory veil-piercing provision — as recommended by some Law Commission consultations — with defined criteria? Or does the current judicial approach (using other doctrines to avoid true piercing) produce acceptable outcomes without sacrificing certainty? ⚠️ No Law Commission recommendation on this is current as of 2026 — verify.
Corporate Personality and Veil Piercing.summary_line: SALOMON · PREST v PETRODEL · EVASION PRINCIPLE · ADAMS v CAPE · OTTOLENGHI · CERTAINTY vs JUSTICE · COMPARATIVE LAW · EFFECTIVELY DEAD?

Incoming / Retiring Partners.front:
Beth retires from a three-person partnership in March. In June (three months after her retirement), the remaining partners take out a bank loan in the firm's name. The bank was unaware of Beth's retirement and had dealt with her previously. The loan is not repaid. The bank seeks to recover from Beth.

Advise Beth.
Incoming / Retiring Partners.answer:
Beth's liability depends on whether she gave adequate notice of her retirement.

Under PA 1890 s.36, a retiring partner remains liable to existing creditors for debts incurred before retirement. For new debts incurred after retirement, she is only liable to persons who had dealings with the firm before her retirement and who were not given notice of her retirement.

The bank had prior dealings with Beth (and therefore with the firm). If Beth did not notify the bank of her retirement — either directly or through a notice in the London Gazette (which gives constructive notice to the world) — the bank can argue it was entitled to treat her as still a partner when the June loan was made. On those facts, Beth would be liable for the June loan.

If Beth gave actual notice to the bank of her retirement before June, she would not be liable for the post-retirement debt. Notice in the Gazette alone would not have been sufficient for the bank, as a prior dealer — actual notice is required for prior-dealing creditors. Beth's solicitors should have advised her to write to all known creditors on retirement.
Incoming / Retiring Partners.summary_line: PA 1890 S.36 · RETIRING PARTNER · PRIOR DEALERS NEED ACTUAL NOTICE · GAZETTE = CONSTRUCTIVE NOTICE FOR NEW DEALERS ONLY

LLP vs Partnership.front:
Yusuf and Zara are architects who currently trade as an ordinary partnership. A major project has just gone wrong and they face a potential £500,000 professional negligence claim. Yusuf has significant personal assets; Zara has very few. Yusuf asks whether converting to an LLP would protect him.

Advise Yusuf.
LLP vs Partnership.answer:
Converting to an LLP going forward would give members limited liability for future debts and claims — but it will not help with the existing claim.

Current claim: The negligence claim arose while Yusuf and Zara were in an ordinary partnership. Partners have unlimited joint and several personal liability for partnership debts and obligations incurred during their membership (PA 1890 ss.9, 12). Conversion to an LLP does not retrospectively protect Yusuf from the existing claim — he remains personally liable for it in full.

Future protection: An LLP under the LLPA 2000 is a body corporate with separate legal personality. Members are not personally liable for the LLP's debts beyond their agreed contribution. However, a member who personally commits a negligent act remains personally liable in tort for that act — the LLP structure does not shield a member from the consequences of their own professional negligence.

Advice: Yusuf should ensure adequate professional indemnity insurance is in place. Conversion to an LLP is worth pursuing for future protection but will not resolve the current claim.
LLP vs Partnership.summary_line: LLPA 2000 · LIMITED LIABILITY FOR FUTURE · NO RETROSPECTIVE PROTECTION · PERSONAL NEGLIGENCE LIABILITY REMAINS · INDEMNITY INSURANCE

Limited Liability Partnership (LLP).front: What are the key features of a Limited Liability Partnership (LLP) under the Limited Liability Partnerships Act 2000?
Limited Liability Partnership (LLP).answer: An LLP is a body corporate with separate legal personality (LLPA 2000 s.1). Members have limited liability — they are not personally liable for the LLP's debts beyond their agreed contribution. An LLP must be incorporated at Companies House and must file accounts and an annual confirmation statement publicly. There must be at least two designated members. An LLP is taxed as a partnership (members pay income tax on their share of profits), not corporation tax.
Limited Liability Partnership (LLP).summary_line: LLPA 2000 · SEPARATE LEGAL PERSONALITY · LIMITED LIABILITY · INCORPORATED · TAXED AS PARTNERSHIP

Limited Liability Partnership (LLP).front: What are the main differences between an ordinary partnership and an LLP?
Limited Liability Partnership (LLP).answer: Key differences: (1) Legal personality — an LLP has separate legal personality; a partnership does not. (2) Liability — LLP members have limited liability; partners have unlimited personal liability. (3) Formation — an LLP must be registered at Companies House; a partnership requires no formal registration. (4) Publicity — an LLP must file accounts and a confirmation statement; a partnership has no public filing requirements. (5) Taxation — both are taxed on a 'look-through' basis (income tax on members'/partners' share of profits). ⚠️ Recommend verifying the current tax treatment of LLP members against current HMRC guidance.
Limited Liability Partnership (LLP).summary_line: LEGAL PERSONALITY · LIABILITY · REGISTRATION · PUBLICITY · TAXATION · LLP vs PARTNERSHIP

Partnership.front: How does the Partnership Act 1890 define a partnership?
Partnership.answer: Section 1 of the Partnership Act 1890 defines a partnership as 'the relation which subsists between persons carrying on a business in common with a view of profit.' Three elements must be present: (1) a business carried on; (2) in common (i.e. jointly); (3) with a view to profit. A partnership has no separate legal personality — it is not a legal entity distinct from its partners.
Partnership.summary_line: PA 1890 S.1 · BUSINESS IN COMMON · VIEW OF PROFIT · NO SEPARATE LEGAL PERSONALITY

Partnership.front: What is the liability of partners in an ordinary partnership for partnership debts?
Partnership.answer: Partners have joint and several unlimited personal liability for all debts and obligations of the partnership incurred while they are a partner (PA 1890 s.9 and s.12). 'Joint and several' means a creditor can sue all partners together or any one partner individually for the full debt. An incoming partner is not liable for pre-existing debts; a retiring partner remains liable for debts incurred during their membership unless released by novation.
Partnership.summary_line: UNLIMITED · JOINT AND SEVERAL · PA 1890 SS.9,12 · INCOMING PARTNER NOT LIABLE FOR PAST DEBTS

Partnership.front: What is the default rule under the Partnership Act 1890 regarding sharing of profits and losses, and can this be altered?
Partnership.answer: Under PA 1890 s.24(1), partners share profits and losses equally by default, regardless of their capital contributions. This is a default rule and can be varied by agreement between the partners — either in a written partnership agreement or orally. Partners should be advised to set out profit-sharing arrangements expressly in a partnership agreement to avoid the default rules applying.
Partnership.summary_line: PA 1890 S.24(1) · EQUAL SHARES BY DEFAULT · VARIED BY AGREEMENT

Partnership.front: Under the Partnership Act 1890, in what circumstances can a partner bind the firm?
Partnership.answer: Under PA 1890 s.8, every partner is an agent of the firm and can bind all partners by acts done in the ordinary course of the firm's business, unless the partner has no authority to act in that way AND the third party knows of the restriction or does not know/believe the person to be a partner. Partners can also bind the firm by acts outside the ordinary course if specifically authorised.
Partnership.summary_line: PA 1890 S.8 · MUTUAL AGENCY · ORDINARY COURSE OF BUSINESS · THIRD PARTY KNOWLEDGE

Partnership.front:
Ahmed, Beatrice and Carlos are partners in a firm of architects. Without the others' knowledge, Ahmed agrees to buy a fleet of cars in the firm's name, saying it is for client site visits. The seller is unaware of any restriction on Ahmed's authority. Are the other partners bound?

Identify the relevant rule and apply it to these facts.
Partnership.answer: Under PA 1890 s.8, every partner is an agent of the firm for acts done in the ordinary course of the kind of business carried on by the firm. The question is whether buying a fleet of cars falls within the ordinary course of an architect's business. It almost certainly does not — this is not a transaction of a type that an architect's firm would ordinarily enter into. The seller cannot rely on s.8 because the act is outside the ordinary course of business. Ahmed would need specific authority from all partners to bind the firm. The firm is therefore not bound, and Ahmed may be personally liable for breach of warranty of authority to the seller. If the seller had known of a restriction on Ahmed's authority, the firm would equally not be bound even for acts within the ordinary course.
Partnership.summary_line: PA 1890 S.8 · ORDINARY COURSE OF BUSINESS · SPECIFIC AUTHORITY NEEDED · SELLER NOT PROTECTED · BREACH OF WARRANTY OF AUTHORITY

Partnership.front:
Tom, Uma and Victor are partners in an accountancy firm. Without the others' knowledge or consent, Uma takes out a £50,000 loan in the firm's name from a bank, representing that it is for new office equipment. Uma spends the money on a personal holiday. The bank now seeks repayment from Tom and Victor.

Advise Tom and Victor on their liability.
Partnership.answer:
The bank will argue that Uma had authority as a partner to bind the firm, and Tom and Victor are jointly and severally liable for the debt.

Under PA 1890 s.8, every partner is an agent of the firm for acts carried out in the ordinary course of the kind of business the firm carries on. The key question is whether taking out a loan for office equipment falls within the ordinary course of an accountancy firm's business. It almost certainly does — borrowing for business equipment is a normal commercial act for a professional firm. The bank had no knowledge of any restriction on Uma's authority, and had no reason to suspect one.

Under PA 1890 s.9, partners are jointly liable for debts incurred in the ordinary course of business. The fact that Uma misappropriated the funds does not affect the bank's position — the bank dealt with Uma in good faith. Tom and Victor are therefore liable for the £50,000 debt in full (jointly and severally — the bank can sue either or both for the full amount).

Tom and Victor's remedy is against Uma personally for breach of her duty of good faith and her misappropriation. A partnership agreement may also provide for indemnification between partners.
Partnership.summary_line: PA 1890 SS.8–9 · ORDINARY COURSE · JOINT AND SEVERAL LIABILITY · GOOD FAITH THIRD PARTY PROTECTED · REMEDY AGAINST UMA

Partnership.front:
Wren and Xena set up a partnership to run a restaurant. Wren contributes £80,000 capital and Xena contributes £20,000. They have no written partnership agreement. At the end of the first year the partnership makes a profit of £60,000. Wren argues she should receive £48,000 (80%) reflecting her capital contribution. Xena argues they should split equally.

Who is correct?
Partnership.answer:
Xena is correct. Under PA 1890 s.24(1), in the absence of any agreement to the contrary, partners share profits and losses equally regardless of their capital contributions. Since Wren and Xena have no written partnership agreement, the default rule applies. Each is entitled to £30,000.

The fact that Wren contributed four times as much capital as Xena is irrelevant to profit sharing in the absence of an agreement — the Act does not make profit sharing proportional to capital. (Under s.24(4), partners are also entitled to 5% interest on any capital contributed beyond what was agreed — but this does not affect the profit split.)

This outcome highlights the importance of a written partnership agreement. Wren should have negotiated a profit-sharing ratio reflecting capital contributions before the partnership commenced trading.
Partnership.summary_line: PA 1890 S.24(1) · EQUAL PROFIT SHARING BY DEFAULT · CAPITAL CONTRIBUTION IRRELEVANT · WRITTEN AGREEMENT ESSENTIAL

Private and Public Companies.front: What is the key distinction between a private company limited by shares and a public company limited by shares?
Private and Public Companies.answer: A private company (Ltd) cannot offer its shares or debentures to the public and is prohibited from doing so (CA 2006 s.755). A public company (PLC) can offer securities to the public. A PLC must have a minimum allotted share capital of £50,000 (s.763), at least 25% of which must be paid up on allotment. A PLC must also have at least two directors and a qualified company secretary, and must hold an AGM.
Private and Public Companies.summary_line: PRIVATE = NO PUBLIC OFFER · PUBLIC = MIN £50K SHARE CAPITAL · AGM · TWO DIRECTORS

Private and Public Companies.front: What is the effect of incorporation — what does it mean for a company to have separate legal personality?
Private and Public Companies.answer: Upon incorporation, a company becomes a legal person distinct from its shareholders and directors (Salomon v Salomon & Co [1897]). This means: (1) the company can own property, enter contracts, and sue/be sued in its own name; (2) shareholders' liability is limited to the amount unpaid on their shares; (3) the company continues to exist regardless of changes in membership; (4) the company has perpetual succession. The 'veil of incorporation' separates the company from its members.
Private and Public Companies.summary_line: SEPARATE LEGAL PERSONALITY · SALOMON v SALOMON · LIMITED LIABILITY · PERPETUAL SUCCESSION

Sole Trader.front: What are the key legal characteristics of a sole trader?
Sole Trader.answer: A sole trader is an individual carrying on business alone. There is no legal separation between the individual and the business: the sole trader has unlimited personal liability for all business debts. No formal registration is required (other than HMRC notification and VAT if applicable). The sole trader owns all assets and is entitled to all profits. The business ceases on death or retirement.
Sole Trader.summary_line: SOLE TRADER · UNLIMITED LIABILITY · NO SEPARATE LEGAL PERSONALITY · NO FORMAL REGISTRATION

Sole Trader.front: How is a sole trader taxed on business profits?
Sole Trader.answer: A sole trader pays income tax on business profits (not corporation tax). Profits are assessed under the self-assessment system. The sole trader also pays Class 2 NIC (flat weekly rate, if profits exceed the small profits threshold) and Class 4 NIC (a percentage of profits between the lower and upper profits limits). The business itself is not a separate taxpayer.
Sole Trader.summary_line: INCOME TAX · SELF-ASSESSMENT · CLASS 2 NIC · CLASS 4 NIC · NOT CORPORATION TAX

Alteration of Articles.front:
Harvest Ltd has five shareholders. The company's articles currently state that no shareholder may transfer their shares to a non-member without first offering them to existing members at a price set by the auditors. Three shareholders (holding 80% of shares) want to remove this restriction so they can sell to a trade buyer. The other two object.

Can the majority validly remove the restriction?
Alteration of Articles.answer:
Yes, in principle — but the alteration must satisfy the bona fide test.

Procedure: Articles may be altered by special resolution (75% majority — CA 2006 s.21). The three shareholders hold 80%, which is sufficient to pass a special resolution. The formality requirements (notice of meeting, special notice etc.) must be complied with.

Validity: The alteration must be bona fide in the interests of the company as a whole (Allen v Gold Reefs [1900]). Removing a pre-emption restriction to facilitate a trade sale could well be in the company's interests — it allows the company to attract new investment and achieve a better commercial outcome. The fact that the majority shareholders happen to benefit is not itself objectionable.

Challenge: The minority (holding 20%) may challenge the alteration if they can show it was not genuinely for the company's benefit but was simply designed to allow the majority to exit at the minority's expense. They might also argue unfair prejudice under s.994 if the removal of pre-emption rights is part of a pattern of conduct prejudicial to their interests as members.

Conclusion: The alteration is procedurally valid. Whether it is substantively challengeable depends on the full facts.
Alteration of Articles.summary_line: SPECIAL RESOLUTION · CA 2006 S.21 · BONA FIDE TEST · ALLEN v GOLD REEFS · MINORITY CHALLENGE · S.994 UNFAIR PREJUDICE

Articles of Association.front: How can a company alter its articles of association and what are the limits?
Articles of Association.answer: Articles may be altered by special resolution (75% majority of votes cast) under CA 2006 s.21. The alteration must be bona fide in the interests of the company as a whole (Allen v Gold Reefs of West Africa [1900]). Certain provisions can be entrenched under s.22, requiring a higher threshold or different procedure for amendment. Shareholders cannot agree by ordinary resolution to waive the right to alter articles — any restriction on future alteration is void except through proper entrenchment.
Articles of Association.summary_line: SPECIAL RESOLUTION · CA 2006 S.21 · BONA FIDE FOR COMPANY AS WHOLE · ENTRENCHMENT S.22

Articles of Association.front: What is the legal effect of a company's articles of association on the company and its members?
Articles of Association.answer: Under CA 2006 s.33, the articles bind the company and its members to the same extent as if they were covenants in a deed signed by the company and each member. The articles constitute a contract between: (1) the company and each member; and (2) each member and every other member — but only in their capacity as members, not in any other capacity (Hickman v Kent or Romney Marsh Sheepbreeders' Association [1915]).
Articles of Association.summary_line: CA 2006 S.33 · STATUTORY CONTRACT · COMPANY ↔ MEMBERS · IN MEMBER CAPACITY ONLY

Articles of Association.front: What is a shareholders' agreement and how does it differ from the articles of association?
Articles of Association.answer: A shareholders' agreement is a private contract between some or all shareholders (and often the company). Key differences from articles: (1) Confidential — not filed at Companies House; (2) Cannot be amended without consent of all parties (unlike articles, which can be altered by special resolution); (3) May contain provisions not permissible in articles (e.g. deadlock provisions, non-compete obligations); (4) Breach gives rise to damages (contractual remedy), whereas breach of articles may require company law remedies. Shareholders' agreements complement but do not replace the articles.
Articles of Association.summary_line: PRIVATE · NOT FILED · REQUIRES UNANIMOUS CONSENT · CONTRACTUAL REMEDIES · COMPLEMENTS ARTICLES

Articles of Association.front: Allen v Gold Reefs of West Africa [1900]
Articles of Association.issue: Whether a company could validly alter its articles to impose a lien on fully paid shares, retrospectively affecting an existing member.
Articles of Association.rule: The power to alter a company's articles by special resolution must be exercised bona fide in the interests of the company as a whole. The majority cannot use the power to benefit themselves at the minority's expense, or for an improper purpose. An alteration valid on its face will be upheld unless it is one that no reasonable body of shareholders could consider bona fide for the company's benefit.
Articles of Association.application: The company's articles gave it a lien over partly-paid shares for debts owed to the company. A member died having partly-paid shares and also fully-paid shares. The company altered its articles to extend the lien to fully-paid shares. The Court of Appeal upheld the alteration: it was passed in good faith and for the company's general benefit, even though it adversely affected the deceased member's estate.
Articles of Association.conclusion: Article alterations are valid if passed bona fide for the benefit of the company as a whole. Incidental prejudice to individual members does not invalidate an alteration made in good faith. This is the foundational test for challenging special resolution alterations to articles.
Articles of Association.summary_line: ARTICLE ALTERATION · BONA FIDE FOR COMPANY AS WHOLE · SPECIAL RESOLUTION · INCIDENTAL PREJUDICE PERMITTED · LINDLEY MR

Articles of Association.front: Hickman v Kent or Romney Marsh Sheepbreeders' Association [1915]
Articles of Association.issue: Whether articles of association constituted a binding contract enforceable by a member against the company, and whether that contract extended to rights exercised in a capacity other than as a member.
Articles of Association.rule: The articles constitute a contract between the company and each member, and between members inter se, but only in respect of rights and obligations in their capacity as members. Rights conferred by the articles in some other capacity (e.g. as a solicitor or director) are not enforceable under the statutory contract.
Articles of Association.application: Hickman, a member, sought to restrain the Association from referring a dispute to arbitration as the articles required, arguing a breach of a separate contractual right. The court held the articles were a contract between the company and members as members — but only as members. Rights operating in other capacities were outside the statutory contract.
Articles of Association.conclusion: The s.33 (formerly s.20) contract is limited to member rights. A member cannot use the articles to enforce rights held in another capacity. This remains good law under CA 2006 s.33.
Articles of Association.summary_line: ARTICLES AS CONTRACT · MEMBER CAPACITY ONLY · S.33 CA 2006 · OTHER CAPACITIES NOT ENFORCEABLE · ASTBURY J

Articles of Association.front:
Green Shoots Ltd was incorporated using the Model Articles without modification. At a board meeting, the two directors (who are also the only shareholders) disagree on whether to open a second office. One director refuses to attend further board meetings. The other director wants to know whether she can make decisions alone.

Advise on the position under the Model Articles.
Articles of Association.answer:
Under the Model Articles for private companies limited by shares (Art. 11), decisions at board meetings require a quorum. The quorum for board meetings is two directors unless otherwise fixed by the directors. If only one director attends, there is no quorum and no valid board decisions can be made.

The attending director cannot unilaterally make decisions on behalf of the company in these circumstances. She is not able to simply override the quorum requirement.

Options: (1) The shareholders could pass a resolution altering the articles to reduce the quorum to one (special resolution — 75% majority — required under CA 2006 s.21). With only two shareholders equally split, this may not be achievable without the non-attending director's agreement. (2) If both directors are also equal shareholders, the company may be deadlocked — a situation that could ultimately lead to a petition under CA 2006 s.994 (unfair prejudice) or IA 1986 s.122(1)(g) (just and equitable winding up) if it cannot be resolved. (3) The parties should consider a shareholders' agreement with deadlock provisions before the company was formed — at this stage retrospective insertion is much harder.
Articles of Association.summary_line: MODEL ARTICLES · QUORUM = TWO · NO UNILATERAL DECISION · DEADLOCK · S.994 / S.122(1)(G) · SHAREHOLDERS' AGREEMENT PREVENTION

Company Name.front: What restrictions apply to a company's choice of name and how may a company change its name?
Company Name.answer: A company name must not: be the same as an existing registered name (CA 2006 s.66); be a 'sensitive' name without approval (e.g. names implying government connection — s.55); be offensive (s.53); or constitute a criminal offence. The name of a private company must end in 'Limited' or 'Ltd'; a public company must end in 'Public Limited Company' or 'PLC' (s.58–59). A company may change its name by special resolution (75% majority) under s.77, or by any other means provided for in its articles. Notice of the change must be filed at Companies House (Form NM01). The change takes effect on registration. A third party who believes a registered name is too similar to their own may apply to the Company Names Tribunal under s.69.
Company Name.summary_line: CA 2006 SS.53,55,66,77 · SAME NAME PROHIBITED · SENSITIVE NAMES · SPECIAL RESOLUTION TO CHANGE · COMPANY NAMES TRIBUNAL

Incorporation.front: What documents must be submitted to Companies House to incorporate a company?
Incorporation.answer: To incorporate a company, the following must be delivered to Companies House under CA 2006 s.9: (1) a memorandum of association — signed by the subscribers, stating they agree to form the company and take at least one share; (2) an application for registration (Form IN01) — including company name, registered office address, details of directors, company secretary (if any), and statement of capital; (3) articles of association (unless Model Articles are adopted in full). Companies House then issues a certificate of incorporation.
Incorporation.summary_line: MEMORANDUM OF ASSOCIATION · FORM IN01 · ARTICLES · CERTIFICATE OF INCORPORATION · CA 2006 S.9

Incorporation.front: What is the legal effect of the certificate of incorporation?
Incorporation.answer: The certificate of incorporation is conclusive evidence that the company is incorporated and the requirements of the Companies Act have been complied with (CA 2006 s.15). The company comes into existence on the date stated in the certificate. From that date, the company is a body corporate and can exercise all the functions of an incorporated company. The certificate cannot be challenged even if there was a defect in the registration process.
Incorporation.summary_line: CA 2006 S.15 · CONCLUSIVE EVIDENCE · COMPANY EXISTS FROM DATE ON CERTIFICATE · UNCHALLENGEABLE

Incorporation.front: What are the Model Articles and when do they apply?
Incorporation.answer: The Model Articles are standard-form articles of association prescribed by the Secretary of State under CA 2006 s.19. There are separate Model Articles for private companies limited by shares, public companies, and companies limited by guarantee. If a company does not register its own articles, the Model Articles automatically apply in full. Companies may adopt the Model Articles with modifications or draft bespoke articles. The Model Articles for private companies limited by shares contain sensible defaults for small companies.
Incorporation.summary_line: MODEL ARTICLES · CA 2006 S.19 · APPLY BY DEFAULT · CAN BE MODIFIED · SEPARATE VERSIONS

Model Articles \u2014 Directors.front: Under the Model Articles for private companies limited by shares (SI 2008/3229), does the chairman of a board meeting have a casting vote in the event of a tied vote? How does this differ from the old Table A?
Model Articles \u2014 Directors.answer:
No. Under the current Model Articles (2008), the chairman does NOT have a casting vote at board meetings.

Article 13 of the Model Articles provides that if a proposed director's decision is put to a vote and there is a tied vote, the chairman does NOT have a second or casting vote. If votes are equal, the motion is lost.

This is a significant change from the old Table A (Companies Act 1985 era), which gave the chairman a casting vote at board meetings (Table A, Art. 50). The removal of the casting vote under the 2008 Model Articles was a deliberate policy choice: a tied board vote means the company cannot act, which is appropriate — the deadlock should be escalated to shareholders or resolved by agreement.

Practical consequence: If a private company incorporated under or adopting the 2008 Model Articles has an even number of directors and they are deadlocked, the resolution fails. The company cannot act. This could produce a governance crisis in a two-director/two-shareholder company.

SRA exam focus: Candidates often assume the chairman has a casting vote (from the old Table A rule). The correct position under the current Model Articles is the opposite — no casting vote; deadlock = resolution fails.
Model Articles \u2014 Directors.summary_line: MODEL ARTICLES 2008 · NO CHAIRMAN CASTING VOTE · ART.13 · TIE = MOTION FAILS · CONTRAST TABLE A ART.50 · DEADLOCK RISK IN EVEN-DIRECTOR BOARDS

Pre-Incorporation Contracts.front:
Before Kara Ltd was incorporated, Kara (acting as its promoter) signed a supply agreement with a stationery company on behalf of 'Kara Ltd (in formation)'. Kara Ltd was incorporated one month later but never ratified the agreement. The stationery company now seeks payment for goods supplied.

Who is liable?
Pre-Incorporation Contracts.answer:
Kara is personally liable. A company cannot be bound by contracts entered into before it was incorporated — it did not exist and therefore could not have been a contracting party (Kelner v Baxter (1866)).

Under CA 2006 s.51, where a contract purports to be made by or on behalf of a company before it is incorporated, the person who purported to act as agent for the company is personally liable on the contract (subject to any agreement to the contrary). This applies even where the company is subsequently incorporated.

The company's failure to ratify makes no difference — a company cannot ratify a pre-incorporation contract because ratification requires the principal to have existed at the time the contract was made. Kara Ltd therefore cannot be liable.

Kara is personally liable to the stationery company for the contract price. She should have ensured the company was incorporated before entering into the contract, or ensured the agreement was conditional on incorporation and subsequent adoption by the company.
Pre-Incorporation Contracts.summary_line: PRE-INCORPORATION CONTRACT · CA 2006 S.51 · PROMOTER PERSONALLY LIABLE · COMPANY CANNOT RATIFY · KELNER v BAXTER

Shareholders' Agreement.front:
Iris and James are equal shareholders in a tech startup. They entered into a shareholders' agreement providing that major decisions (including taking on investment) require unanimous consent. James now wants to accept a £500,000 investment from a VC fund that would dilute Iris's shareholding from 50% to 30%. Iris objects. The company's articles say nothing about this.

Advise James on whether he can proceed without Iris's consent.
Shareholders' Agreement.answer:
James cannot proceed without Iris's consent, on these facts.

The shareholders' agreement expressly requires unanimous consent for major decisions, and taking on external investment that dilutes existing shareholders falls squarely within that category. Unlike the articles (which can be altered by special resolution under s.21), a shareholders' agreement is a private contract that can only be amended with the consent of all parties to it. Iris's consent is therefore needed to vary or override the agreement.

Allotment of new shares: Even if James persuaded the board to proceed, the company would need authority to allot the new shares (CA 2006 s.551) and Iris's statutory pre-emption rights on a cash allotment (s.561) would need to be disapplied — requiring a special resolution (75%), which James as a 50% holder cannot pass alone.

If James proceeds in breach of the shareholders' agreement, Iris's remedy is a contractual claim for breach. She could seek an injunction to prevent the allotment going ahead, or damages. She might also petition under s.994 if the conduct constitutes unfair prejudice to her interests.

Advice: James must obtain Iris's agreement or renegotiate the shareholders' agreement with her consent.
Shareholders' Agreement.summary_line: SHAREHOLDERS' AGREEMENT · UNANIMOUS CONSENT · CANNOT UNILATERALLY AMEND · PRE-EMPTION S.561 · INJUNCTION · S.994

Shareholders' Agreement.front: Going deeper: Is the current legal treatment of shareholders' agreements — as purely private contracts, separate from and not overriding the articles — appropriate for modern closely-held companies, or does it create unnecessary complexity and risk?
Shareholders' Agreement.answer:
THE CURRENT POSITION
A shareholders' agreement is a private contract between shareholders (and often the company). It is not filed at Companies House and is not subject to the statutory majority-amendment rule that applies to articles (CA 2006 s.21 — special resolution). It can only be amended with the unanimous consent of all parties. Russell v Northern Bank Development Corporation [1992] (HL) confirmed that a shareholders' agreement can validly restrict the manner in which shareholders exercise their statutory rights (e.g. agreement not to vote for a capital increase) — but cannot restrict the company's own statutory powers (e.g. a term purporting to bind the company never to increase its share capital was void as a fetter on a statutory power).

THE COMPLEXITY PROBLEM
Having two constitutional documents (articles + shareholders' agreement) creates significant practical risks:
1. Inconsistency: The articles and the shareholders' agreement may conflict, particularly after amendment of one but not the other. Courts must determine which prevails — usually the shareholders' agreement if it is more specific, but the result is uncertain.
2. Third parties: Third parties (e.g. a purchaser of shares) are bound by the articles (public) but may be unaware of, and not bound by, the shareholders' agreement (private). This can cause the agreement's protections to evaporate on a share transfer.
3. Enforcement: Breach of the articles gives rise to a CA 2006 s.33 statutory contract claim. Breach of the shareholders' agreement is a private contractual claim for damages — but the remedy may be inadequate if specific performance is the real need.

THE CASE FOR THE CURRENT APPROACH
Privacy is a genuine commercial advantage: confidential terms (e.g. non-compete obligations, price protection mechanisms, exit provisions) cannot be disclosed to competitors. The unanimity requirement for amendment provides minority protection that the articles (amendable by 75%) cannot.

REFORM OPTIONS
The Law Commission's 1996 consultation on shareholder remedies raised the possibility of a single constitutional document incorporating both public and private terms (with different disclosure thresholds). Several jurisdictions (e.g. Delaware's LLC agreement framework) allow a single, highly flexible constitutional instrument. The UK has not pursued this. ⚠️ Verify whether any Companies House reform has changed disclosure requirements for shareholders' agreements.

OPEN QUESTION
Should England allow shareholders to designate certain shareholders' agreement provisions as 'constitutional' (binding on all future shareholders, like articles) while keeping other provisions private? This would combine the benefits of both instruments without the duplication risk.
Shareholders' Agreement.summary_line: SHAREHOLDERS' AGREEMENT · ARTICLES DISTINCTION · RUSSELL v NORTHERN BANK [1992] · PRIVACY · INCONSISTENCY RISK · THIRD PARTIES · LAW COMMISSION 1996 · DELAWARE LLC

Written Resolutions.front: What is the minimum notice period for a general meeting of a private company under CA 2006 s.307, and what information must the notice contain?
Written Resolutions.answer:
A general meeting of a private company requires at least 14 clear days' notice (CA 2006 s.307(1)). 'Clear days' means the day notice is given and the day of the meeting are both excluded from the count.

The notice must state (CA 2006 s.311): the time, date and place of the meeting; the general nature of the business to be transacted; and (for a special resolution) the text of the proposed resolution.

Notice must be given to every member entitled to attend and vote, every director, and the auditors (if any). Notice may be given in hard copy, electronic form, or (if the articles permit) by making it available on a website (s.309).

The 14-day minimum applies to all general meetings of private companies other than those requiring special notice (28 days — e.g. removing a director under s.168 or auditor under s.510). A company's articles may require a longer period but cannot reduce the statutory minimum.
Written Resolutions.summary_line: GM NOTICE = 14 CLEAR DAYS · S.307 · CONTENTS S.311 · SPECIAL NOTICE = 28 DAYS · ARTICLES CAN EXTEND NOT REDUCE

Written Resolutions.front: A private company wishes to hold a general meeting on shorter notice than the statutory 14-day minimum. In what circumstances is this permitted, and what consent is required? (CA 2006 s.307(4)–(6))
Written Resolutions.answer:
A general meeting of a private company may be held on shorter notice if the requisite majority of members with the right to attend and vote at the meeting consent to the shorter notice (CA 2006 s.307(4)).

The required consent threshold for a private company is a majority in number holding not less than 90% in nominal value of the shares giving a right to attend and vote — or such higher percentage (up to 95%) as the articles specify (s.307(6)). So, if the articles are silent, 90% is sufficient; the articles may increase this up to 95% but cannot reduce it below 90%.

Note: Short notice cannot be used for a meeting at which a special resolution is to be proposed unless the members holding the requisite percentage consent in writing to the shorter notice. Short notice cannot be used to circumvent the 28-day special notice requirement for removal of directors (s.168) or auditors.

Consent to short notice may be given before or at the meeting (in writing or by conduct), but must be clear and unambiguous.
Written Resolutions.summary_line: SHORT NOTICE · S.307(4)–(6) · 90% CONSENT (OR UP TO 95% IF ARTICLES) · CANNOT CIRCUMVENT SPECIAL NOTICE · SPECIAL RESOLUTIONS INCLUDED

Written Resolutions.front: Which resolutions passed by a company must be filed at Companies House, and within what time period? (CA 2006 ss.29–30)
Written Resolutions.answer:
Under CA 2006 s.30, a copy of every special resolution must be forwarded to Companies House within 15 days of it being passed. The same applies to certain ordinary resolutions specified in the Act — including:

• Ordinary resolutions granting directors authority to allot shares (s.551)
• Ordinary resolutions disapplying pre-emption rights under s.570 (if passed as an ordinary resolution under s.568)
• Resolutions agreeing to a company being wound up voluntarily
• Resolutions of a class of members varying their class rights

Under s.29, agreements affecting a company's constitution (e.g. shareholder agreements that are treated as equivalent to special resolutions) must also be forwarded within 15 days.

Failure to file within 15 days is a criminal offence by the company and every officer in default, but does not affect the validity of the resolution itself.

SRA exam focus: The 15-day filing deadline and the fact that ALL special resolutions must be filed are high-yield points. Ordinary resolutions need only be filed if the Act specifically requires it.
Written Resolutions.summary_line: CA 2006 S.30 · ALL SPECIAL RESOLUTIONS · WITHIN 15 DAYS · SPECIFIED ORDINARY RESOLUTIONS · OFFENCE NOT INVALIDITY · S.29 AGREEMENTS

Written Resolutions.front: What are the notice requirements for board meetings of a private company under the Model Articles, and how do they differ from general meeting requirements?
Written Resolutions.answer:
The Model Articles (for private companies limited by shares, SI 2008/3229) do not prescribe a specific minimum notice period for board meetings. Article 9 provides that notice of a directors' meeting must be given to each director but does not specify a minimum period — only that reasonable notice must be given.

What is 'reasonable' depends on the circumstances: the urgency of the matter, the directors' usual availability, and any practice established by the company. Courts have generally found that very short notice can be reasonable where the matter is urgent and directors are accessible.

Key contrasts with general meetings:
• GMs: 14 clear days statutory minimum (s.307 CA 2006) — hard floor
• Board meetings: no statutory minimum — 'reasonable notice' only
• GMs: contents of notice prescribed by statute (s.311)
• Board meetings: no statutory content requirements
• GMs: formal written notice required; board meetings can be called informally

Note: Art.13 of the Model Articles — a director may waive notice of a board meeting either prospectively or retrospectively. If all directors agree to meet informally without notice, any decisions taken are valid.
Written Resolutions.summary_line: BOARD MEETINGS · MODEL ARTICLES · REASONABLE NOTICE ONLY · NO STATUTORY MINIMUM · CONTRAST GM 14 CLEAR DAYS · ART.9 · WAIVER PERMITTED

Acting Within Powers \u2014 s.171.front:
The articles of Vortex Ltd restrict the directors to borrowing no more than £100,000 without shareholder approval. The directors enter into a £300,000 bank loan without obtaining any shareholder approval. The company later defaults.

Advise the bank on whether the loan is enforceable against the company.
Acting Within Powers \u2014 s.171.answer:
The loan is almost certainly enforceable against the company by the bank.

The directors breached CA 2006 s.171 (duty to act within powers) by entering into the loan without shareholder approval as required by the articles. However, this is an internal constitutional matter.

Under CA 2006 s.40, the power of the directors to bind the company, or authorise others to do so, is deemed free of any limitation under the company's constitution as against a person dealing with the company in good faith. A person dealing with a company is not bound to enquire whether any limitation on the directors' powers has been exceeded and is presumed to have acted in good faith unless the contrary is proved.

Unless the bank had actual knowledge of the limitation in the articles (which is unlikely — actual knowledge is needed, not constructive notice of publicly filed documents for this purpose), the bank is protected by s.40. The loan is therefore enforceable against Vortex Ltd.

The directors are, however, in breach of s.171 and are liable to the company for any loss suffered as a result of the unauthorised borrowing. The shareholders could ratify the transaction by ordinary resolution.
Acting Within Powers \u2014 s.171.summary_line: CA 2006 S.171 · ACT WITHIN POWERS · S.40 THIRD PARTY PROTECTION · GOOD FAITH · DIRECTORS LIABLE TO COMPANY · RATIFICATION

Conflicts of Interest and Corporate Oppo.subtopic: Conflicts of Interest and Corporate Opportunity
Conflicts of Interest and Corporate Oppo.front: Going deeper: Is the rule in Regal (Hastings) v Gulliver — that a director must account for any profit made from a corporate opportunity regardless of good faith — too strict? Should English law adopt a more flexible 'business judgment' approach?
Conflicts of Interest and Corporate Oppo.answer:
THE CURRENT RULE
Regal (Hastings) v Gulliver [1942] established that a director who profits from a corporate opportunity acquired in their capacity as a director must account for that profit — regardless of good faith, and regardless of whether the company could itself have exploited the opportunity. This strict prophylactic rule is now codified in CA 2006 s.175. IDC v Cooley [1972] extended it to cases where the director engineered their own release from office to take the opportunity personally.

THE CASE FOR STRICTNESS
Fiduciary law scholars (e.g. Conaglen, 'Fiduciary Loyalty' [2010]) argue the strict rule is justified precisely because it does not require the claimant to prove loss or fault. The rule deters self-dealing efficiently: if directors know they cannot profit from any conflict without authorisation, they will not try. The 'no profit' and 'no conflict' rules are complementary prophylactics. Any judicial discretion would invite satellite litigation about whether the company 'really' could have taken the opportunity.

THE CASE FOR FLEXIBILITY
Critics (e.g. Lowry & Edmunds) argue the rule overreaches: why should a director account for a profit on an opportunity the company could never have taken? The Canadian Supreme Court in Peso Silver Mines v Cropper [1966] allowed a 'rejection by the company' defence — if the board in good faith considered and declined the opportunity, the director could take it personally. Australian courts have adopted a similar approach. US corporate law applies a 'business judgment rule' deferring to board decisions made in good faith.

THE CA 2006 POSITION
Section 175(4)–(6) provides a partial answer: in private companies, conflicts can be authorised by independent directors without shareholder approval. This gives directors a legitimate route to exploit opportunities with board consent — the authorisation mechanism is the English law's concession to flexibility. But the underlying strict duty (absent authorisation) remains unchanged.

OPEN QUESTION
Should English law import a 'maturing business opportunity' test or a 'fairness' standard (as recommended by some Law Commission Working Papers in the 1990s)? The CA 2006 chose not to — but the debate continues, particularly for startup ecosystems where director-entrepreneurs frequently operate across multiple ventures.
Conflicts of Interest and Corporate Oppo.summary_line: REGAL (HASTINGS) · STRICT NO-PROFIT RULE · S.175 CA 2006 · IDC v COOLEY · CONAGLEN · PESO SILVER MINES · CANADIAN/AUSTRALIAN FLEXIBILITY · AUTHORISATION MECHANISM

Creditor Duty on Insolvency.front:
United Frames Ltd is struggling. Its directors know the company has liabilities exceeding its assets. Despite this, they authorise the payment of a £30,000 dividend to shareholders (who are also the directors) and continue trading for a further four months before the company goes into insolvent liquidation. The net deficiency increases by £80,000 during those four months.

Advise the liquidator on potential claims against the directors.
Creditor Duty on Insolvency.answer:
The liquidator has three potential claims:

1. Wrongful trading (IA 1986 s.214): The directors must account for the increase in the net deficiency caused by continued trading after the point when they knew or ought to have known there was no reasonable prospect of avoiding insolvent liquidation. On these facts, knowing the company was insolvent and continuing to trade for four months generates a strong claim. The £80,000 increase in the deficiency is the likely quantum of any contribution order.

2. Breach of duty — creditor duty (CA 2006 s.172(3)): On insolvency, the directors' duty shifts to require them to have regard to (and ultimately prioritise) the interests of creditors (West Mercia v Dodd [1988]; Sequana [2022]). Paying a £30,000 dividend to themselves when the company was already insolvent is a clear breach — it depletes assets available to creditors. The liquidator can pursue a misfeasance claim under IA 1986 s.212 for this breach.

3. Unlawful dividend (CA 2006 s.830): A dividend can only be paid from distributable profits. If the company was insolvent (liabilities exceeding assets), there were no distributable profits. The dividend was unlawful. As shareholders/directors who knew (or ought to have known) the company was insolvent, they must repay the £30,000 to the company.

The directors face both civil liability (contribution/repayment) and potential disqualification proceedings under the Company Directors Disqualification Act 1986.
Creditor Duty on Insolvency.summary_line: WRONGFUL TRADING S.214 · CREDITOR DUTY S.172(3) · UNLAWFUL DIVIDEND S.830 · MISFEASANCE S.212 · DISQUALIFICATION

Creditor Duty on Insolvency.front: Going deeper: Has the wrongful trading regime under IA 1986 s.214 successfully incentivised directors to act responsibly on the eve of insolvency, or does it create a 'too early to act, too late to stop' dilemma that deters legitimate rescue attempts?
Creditor Duty on Insolvency.answer:
THE POLICY RATIONALE
Section 214 IA 1986 was introduced following the Cork Report (1982), which found that directors of insolvent companies were too often allowed to trade at creditors' expense without personal consequence. The wrongful trading provision was designed to incentivise directors to monitor solvency, take professional advice early, and cease or restructure trading when rescue is impossible.

THE 'CHILLING EFFECT' CRITIQUE
The leading concern (raised by Finch, 'Company Insolvency Law' [2009] and others) is that s.214 creates a dilemma: if directors cease trading too early (at the first sign of difficulty), they may destroy a viable business and render legitimate rescue impossible. If they continue in the hope of rescue, they risk personal liability for the increase in the net deficiency. The result may be a 'defensive' insolvency culture — directors filing for administration or liquidation earlier than necessary to protect themselves, rather than pursuing a genuine turnaround.

SEQUANA AND THE SLIDING SCALE
BTI v Sequana [2022] (SC) refined the trigger for the creditor duty: it is not engaged by a mere risk of insolvency, but requires that insolvency or insolvent liquidation be probable. This gives directors more room to pursue rescue without triggering personal liability — a calibration that partially addresses the 'too early' problem. Lord Briggs described the duty as operating on a 'sliding scale', intensifying as insolvency approaches.

EMPIRICAL EVIDENCE
Research suggests s.214 is used infrequently in practice. Liquidators rarely bring wrongful trading claims because: (1) proving what directors 'ought to have known' is difficult and expensive; (2) insolvent directors often have no personal assets to satisfy a contribution order anyway; (3) misfeasance claims (s.212) are often more straightforward. The deterrent effect of s.214 may therefore be more theoretical than real.

REFORM PROPOSALS
The Insolvency Service has considered (and in some respects implemented) reforms to make wrongful trading easier to pursue. The Corporate Insolvency and Governance Act 2020 temporarily suspended wrongful trading liability during COVID-19 — acknowledging that during systemic crisis, personal liability rules may be counterproductive. ⚠️ The suspension has ended — verify current position.

OPEN QUESTION
Should s.214 be replaced with a regime that focuses on process obligations (e.g. requiring directors to seek insolvency advice once certain solvency metrics are breached) rather than outcome-based liability? Several European jurisdictions (e.g. Germany's Insolvenzantragspflicht — mandatory filing obligation) take this approach, which arguably provides clearer guidance and stronger incentives.
Creditor Duty on Insolvency.summary_line: S.214 WRONGFUL TRADING · CORK REPORT · CHILLING EFFECT · SEQUANA SLIDING SCALE · LOW USAGE IN PRACTICE · CIGA 2020 · GERMAN COMPARISON · FINCH

Director Appointment, Removal, and Relat.subtopic: Director Appointment, Removal, and Related Rules
Director Appointment, Removal, and Relat.front: How may a director be removed from office under the Companies Act 2006?
Director Appointment, Removal, and Relat.answer: A director may be removed by ordinary resolution (simple majority) of the shareholders at a general meeting, regardless of any contrary provision in the articles or any service contract (CA 2006 s.168). Special notice of 28 days must be given to the company before proposing the resolution. The director has a right to make written representations to be circulated to members, and to speak at the meeting. Removal under s.168 does not prevent the director from claiming wrongful dismissal or breach of service contract.
Director Appointment, Removal, and Relat.summary_line: CA 2006 S.168 · ORDINARY RESOLUTION · 28 DAYS SPECIAL NOTICE · DIRECTOR CAN SPEAK · SERVICE CONTRACT CLAIM SURVIVES

Director Appointment, Removal, and Relat.subtopic: Director Appointment, Removal, and Related Rules
Director Appointment, Removal, and Relat.front: What are the rules on loans to directors under CA 2006 and when is shareholder approval required?
Director Appointment, Removal, and Relat.answer: CA 2006 s.197 prohibits a company from making a loan to a director unless approved by an ordinary resolution of shareholders. Key exceptions under s.197: (1) loans up to £10,000 aggregate outstanding do not require approval; (2) loans by a money-lending company in the ordinary course of business on normal market terms. Separately, s.204 permits the company to fund a director's expenditure on company business (e.g. defending proceedings brought by the company) up to £50,000 without shareholder approval. Making an unlawful loan makes it voidable and renders the director liable to account for any gain and indemnify the company for any loss. ⚠️ Recommend verifying thresholds against current CA 2006 — amounts may be amended by statutory instrument.
Director Appointment, Removal, and Relat.summary_line: CA 2006 S.197 · LOAN TO DIRECTOR · £10K EXCEPTION · S.204 COMPANY EXPENDITURE £50K · VOIDABLE IF UNLAWFUL

Director Appointment, Removal, and Relat.subtopic: Director Appointment, Removal, and Related Rules
Director Appointment, Removal, and Relat.front: What is a shadow director and why does it matter?
Director Appointment, Removal, and Relat.answer: A shadow director is a person in accordance with whose directions or instructions the directors of a company are accustomed to act (CA 2006 s.251). A person who gives advice in a professional capacity (e.g. a solicitor or accountant) is not thereby a shadow director. Shadow directors owe certain statutory duties (including the general duties in ss.171–177 where applicable) and can be subject to disqualification orders. The concept is relevant to insolvency — a shadow director can be liable for wrongful trading under IA 1986 s.214.
Director Appointment, Removal, and Relat.summary_line: CA 2006 S.251 · DIRECTIONS FOLLOWED BY BOARD · PROFESSIONAL ADVICE EXCLUDED · DUTIES APPLY · WRONGFUL TRADING

Director Appointment, Removal, and Relat.subtopic: Director Appointment, Removal, and Related Rules
Director Appointment, Removal, and Relat.front: What approval is needed for a director's service contract with a term exceeding two years?
Director Appointment, Removal, and Relat.answer: Under CA 2006 s.188, a company cannot agree to a director's service contract with a guaranteed term of more than two years without prior approval by ordinary resolution of the shareholders. If entered into without approval, the offending term is void and the contract becomes terminable by the company on reasonable notice (s.189). This prevents directors from entrenching themselves with long service contracts that make removal financially prohibitive.
Director Appointment, Removal, and Relat.summary_line: CA 2006 S.188 · OVER 2 YEARS · ORDINARY RESOLUTION REQUIRED · VOID IF UNAPPROVED · TERMINABLE ON NOTICE

Directors Duties.front: List the seven statutory directors' duties under the Companies Act 2006 and the sections in which they appear.
Directors Duties.answer: The seven general duties owed by directors to the company (CA 2006 ss.171–177) are: s.171 — duty to act within powers; s.172 — duty to promote the success of the company; s.173 — duty to exercise independent judgment; s.174 — duty to exercise reasonable care, skill and diligence; s.175 — duty to avoid conflicts of interest; s.176 — duty not to accept benefits from third parties; s.177 — duty to declare interest in proposed transactions or arrangements.
Directors Duties.summary_line: CA 2006 SS.171–177 · SEVEN DUTIES · OWED TO COMPANY

Directors Duties.front: What is the content of the duty to promote the success of the company under CA 2006 s.172?
Directors Duties.answer: A director must act in the way they consider, in good faith, would be most likely to promote the success of the company for the benefit of its members as a whole. In doing so, the director must have regard (among other matters) to: long-term consequences; interests of employees; relationships with suppliers and customers; impact on the community and environment; maintaining a reputation for high standards of business conduct; and the need to act fairly between members. The duty is subjective in the sense that the director must act as they genuinely believe is in the company's interests, but the court will scrutinise whether that belief is reasonable.
Directors Duties.summary_line: CA 2006 S.172 · GOOD FAITH · SUCCESS FOR MEMBERS AS WHOLE · SIX CONSIDERATIONS · SUBJECTIVE

Directors Duties.front: What standard of care and skill must a director meet under CA 2006 s.174?
Directors Duties.answer: A director must exercise the care, skill and diligence that would be exercised by a reasonably diligent person with: (1) the general knowledge, skill and experience that may reasonably be expected of a person carrying out the functions of a director of that company (objective minimum floor); and (2) the actual knowledge, skill and experience that the particular director has (subjective — higher standard applies where the director has special expertise). The standard is therefore a 'dual test' and the higher of the two standards applies.
Directors Duties.summary_line: CA 2006 S.174 · DUAL TEST · OBJECTIVE FLOOR + SUBJECTIVE EXPERTISE · HIGHER STANDARD APPLIES

Directors Duties.front: How does the duty to avoid conflicts of interest (CA 2006 s.175) operate and how can it be authorised?
Directors Duties.answer: Under s.175, a director must avoid situations where they have (or could have) a direct or indirect interest that conflicts, or possibly may conflict, with the interests of the company — including exploiting company property, information or opportunities. The duty applies even if the company could not itself have taken advantage of the opportunity. Conflicts can be authorised: (1) for private companies — by independent directors (non-interested) under the Model Articles, without shareholder approval; (2) for public companies — by shareholders unless the articles otherwise permit board authorisation.
Directors Duties.summary_line: CA 2006 S.175 · NO CONFLICT · INCLUDES COMPANY OPPORTUNITIES · AUTHORISED BY INDEPENDENT DIRECTORS

Directors Duties.front: What must a director do when they have an interest in a proposed transaction or arrangement with the company (CA 2006 s.177)?
Directors Duties.answer: Under s.177, a director must declare the nature and extent of any interest in a proposed transaction or arrangement with the company to the other directors before the company enters into the transaction. The declaration may be made at a board meeting or by notice in writing. The director does not need to refrain from voting or be excluded from discussions unless the articles so require. The duty applies to direct and indirect interests, including interests of connected persons. Failure to declare is a breach but does not automatically make the transaction void.
Directors Duties.summary_line: CA 2006 S.177 · DECLARE TO BOARD · BEFORE TRANSACTION · NATURE AND EXTENT · DOES NOT VOID TRANSACTION

Directors Duties.front: To whom are the general duties in CA 2006 ss.171–177 owed?
Directors Duties.answer: The general duties are owed by directors to the company (CA 2006 s.170(1)) — not to individual shareholders, creditors, or third parties. This means only the company can enforce them (subject to derivative claim rules). Shareholders cannot individually sue a director for breach of duty. However, when a company is insolvent or approaching insolvency, s.172(3) requires directors to have regard to the interests of creditors, and the wrongful trading provisions may expose directors to personal liability.
Directors Duties.summary_line: OWED TO COMPANY · NOT SHAREHOLDERS OR CREDITORS · INSOLVENCY EXCEPTION S.172(3)

Directors Duties.front: At what point does the directors' duty under CA 2006 s.172 shift to require them to consider creditors' interests, and what is the practical effect of that shift?
Directors Duties.answer: Section 172(3) CA 2006 provides that the duty to promote the success of the company has effect subject to any rule of law requiring directors to consider, or act in the interests of, creditors. The courts have held that when a company is insolvent or bordering on insolvency, directors must have regard to the interests of creditors as a whole, not just shareholders (West Mercia Safetywear v Dodd [1988]; confirmed by the Supreme Court in BTI 2014 LLC v Sequana SA [2022]).
