In Sequana, the Supreme Court held the creditor duty is engaged when the directors know or ought to know the company is insolvent or bordering on insolvency, or that an insolvent liquidation is probable. The practical effect: paying dividends or preferring shareholders over creditors at this point may constitute a breach — and expose directors to personal liability. The wrongful trading regime under IA 1986 s.214 operates in parallel. ⚠️ Sequana [2022] is recent — recommend verifying against current commentary.
Directors Duties.summary_line: CA 2006 S.172(3) · CREDITOR DUTY · INSOLVENCY OR BORDERLINE · SEQUANA [2022] · WEST MERCIA · WRONGFUL TRADING IN PARALLEL

Directors Duties.issue: Whether directors exercising a discretion under the articles to refuse to register a share transfer were required to act in the company's interests, and on what basis a court could review that decision.
Directors Duties.rule: Directors must exercise their powers bona fide in what they consider (not what the court considers) to be in the interests of the company, and not for any collateral purpose. The test is subjective: the court will not substitute its own view for the directors' honest business judgment.
Directors Duties.application: The articles gave directors an absolute discretion to refuse to register a share transfer. A director died; his estate sought to transfer shares. The surviving director refused to register some of the shares. The Court of Appeal held the directors' discretion was genuine — if exercised bona fide for the company's benefit, the court would not interfere.
Directors Duties.conclusion: The foundational case on the director's subjective duty to act bona fide. Now codified (with modifications) in CA 2006 s.172 (duty to promote success) and s.171 (duty to act within powers). Although s.172 introduces an objective gloss, the bona fide core survives.
Directors Duties.summary_line: BONA FIDE IN INTERESTS OF COMPANY · SUBJECTIVE TEST · DISCRETION TO REFUSE TRANSFER · NOW S.172 CA 2006 · LORD GREENE MR

Directors Duties.issue: Whether directors who used knowledge acquired in their capacity as directors to make a personal profit from a corporate opportunity were liable to account to the company for that profit, even where the company could not itself have taken the opportunity.
Directors Duties.rule: A director who acquires a profit by reason of their fiduciary position and in the course of the execution of their office must account for that profit to the company. It is irrelevant that the company could not itself have exploited the opportunity, or that the company suffered no loss, or that the directors acted in good faith.
Directors Duties.application: Regal wanted to acquire cinemas by taking leases through a subsidiary. The subsidiary needed more capital than Regal could provide. The directors (and others) personally subscribed for shares in the subsidiary. When the group was sold, the directors made a substantial profit on their shares. The House of Lords held the directors must account to Regal for that profit — they had acquired it by reason of their fiduciary position.
Directors Duties.conclusion: Directors cannot profit from corporate opportunities that come to them in their capacity as directors, regardless of good faith. Now codified in CA 2006 s.175 (duty to avoid conflicts). The conflict can be authorised by independent directors (private companies) or shareholders.
Directors Duties.summary_line: ACCOUNT FOR PROFIT · FIDUCIARY POSITION · NO LOSS TO COMPANY IRRELEVANT · GOOD FAITH IRRELEVANT · NOW S.175 CA 2006

Directors Duties.front: Industrial Development Consultants Ltd v Cooley [1972]
Directors Duties.issue: Whether a managing director who diverted a business contract to himself personally — by obtaining his release from the company on false pretences — was liable to account to the company for the benefit received.
Directors Duties.rule: A director who acquires information or an opportunity in their capacity as a director and then diverts it for personal gain breaches their fiduciary duty. The fact that the company might not have obtained the opportunity is no defence. The director's personal position was obtained by exploiting the directorship.
Directors Duties.application: Cooley, managing director of IDC, learned that the Eastern Gas Board wanted to award a lucrative contract. The Board would not contract with IDC, but Cooley obtained his release from his directorship by falsely claiming personal ill health, then contracted with the Gas Board personally. Roskill J held Cooley must account for the benefit: the opportunity had come to him in his capacity as managing director.
Directors Duties.conclusion: A director cannot escape liability by arranging their own release from the company in order to exploit an opportunity. Extends Regal (Hastings) to cases where the company was effectively excluded by the director's own conduct. Now reflected in CA 2006 s.175.
Directors Duties.summary_line: DIVERSION OF CORPORATE OPPORTUNITY · CAPACITY AS DIRECTOR · ACCOUNT FOR BENEFIT · FALSE PRETENCE RELEASE · NOW S.175 CA 2006

Directors Duties.issue: Whether a director who signed an insurance proposal form without reading it, containing inaccurate information, had breached the duty of care owed to the company.
Directors Duties.rule: The standard of care and skill required of a director is that of a reasonably diligent person with: (1) the general knowledge, skill and experience reasonably expected of someone in that position (objective); and (2) the actual knowledge, skill and experience of the particular director (subjective — whichever is higher applies). This dual test (anticipating CA 2006 s.174) requires directors to apply proper care even to routine matters.
Directors Duties.application: D'Jan signed an insurance proposal form prepared by his broker without reading it. The form contained inaccuracies. When the company's premises were destroyed by fire, the insurer avoided the policy. The company went into liquidation. Hoffmann LJ held D'Jan had breached his duty of care — a reasonably diligent director would have read the form before signing it.
Directors Duties.conclusion: The objective-subjective dual standard of care was applied here before its statutory codification in s.174 CA 2006. Even routine administrative acts must be carried out with proper care. Liquidators may pursue directors for breach if the company suffers loss.
Directors Duties.summary_line: DUTY OF CARE · DUAL TEST · OBJECTIVE + SUBJECTIVE · SIGN WITHOUT READING = BREACH · NOW S.174 CA 2006 · HOFFMANN LJ

Directors Duties.front: West Mercia Safetywear Ltd v Dodd [1988]
Directors Duties.issue: Whether a director of an insolvent company who transferred money from the company to its parent company (to pay off a debt guaranteed by the director personally) had breached his duty to the company.
Directors Duties.rule: When a company is insolvent, directors owe a duty to take into account, and to act in the interests of, the company's creditors as a whole. The duty to consider creditors' interests overrides the duty to act in the interests of shareholders. A director who prefers their own interests (or those of shareholders) over creditors when the company is insolvent breaches this duty.
Directors Duties.application: Dodd was a director of West Mercia and its parent. West Mercia was insolvent. Dodd transferred £4,000 from West Mercia to the parent to reduce a debt guaranteed by him personally. This benefited him (by reducing his exposure) at the expense of West Mercia's unsecured creditors. The Court of Appeal held this was a breach of duty — the creditors' interests had to be paramount.
Directors Duties.conclusion: The creditor duty on insolvency is now reflected in CA 2006 s.172(3). The case is also the foundation of the misfeasance claim available to liquidators. The duty's precise trigger (insolvency vs. near-insolvency) was clarified by the Supreme Court in Sequana [2022].
Directors Duties.summary_line: CREDITOR DUTY ON INSOLVENCY · INTERESTS OF CREDITORS PARAMOUNT · DIRECTOR SELF-INTEREST BREACH · NOW S.172(3) CA 2006 · SEQUANA [2022]

Directors Duties.issue: When exactly does the directors' duty to have regard to creditors' interests under CA 2006 s.172(3) become engaged, and what is the content of that duty?
Directors Duties.rule: The creditor duty is engaged when the directors know or ought to know that the company is insolvent or bordering on insolvency (in the sense that insolvent liquidation is probable). The duty requires directors to give appropriate weight to creditors' interests — the weight increasing as the company's financial position deteriorates. When insolvency is certain and imminent, the creditors' interests become paramount and shareholders' interests are subordinated.
Directors Duties.application: AWA paid a large dividend to its parent (Sequana) when AWA had substantial contingent environmental liabilities. The question was whether, at the time of the dividend, the creditor duty was engaged. The Supreme Court held: a real risk of insolvency is insufficient to trigger the duty; there must be actual insolvency, or a probability of insolvent liquidation. On the facts, the duty had not been engaged at the relevant time.
Directors Duties.conclusion: The leading Supreme Court authority on the creditor duty. Confirms the duty exists, clarifies its trigger (insolvent or bordering on insolvent — not merely a risk), and explains that its content is a sliding scale. ⚠️ A very recent decision — recommend monitoring for further commentary.
Directors Duties.summary_line: CREDITOR DUTY TRIGGER · INSOLVENT OR BORDERLINE · NOT MERE RISK · SLIDING SCALE · SUPREME COURT [2022] · ⚠️ RECENT

Duty of Care and Skill \u2014 s.174.subtopic: Duty of Care and Skill — s.174
Duty of Care and Skill \u2014 s.174.front:
Petra is a non-executive director of Quinn Retail Ltd. She attends board meetings irregularly and does not review management accounts or financial reports sent to her. The company's finance director fraudulently inflates sales figures over 18 months. The fraud is eventually discovered; the company is insolvent. The liquidator seeks to hold Petra liable.

Advise on Petra's potential liability.
Duty of Care and Skill \u2014 s.174.answer:
Petra may be liable under CA 2006 s.174 for breach of her duty to exercise reasonable care, skill and diligence.

The dual test under s.174 requires the standard of a reasonably diligent person with: (1) the general knowledge, skill and experience expected of someone carrying out the functions of a director of that company; and (2) Petra's actual knowledge, skill and experience. Whichever is higher applies.

A non-executive director is not a passive figurehead. They are expected to participate in the governance of the company, review financial information provided to them, and apply an inquiring mind. Regular non-attendance and failure to review management accounts falls below the objective standard expected of a director in that role.

The question is whether a reasonably diligent non-executive director reviewing the accounts would have detected or questioned the inflated figures earlier. If regular review of the management accounts would have revealed red flags, Petra's failure to read them is a breach.

Petra may also face liability under IA 1986 s.214 (wrongful trading) if she ought to have concluded at some point that insolvent liquidation was probable and failed to take steps to minimise loss to creditors.

The liquidator can pursue a misfeasance claim under IA 1986 s.212.
Duty of Care and Skill \u2014 s.174.summary_line: CA 2006 S.174 · DUAL TEST · NON-EXEC NOT PASSIVE · FAILURE TO REVIEW ACCOUNTS · WRONGFUL TRADING S.214 · MISFEASANCE S.212

Duty to Avoid Conflicts \u2014 s.175.front:
Nadia is a director of Optic Solutions Ltd, a tech company. She learns through her role that Optic is looking to lease commercial premises in Bristol but cannot afford the asking price. Nadia personally acquires the Bristol property through her own company and then leases it to Optic at a profit. She did not disclose this to the board before acting.

Has Nadia breached her duties and what are the consequences?
Duty to Avoid Conflicts \u2014 s.175.answer:
Yes, Nadia has breached her duties under CA 2006 s.175 (duty to avoid conflicts) and potentially s.177 (duty to declare interest in proposed transactions).

Section 175: Nadia learned of the Bristol opportunity in her capacity as a director. She then exploited that information for personal gain — diverting what was, at least in commercial terms, a corporate opportunity. The fact that Optic 'could not afford' the property at the asking price does not matter: the duty applies even where the company could not itself have taken the opportunity (Regal (Hastings) v Gulliver [1942]). Her failure to disclose and seek authorisation from the independent directors means the conflict was not validly authorised.

Section 177: If Nadia arranged for her own company to lease the property to Optic, she had an interest in that transaction and was required to declare it to the board before the transaction was entered into.

Consequences: Nadia must account to Optic for any profit she has made. The lease may be voidable by the company. The other directors could authorise the arrangement retrospectively (under s.175(4)–(6)) but only if Nadia disclosed fully.

Nadia is also potentially liable for breach of her s.172 duty (failure to act in Optic's interests).
Duty to Avoid Conflicts \u2014 s.175.summary_line: CA 2006 S.175 · CONFLICT OF INTEREST · CORPORATE OPPORTUNITY · S.177 DECLARATION · ACCOUNT FOR PROFIT · VOIDABLE TRANSACTION

Duty to Promote Success \u2014 s.172.front:
Leo is a director of Meadow Foods Ltd, a food manufacturer. A major supermarket chain offers to acquire all of Meadow's product range for 3 years on a lucrative exclusive contract. However, the contract requires Meadow to make 40 factory workers redundant and to switch all sourcing to cheaper overseas suppliers, ending local supplier relationships built up over 20 years.

What must Leo consider in deciding whether to recommend this contract to the board?
Duty to Promote Success \u2014 s.172.answer:
Leo must comply with his duty to promote the success of the company under CA 2006 s.172. He must act in the way he considers, in good faith, would be most likely to promote the success of the company for the benefit of its members as a whole.

In doing so, s.172(1) requires him to have regard to (among other matters):

(a) The long-term consequences — is a 3-year exclusive contract strategically wise? Does it make Meadow too dependent on a single customer?

(b) The interests of employees — 40 redundancies are a significant consequence. Leo must genuinely consider the impact on the workforce, not just commercially but as a factor in the decision. This does not mean he must prevent redundancies if the contract is otherwise in the company's interests — but he must have regard to it.

(c) Relationships with suppliers — the end of 20-year local supplier relationships is a factor Leo must weigh. Reputational damage and loss of goodwill are real risks.

(d) Impact on the community and the company's reputation for high standards.

The duty is ultimately one of acting in the company's interests — Leo must weigh all these factors and reach a genuine business judgment. He does not need to maximise short-term profit at the expense of all other considerations. The board should minute that these matters were considered, as evidence of compliance with s.172.
Duty to Promote Success \u2014 s.172.summary_line: CA 2006 S.172 · PROMOTE SUCCESS · LONG-TERM CONSEQUENCES · EMPLOYEES · SUPPLIERS · COMMUNITY · GENUINE GOOD FAITH

Duty to Promote Success \u2014 s.172.front: Going deeper: Does the duty to promote the success of the company under CA 2006 s.172 genuinely embed stakeholder interests in English company law, or does shareholder primacy remain the dominant model?
Duty to Promote Success \u2014 s.172.answer:
THE STATUTORY FRAMEWORK
CA 2006 s.172 requires directors to promote the success of the company for the benefit of 'its members as a whole' — shareholders remain the primary beneficiaries. However, s.172(1)(a)–(f) lists six factors directors must 'have regard to', including: long-term consequences, employee interests, supplier and customer relationships, environmental and community impact, and high standards of business conduct. This is the 'enlightened shareholder value' (ESV) model adopted by the Company Law Review Steering Group.

THE DEBATE
Proponents of ESV (e.g. Keay, 'Enlightened Shareholder Value' [2007]) argue s.172 achieves a pragmatic balance: it aligns stakeholder and shareholder interests by recognising that long-term corporate success depends on treating all stakeholders well. Directors are incentivised to consider employees and communities without sacrificing the commercial discipline that shareholder accountability provides.

Critics argue the ESV model is illusory. The six factors in s.172(1) are not independently enforceable — only the company (i.e. the shareholders) can enforce s.172. Employees, communities and suppliers have no standing to compel compliance with the requirement to 'have regard' to their interests. Dodd ('For Whom Are Corporate Managers Trustees?' [1932]) and more recently Millon argue that genuine stakeholder theory requires enforceable rights, not discretionary consideration.

POST-SEQUANA: A THIRD WAY?
BTI v Sequana [2022] confirmed that on insolvency (or approaching insolvency), creditors' interests become paramount — s.172(3) effectively overrides the shareholder-primacy default. This suggests s.172 is not purely shareholder-centric: it contains a dynamic, context-sensitive duty that shifts with the company's financial health. Some scholars (e.g. Loughrey) argue this creates a more genuinely pluralist duty than s.172's drafting suggests.

LAW REFORM CONTEXT
The BEIS 2021 Corporate Governance Reform consultation and the FRC's work on the UK Corporate Governance Code have pushed ESG (Environmental, Social, Governance) disclosure obligations further into listed company governance. The question is whether voluntary ESG compliance and 'stakeholder capitalism' rhetoric has changed director behaviour in practice, or whether shareholder returns continue to dominate. ⚠️ This area is rapidly evolving — verify current government and FRC positions.

OPEN QUESTION
Should s.172 be amended to replace 'members as a whole' with 'the company' as a broader concept encompassing all stakeholders? Or would this create unenforceable uncertainty? The Companies Act 2006 Review and ongoing academic debate have not produced consensus.
Duty to Promote Success \u2014 s.172.summary_line: S.172 · ENLIGHTENED SHAREHOLDER VALUE · STAKEHOLDER INTERESTS · ENFORCEMENT GAP · SEQUANA [2022] · ESG · REFORM DEBATE · KEAY · MILLON

Loans to Directors.front:
Rex is the sole director and 60% shareholder of Stride Ltd. Without convening a shareholder meeting or passing any resolution, Rex arranges for Stride to lend him £25,000 to pay for a home extension. He repays £5,000 within a month, so the outstanding loan is now £20,000. Stride has two other shareholders.

Advise on the legal position.
Loans to Directors.answer:
The loan is unlawful and Rex is in breach of CA 2006 s.197.

Section 197 prohibits a company from making a loan to a director unless approved in advance by an ordinary resolution of the shareholders. The £10,000 small loan exception under s.207 means loans up to £10,000 aggregate outstanding do not require approval — but the outstanding balance here is £20,000 (even after partial repayment), which exceeds the threshold. The full original loan of £25,000 also exceeded it.

The loan was made without shareholder approval and without any applicable exception. It is therefore unlawful.

Consequences: The loan is voidable at the company's option (s.213 CA 2006 — the company can rescind it). Rex is liable to: account to the company for any gain made from the loan; and indemnify the company for any loss or damage suffered. The other two shareholders (as minority) could pursue this through the company or, if Rex refuses to remedy the breach, could petition under s.994 for unfair prejudice — self-dealing by a director/majority shareholder is a classic s.994 situation.

Rex should immediately seek shareholder ratification by ordinary resolution and arrange full repayment.
Loans to Directors.summary_line: CA 2006 S.197 · LOAN TO DIRECTOR · £10K EXCEPTION · VOIDABLE · ACCOUNT FOR GAIN · S.994 UNFAIR PREJUDICE

Removal of Directors.front:
Sara is a director of Turnstone Ltd under a service contract with 3 years remaining. The majority shareholders (holding 75% of shares) are unhappy with her performance and want to remove her immediately. They propose to pass a written resolution.

Advise on the procedure for removal and the consequences.
Removal of Directors.answer:
Procedure: A director can be removed by ordinary resolution (simple majority) under CA 2006 s.168 — but this cannot be done by written resolution. Section 168 requires the resolution to be passed at a general meeting, because Sara has the right under s.169 to receive special notice (28 days) of the intended resolution, make written representations to be circulated to members, and speak at the meeting. A written resolution procedure would deprive her of the right to address the members.

The shareholders hold 75% — more than sufficient for an ordinary resolution (>50%). They must give Sara 28 days' special notice before the meeting.

Consequences for the service contract: Removal under s.168 does not terminate Sara's contractual rights. Her 3-year service contract remains enforceable. If the company ends her contract early, Sara is entitled to damages for wrongful termination of the contract (the unexpired 3-year term, subject to her duty to mitigate). However, note that a service contract of more than 2 years requires prior shareholder approval under s.188 — if that approval was not obtained when Sara was appointed, the contract is terminable on reasonable notice only (s.189), which significantly limits the company's exposure.

Sara may also bring an unfair dismissal claim if she qualifies (2 years' employment).
Removal of Directors.summary_line: CA 2006 S.168 · ORDINARY RESOLUTION · NOT WRITTEN RESOLUTION · 28 DAYS NOTICE · SERVICE CONTRACT SURVIVES · S.188 OVER 2 YEARS

Shadow Director.front:
Whitfield plc is the parent company of Sunray Ltd. The board of Sunray always follows instructions given by Whitfield's Group Finance Director, Marcus, without question. Marcus has never been formally appointed as a director of Sunray. When Sunray becomes insolvent, the liquidator seeks to make Marcus personally liable for wrongful trading.

Advise on whether Marcus can be caught by the wrongful trading provisions.
Shadow Director.answer:
Marcus may be liable as a shadow director.

Under CA 2006 s.251, a shadow director is a person in accordance with whose directions or instructions the directors of a company are accustomed to act. The key question is whether the Sunray board consistently followed Marcus's instructions without independent consideration. On the facts, they acted 'without question' — this strongly suggests the shadow director threshold is met.

The exception for professional advice does not apply here: Marcus was giving operational instructions, not professional advice in an independent capacity.

Wrongful trading under IA 1986 s.214 applies to 'a person who is or has been a director of the company', and s.214(7) expressly extends this to shadow directors. If Marcus knew or ought to have concluded that there was no reasonable prospect of avoiding insolvent liquidation, and failed to take every step to minimise potential loss to creditors, he is exposed to a contribution order.

The parent company Whitfield may also face scrutiny — if Whitfield itself gave the instructions, it could be a shadow director and face similar exposure. ⚠️ Corporate shadow directors is a developing area — recommend verifying current case law.
Shadow Director.summary_line: SHADOW DIRECTOR S.251 · ACCUSTOMED TO ACT · PROFESSIONAL ADVICE EXCLUDED · WRONGFUL TRADING S.214 APPLIES · CONTRIBUTION ORDER

Derivative Claims.front:
Kai is a minority shareholder in Lunar Ltd. The majority shareholders (who control the board) have caused the company to sell its most valuable asset — a portfolio of patents — to a company owned by one of the majority shareholders at a gross undervalue. Kai wants to challenge this.

What claims are available to Kai and through what procedure?
Derivative Claims.answer:
Kai has two main routes:

1. Derivative claim (CA 2006 s.260): The sale of patents at an undervalue to a connected party involves a breach of the majority director's duties (s.172 — failure to promote the company's success; s.175 — conflict of interest; potentially s.177 — failure to declare interest). A derivative claim allows Kai to sue on behalf of the company with court permission. The court will consider whether a hypothetical independent director would continue the litigation. On these facts — an apparent self-dealing transaction at an undervalue — permission is likely to be granted. Any remedy (account of profits, compensation) runs to the company, not Kai.

2. Unfair prejudice petition (s.994): The conduct may also be unfairly prejudicial to Kai as a minority shareholder. A transaction at undervalue that enriches the majority at the company's expense is a well-established basis for a s.994 petition. The court has wider remedial discretion under s.996 — it could order the majority to buy out Kai's shares or regulate the company's affairs.

The liquidator (if the company enters insolvency) could also challenge the transaction as a transaction at an undervalue under IA 1986 s.238.

Kai should bring both claims in the alternative. S.994 is typically the more practical route if Kai wants a commercial exit.
Derivative Claims.summary_line: DERIVATIVE CLAIM S.260 · COURT PERMISSION · REMEDY TO COMPANY · S.994 UNFAIR PREJUDICE · TRANSACTION AT UNDERVALUE S.238

Just and Equitable Winding Up.front: What did Ebrahimi v Westbourne Galleries [1973] decide, and what are the conditions for a just and equitable winding-up order under IA 1986 s.122(1)(g)?
Just and Equitable Winding Up.answer:
In Ebrahimi v Westbourne Galleries [1973] (HL), Ebrahimi and Nazar founded a carpet business as equal partners, later incorporating it. Nazar's son joined, giving the Nazars a majority. They used that majority to remove Ebrahimi as director (under what is now s.168), cutting off his only income from the company. Ebrahimi petitioned for winding up on just and equitable grounds.

The House of Lords (Lord Wilberforce) held the petition should succeed. Even where the majority act within their strict legal rights, equitable principles may superimpose obligations on the exercise of those rights in companies formed on the basis of personal relationships and mutual confidence — 'quasi-partnerships'.

Indicators of a quasi-partnership (not exhaustive):
• Association formed on the basis of a personal relationship involving mutual confidence
• Agreement or understanding that some or all members will participate in conducting the business
• Restriction on share transfers, so members cannot simply exit

Just and equitable winding up (IA 1986 s.122(1)(g)) is available where:
• The company's substratum (purpose) has disappeared
• The quasi-partnership basis has broken down (as in Ebrahimi)
• There is deadlock in management
• The company is a 'bubble' (never had a genuine purpose)

The court will refuse to wind up if an alternative remedy (e.g. s.994 buy-out) is available and reasonable.
Just and Equitable Winding Up.summary_line: EBRAHIMI v WESTBOURNE GALLERIES [1973] · QUASI-PARTNERSHIP · EQUITABLE OBLIGATIONS · S.122(1)(G) · JUST AND EQUITABLE · ALTERNATIVE REMEDY BARS WINDING UP

Just and Equitable Winding Up.front:
Rosa and Sam founded Tandem Ltd 10 years ago as equal shareholders and co-directors, intending to run the business together indefinitely. The company's articles are silent on management rights. Relations have broken down entirely. Sam has used his majority at board meetings (the articles give the chairman — Sam — a casting vote) to exclude Rosa from all meaningful decisions. Sam has offered to buy Rosa's shares but only at a 40% discount to their fair value.

Advise Rosa on her options.
Just and Equitable Winding Up.answer:
Rosa has two main options: an unfair prejudice petition (s.994) or a just and equitable winding-up petition (IA 1986 s.122(1)(g)). In practice, both are often pleaded in the alternative.

Unfair prejudice (s.994): The facts strongly support a petition. Tandem is a quasi-partnership — founded on mutual trust between two individuals with equal management expectations. Sam's use of a casting vote to exclude Rosa from meaningful management is the paradigm case of unfair prejudice (Ebrahimi; O'Neill v Phillips). Sam's offer at a 40% discount makes an agreed exit impossible.

The most likely remedy under s.996 is a buy-out order at fair value — courts routinely decline to apply a minority discount in quasi-partnership companies where the petitioner has been wrongfully excluded. Rosa should get full pro-rata value, not a discounted figure.

Just and equitable winding up (s.122(1)(g)): Rosa could also petition on the ground that the company's management is deadlocked and the quasi-partnership basis has broken down (Ebrahimi v Westbourne Galleries). However, the court will refuse to wind up if an alternative remedy (the s.994 buy-out) is available and reasonable — which it is here.

Advice: Pursue s.994 as the primary claim. Wind-up under s.122(1)(g) as a fallback. Rosa is likely to obtain a fair-value buy-out.
Just and Equitable Winding Up.summary_line: S.994 UNFAIR PREJUDICE · S.122(1)(G) WINDING UP · QUASI-PARTNERSHIP · BUY-OUT AT FAIR VALUE · NO MINORITY DISCOUNT · EBRAHIMI

Minority Protection.front: What is an unfair prejudice petition under CA 2006 s.994 and what remedies are available?
Minority Protection.answer: A member may petition the court under s.994 on the ground that the company's affairs are being or have been conducted in a manner unfairly prejudicial to the interests of members generally or some members (including the petitioner). Classic examples include: exclusion from management in a quasi-partnership, diversion of business opportunity, failure to pay dividends, excessive remuneration. The most common remedy is an order that the majority buy the petitioner's shares at a fair price (s.996). The court can also regulate future conduct or wind up the company.
Minority Protection.summary_line: CA 2006 S.994 · UNFAIRLY PREJUDICIAL · EXCLUSION FROM MANAGEMENT · BUY-OUT ORDER · S.996 REMEDIES

Minority Protection.front: What is a derivative claim and when may a member bring one?
Minority Protection.answer: A derivative claim is a claim brought by a member on behalf of the company to enforce a cause of action vested in the company (CA 2006 s.260). It arises from an actual or proposed act or omission involving negligence, default, breach of duty or breach of trust by a director. The member must first obtain permission from the court. The court will consider whether a hypothetical independent director would seek to continue the claim. The remedy runs to the company, not to the member personally. This is an exception to the rule in Foss v Harbottle.
Minority Protection.summary_line: CA 2006 S.260 · MEMBER SUES ON BEHALF OF COMPANY · COURT PERMISSION REQUIRED · REMEDY TO COMPANY · FOSS v HARBOTTLE

Minority Protection.front: What is the rule in Foss v Harbottle and what are its main exceptions?
Minority Protection.answer: The rule in Foss v Harbottle (1843) provides that: (1) the proper claimant in respect of a wrong done to a company is the company itself (not individual shareholders); and (2) if the act complained of is capable of ratification by a simple majority, the court will not interfere at the suit of a minority. The main exceptions allowing minority shareholders to bring an action are: (1) the act is illegal or ultra vires; (2) the act requires a special majority that has not been obtained; (3) the personal rights of the shareholder are infringed; (4) fraud on the minority where wrongdoers control the company (now addressed by the statutory derivative claim under s.260).
Minority Protection.summary_line: FOSS v HARBOTTLE · COMPANY IS PROPER CLAIMANT · MAJORITY RULE · EXCEPTIONS · DERIVATIVE CLAIM

Minority Protection.front: When may a court order a just and equitable winding up under IA 1986 s.122(1)(g) at the petition of a minority shareholder?
Minority Protection.answer: The court has a discretion to wind up a company on the just and equitable ground under IA 1986 s.122(1)(g). In the context of quasi-partnerships (small companies built on mutual trust and confidence), the court may order winding up where: (1) there has been a breakdown of mutual trust between the participants (Ebrahimi v Westbourne Galleries [1973]); (2) the petitioner has been excluded from management in circumstances where they had a legitimate expectation of participation; (3) the substratum of the company has failed (the main business purpose can no longer be carried out). The court will not wind up if a less drastic remedy (such as a buy-out under s.994) is available and reasonable. A petitioner who has acted inequitably may be denied relief.
Minority Protection.summary_line: IA 1986 S.122(1)(G) · JUST AND EQUITABLE · QUASI-PARTNERSHIP · EBRAHIMI v WESTBOURNE GALLERIES · EXCLUSION FROM MANAGEMENT

Minority Protection.front: Compare and contrast the unfair prejudice petition (CA 2006 s.994) and the just and equitable winding-up petition (IA 1986 s.122(1)(g)) as remedies for a minority shareholder. When would each be more appropriate?
Minority Protection.answer:
Both remedies protect minority shareholders in small quasi-partnership companies, but they differ significantly:

Unfair prejudice (s.994): Does not require insolvency or company failure. The petitioner must show conduct that is both unfair and prejudicial to their interests as a member. The court has wide remedial discretion under s.996 — most commonly ordering a buy-out of the petitioner's shares at a fair (often non-discounted) price. This preserves the company as a going concern. It is generally the preferred remedy where the company is viable and the petitioner simply wants out at a fair price.

Just and equitable winding up (s.122(1)(g)): Results in the company being dissolved — a drastic, irreversible remedy. The court will refuse the order if a less extreme remedy is available (s.125(2) IA 1986 — the court may make an alternative order). More appropriate where: the company is deadlocked and cannot function; the substratum has failed; or the petitioner genuinely wants the company wound up rather than a buy-out. The petitioner must come with clean hands.

In practice, a petitioner often pleads both in the alternative, with s.994 as the primary claim and s.122(1)(g) as a fallback.
Minority Protection.summary_line: S.994 UNFAIR PREJUDICE · BUY-OUT ORDER · GOING CONCERN · S.122(1)(G) WINDING UP · LAST RESORT · PLEAD IN ALTERNATIVE

Minority Protection.front: What did Bushell v Faith [1970] decide, and why is it significant for the protection of director-shareholders against removal under CA 2006 s.168?
Minority Protection.answer:
In Bushell v Faith [1970] (HL), three equal shareholders in a private company each held 100 shares. The articles provided that on any resolution to remove a director, that director's shares would carry three votes per share. When the other two shareholders tried to remove Faith as director by ordinary resolution under what is now s.168, Faith's weighted vote (300 votes) defeated the other 200 votes of the majority. The House of Lords upheld the clause.

Significance: CA 2006 s.168 provides that a director may be removed by ordinary resolution (simple majority). However, s.168 cannot prevent the articles from conferring weighted voting rights on shares — a company has freedom under s.22 CA 2006 to create different classes of shares with different voting rights. A weighted voting clause in the articles does not exclude s.168 (which remains intact as a statutory mechanism) — it simply means the weighted votes may defeat the ordinary resolution in practice.

SQE exam focus: This is tested as a 'can the majority remove the director?' question where the answer is 'yes in principle under s.168, but weighted voting rights in the articles may defeat the resolution in practice'. Bushell v Faith is the mechanism by which director-shareholders in small private companies protect their position against removal.
Minority Protection.summary_line: BUSHELL v FAITH [1970] · WEIGHTED VOTING · 3 VOTES PER SHARE ON REMOVAL RESOLUTION · S.168 INTACT BUT DEFEATED IN PRACTICE · ARTICLES FREEDOM

Minority Protection.issue: Whether individual shareholders could bring a claim in respect of wrongs allegedly done to their company by its directors.
Minority Protection.rule: Two rules emerge: (1) the proper claimant for a wrong done to a company is the company itself, not individual shareholders (the 'proper plaintiff' rule); (2) if the alleged wrong is capable of ratification by a simple majority, the court will not interfere at the suit of a minority (the 'majority rule'). These rules uphold majority rule and avoid a multiplicity of actions.
Minority Protection.application: Shareholders alleged directors had improperly applied company property. The court dismissed the action: the company was the proper party to bring any claim, and the majority shareholders could ratify the acts complained of. The minority had no standing to sue.
Minority Protection.conclusion: This case established the foundational rule of majority control in company law. It is now substantially supplemented by the statutory derivative claim under CA 2006 s.260, which provides a structured route for members to litigate on the company's behalf with court permission.
Minority Protection.summary_line: PROPER PLAINTIFF RULE · MAJORITY RULE · COMPANY IS CLAIMANT · RATIFICATION · NOW SUPPLEMENTED BY S.260 CA 2006

Minority Protection.issue: Whether a minority shareholder who had been excluded from management could obtain a just and equitable winding-up order where the company was solvent and there was no fraud.
Minority Protection.rule: In quasi-partnership companies founded on mutual trust and personal relationships, equitable considerations can override strict legal rights. Where a member has a legitimate expectation of participation in management — arising from the personal relationship on which the company was founded — exclusion from management may justify a just and equitable winding-up order under what is now IA 1986 s.122(1)(g).
Minority Protection.application: Ebrahimi and Nazar had run a carpet business together as partners, then incorporated it. Both were directors and shareholders. Nazar's son was brought in. Nazar and his son then used their majority voting power to remove Ebrahimi as a director. The company was profitable, but Ebrahimi had no other way to participate in the business (no salary, dividends only declared intermittently). The House of Lords upheld a winding-up order.
Minority Protection.conclusion: The just and equitable ground looks beyond legal rights to the equitable basis on which the company was formed. Exclusion from a quasi-partnership company, without fair buy-out, can justify dissolution. Today a buy-out under s.994 is usually the preferred remedy and the court will not wind up if that remedy is available.
Minority Protection.summary_line: QUASI-PARTNERSHIP · LEGITIMATE EXPECTATION · EXCLUSION FROM MANAGEMENT · JUST AND EQUITABLE WINDING UP · LORD WILBERFORCE

Minority Protection.issue: What constitutes 'unfair prejudice' for the purposes of a petition under what is now CA 2006 s.994, and in particular whether the frustration of a member's expectation of increased profit share was unfairly prejudicial.
Minority Protection.rule: Unfair prejudice requires both unfairness and prejudice. Conduct is unfair if it involves either: (a) breach of the terms on which the petitioner agreed to participate in the company (including terms arising from equitable principles in quasi-partnerships); or (b) use of legal rights in a way that equity would regard as contrary to good faith. A member has no right to insist the company be run differently simply because they are disappointed.
Minority Protection.application: Phillips informally indicated O'Neill might eventually receive 50% of profits if the company did well. Negotiations for a formal 50% shareholding broke down. O'Neill petitioned on the grounds that his exclusion from management and the failure to give him 50% was unfairly prejudicial. The House of Lords dismissed the petition: there had been no breach of any legal or equitable obligation. Informal understandings, without more, did not create enforceable legitimate expectations.
Minority Protection.conclusion: The leading authority on the scope of unfair prejudice. Confirms the concept of 'legitimate expectation' in quasi-partnerships (derived from Ebrahimi) but limits it: informal statements do not automatically create binding obligations. The company must have conducted itself in breach of the terms on which the petitioner joined.
Minority Protection.summary_line: UNFAIR PREJUDICE S.994 · BREACH OF TERMS OR BAD FAITH · LEGITIMATE EXPECTATION · INFORMAL UNDERSTANDINGS INSUFFICIENT · LORD HOFFMANN

Minority Protection.front:
Holly and Ivan set up Jasper Interiors Ltd as equal shareholders and co-directors, expecting to share profits through salaries. Three years in, Ivan uses his casting vote as chairman to remove Holly as a director. Ivan then votes to award himself a large pay rise and stops paying dividends. The company is profitable. Holly has received no income from the company since her removal.

Advise Holly.
Minority Protection.answer:
Holly has a strong claim for unfair prejudice under CA 2006 s.994.

The classic unfair prejudice situation is exclusion from management in a quasi-partnership company — a small company formed on the basis of a personal relationship and mutual expectations of participation (O'Neill v Phillips [1999]). Jasper Interiors was set up as an equal partnership — Holly had a legitimate expectation of continuing to act as director and receive income from the company.

Ivan's conduct arguably constitutes unfair prejudice on multiple grounds: (1) using a casting vote to remove Holly from management contrary to their mutual expectations; (2) awarding himself an excessive salary (diverting company value to himself while excluding Holly from income); (3) withholding dividends, leaving Holly with no economic return from her 50% shareholding.

Remedy: The most likely remedy under s.996 is a buy-out order — Ivan (or the company) purchasing Holly's shares at a fair value, which is typically not discounted for minority status in a quasi-partnership. The court may also regulate future conduct.

Holly should also consider whether Ivan's excessive salary constitutes an unlawful payment requiring shareholder approval.
Minority Protection.summary_line: CA 2006 S.994 · UNFAIR PREJUDICE · QUASI-PARTNERSHIP · EXCLUSION FROM MANAGEMENT · BUY-OUT ORDER S.996 · O'NEILL v PHILLIPS

Minority Protection.front: Going deeper: Is the unfair prejudice remedy under CA 2006 s.994 now so broadly used that it has become an unprincipled 'catch-all', undermining the majority rule principle in Foss v Harbottle?
Minority Protection.answer:
THE EVOLUTION OF S.994
The unfair prejudice remedy (originally s.459 CA 1985; now s.994 CA 2006) was introduced to address the harshness of Foss v Harbottle (1843) in small quasi-partnership companies. O'Neill v Phillips [1999] (HL) attempted to rein in the remedy: Lord Hoffmann held that unfair prejudice requires breach of the legal or equitable terms on which the petitioner agreed to participate — mere disappointment or failed commercial expectations are insufficient.

THE 'CATCH-ALL' CRITIQUE
Despite O'Neill, empirical research (e.g. Reisberg, 'Derivative Claims Under the Companies Act 2006' [2007]; Hollington, 'Shareholders' Rights') shows s.994 petitions are used in an extraordinarily wide range of situations: exclusion from management, dividend policy, excessive remuneration, related-party transactions, failure to comply with articles, and even alleged mismanagement. Critics argue the courts have broadened the concept of 'legitimate expectation' beyond what Ebrahimi v Westbourne Galleries [1973] contemplated, effectively allowing minority shareholders to challenge any corporate decision they dislike if they can frame it as unfair.

THE COST AND SATELLITE LITIGATION PROBLEM
Section 994 petitions are notoriously expensive and lengthy. The Law Commission's 1997 report 'Shareholder Remedies' recommended procedural reforms to discourage frivolous petitions, including a threshold requirement. The Companies Act 2006 did not implement these recommendations. Some practitioners argue the remedy has become a 'nuclear option' in shareholder disputes, used as leverage rather than genuine legal redress.

THE DEFENCE OF BREADTH
Proponents argue the breadth of s.994 is its virtue: small company disputes are infinitely varied, and a flexible equitable remedy — rather than a rigid rule — is the appropriate response. The buy-out order (the most common remedy under s.996) preserves the company and provides a clean exit, which is almost always preferable to winding up. The remedy's flexibility is a feature, not a bug.

OPEN QUESTION
Should a threshold be introduced (e.g. minimum shareholding, time limit, or 'gateway' test) to filter out abusive petitions? The Law Commission thought so in 1997 — Parliament disagreed. Given the volume of s.994 litigation, the debate remains live.
Minority Protection.summary_line: S.994 · FOSS v HARBOTTLE · O'NEILL v PHILLIPS · LEGITIMATE EXPECTATION · CATCH-ALL CRITIQUE · LAW COMMISSION 1997 · BUY-OUT ORDER · REISBERG

Pre-emption Rights.front: What are statutory pre-emption rights on allotment of shares and how can they be disapplied?
Pre-emption Rights.answer: Under CA 2006 s.561, when a company proposes to allot new 'equity securities' for cash, it must first offer them to existing shareholders pro rata to their existing holdings (pre-emption rights). The offer must remain open for 21 days. Pre-emption rights can be disapplied: (1) by special resolution (s.570) — giving directors authority to allot without pre-emption; or (2) by a provision in the articles doing the same. In private companies, all shareholders can waive pre-emption rights by written agreement for a specific allotment. Pre-emption rights only apply to cash allotments — non-cash consideration allotments are exempt.
Pre-emption Rights.summary_line: CA 2006 S.561 · PRO RATA OFFER · 21 DAYS · DISAPPLICATION BY SPECIAL RESOLUTION S.570 · CASH ONLY

Pre-emption Rights.front:
Nova Ltd has three shareholders: Orla (50%), Pete (30%) and Quinn (20%). The directors want to allot 200 new shares for cash to a new investor, Rosa, without offering them to existing shareholders first. Orla and Pete agree. Quinn objects.

Advise on whether the allotment can proceed.
Pre-emption Rights.answer:
The allotment cannot proceed without first disapplying pre-emption rights or complying with them.

Statutory pre-emption rights (CA 2006 s.561): On a cash allotment of equity securities, the company must first offer the new shares to existing shareholders pro rata to their existing holdings. The offer must remain open for 21 days. Quinn is entitled to be offered 20% of the new shares (40 shares) before Rosa can be offered any.

Disapplication: Pre-emption rights can be disapplied by special resolution under s.570 (75% majority). Orla (50%) and Pete (30%) together hold 80% — sufficient to pass the special resolution disapplying pre-emption rights for this allotment. Quinn cannot block this.

However, even with a validly passed special resolution disapplying s.561 rights, the directors must also have authority to allot under s.551 (ordinary resolution or articles).

If the allotment proceeds without following s.561 and without a valid disapplication, the allotment is not void — but the company and any officer in default commit an offence (s.563), and Quinn could seek injunctive relief before the allotment is completed. After allotment, his remedy is more limited.
Pre-emption Rights.summary_line: PRE-EMPTION RIGHTS S.561 · PRO RATA OFFER · 21 DAYS · DISAPPLICATION S.570 SPECIAL RESOLUTION · AUTHORITY TO ALLOT S.551

Written Resolutions.front: What is the difference between an ordinary resolution and a special resolution, and when is each required?
Written Resolutions.answer: An ordinary resolution requires a simple majority (more than 50%) of votes cast (CA 2006 s.282). A special resolution requires a 75% supermajority of votes cast (s.283). Ordinary resolutions are used for routine matters including: removal of directors (s.168), approval of political donations. Special resolutions are required for: alteration of articles (s.21), change of company name (s.77), reduction of capital (s.641), re-registration (s.97), winding up by members (Insolvency Act 1986).
Written Resolutions.summary_line: ORDINARY = >50% · SPECIAL = 75% · KEY USES FOR EACH · CA 2006 SS.282–283

Written Resolutions.front: Can a private company pass resolutions without holding a physical meeting?
Written Resolutions.answer: Yes. Private companies can pass written resolutions under CA 2006 ss.288–300. A written resolution is passed when the required majority of eligible members have signed or otherwise indicated agreement. The same majority thresholds apply (simple majority for ordinary, 75% for special). The eligible members are those who would have been entitled to vote at a meeting. Written resolutions cannot be used to remove a director (s.168) or auditor — these must be passed at a meeting so the director/auditor has the right to speak.
Written Resolutions.summary_line: PRIVATE COMPANIES ONLY · CA 2006 SS.288–300 · SAME MAJORITY THRESHOLDS · CANNOT REMOVE DIRECTOR/AUDITOR

Written Resolutions.front: Are private companies required to hold an Annual General Meeting (AGM)?
Written Resolutions.answer: No. Private companies are not required to hold an AGM under the CA 2006. The obligation to hold an AGM applies only to public companies, which must hold one within 6 months of the end of each financial year (CA 2006 s.336). Private companies may choose to hold an AGM if their articles require it or if they choose to do so, but there is no statutory obligation.
Written Resolutions.summary_line: PRIVATE COMPANIES — NO AGM REQUIRED · PUBLIC COMPANIES — AGM WITHIN 6 MONTHS · CA 2006 S.336

Written Resolutions.front:
Which of the following decisions in a private company CANNOT be taken by written resolution — and why?

(a) Altering the articles of association
(b) Removing a director under s.168
(c) Disapplying pre-emption rights under s.570
(d) Removing an auditor before the end of their term
Written Resolutions.answer:
(b) and (d) cannot be passed by written resolution.

(b) Removal of a director under CA 2006 s.168 must be passed at a general meeting — not by written resolution. This is because the director has a statutory right under s.169 to receive notice of the proposed resolution, make written representations to members, and address the meeting in person. A written resolution procedure would deprive the director of the right to speak.

(d) Removal of an auditor before the end of their term of office (s.510) equally cannot be done by written resolution — the auditor has the right to make representations and address the general meeting (s.511).

(a) and (c) can be passed by written resolution — a special resolution (75%) for (a) and a special resolution for (c). The written resolution procedure is available for all decisions except those requiring the affected person to have a right to speak.
Written Resolutions.summary_line: WRITTEN RESOLUTION · CANNOT REMOVE DIRECTOR S.168 · CANNOT REMOVE AUDITOR S.510 · RIGHT TO SPEAK · CA 2006 SS.288–300

Written Resolutions.front: Maple Ltd is a private company with three shareholders. The directors want to allot 1,000 new shares to a new investor and need to disapply pre-emption rights. Can this be done without holding a meeting? If so, what majority is required?
Written Resolutions.answer:
Yes — this can be done by written resolution, as private companies may pass resolutions without holding a meeting under CA 2006 ss.288–300.

Disapplying pre-emption rights under s.570 requires a special resolution — a 75% majority of the eligible members (those who would have been entitled to vote at a general meeting). The eligible members are the three existing shareholders.

Procedure: The directors (or the company) circulate the proposed written resolution to all eligible members together with any accompanying information. Each member must signify agreement in writing (or electronically). The resolution lapses if not passed within 28 days (or longer if the articles permit). Once the required majority has agreed, the resolution is passed — there is no need to wait for all three shareholders to respond provided 75% have agreed.

If all three shareholders hold equal shares (each 33.3%), all three must agree to reach 75%. If the shareholding is weighted differently, two out of three might suffice depending on the exact split.

Once the disapplication is in place, the directors also need authority to allot under s.551 — this requires an ordinary resolution (or the articles provide it).
Written Resolutions.summary_line: WRITTEN RESOLUTION · PRIVATE COMPANY ONLY · SPECIAL RESOLUTION 75% · DISAPPLICATION PRE-EMPTION S.570 · 28-DAY LAPSE PERIOD

Written Resolutions.front: What is the Duomatic principle and when does it apply?
Written Resolutions.answer:
The Duomatic principle (Re Duomatic Ltd [1969]) provides that if all shareholders who have the right to attend and vote at a general meeting assent to a transaction, that assent is as binding as a formal resolution — even if no meeting is held and no written resolution is passed.

Requirements:
1. All shareholders with voting rights must consent.
2. They must have full knowledge of the transaction.
3. Their assent must be clear (can be informal — e.g., conduct, verbal agreement).

Key limit: cannot authorise an unlawful act (e.g., an illegal dividend or breach of the CA 2006 in a way that cannot be ratified). Does not override statutory requirements that mandate a formal procedure (e.g., s.190 CA 2006 substantial property transactions).

Distinguished from written resolutions (CA 2006 ss.288-300): those are the statutory mechanism; Duomatic is a common law equitable principle filling the gap where formalities are absent but unanimous consent is clear.
Written Resolutions.summary_line: DUOMATIC PRINCIPLE · RE DUOMATIC [1969] · UNANIMOUS INFORMAL CONSENT · AS EFFECTIVE AS FORMAL RESOLUTION · FULL KNOWLEDGE · LIMITS

Written Resolutions.front:
⚠️ TRAP CARD

Aaron is the sole director and 60% shareholder of Bright Ltd. The other 40% is held by his sister Beth, who is not involved in the business. Aaron emails Beth to say he intends to pay himself a £50,000 bonus from company funds without a board resolution or shareholder vote. Beth replies: 'Fine by me.' Aaron argues the Duomatic principle means the payment is authorised. Is he correct?
Written Resolutions.issue: The Trap: Students assume that because both shareholders have consented — even informally — the Duomatic principle validates the transaction.
Written Resolutions.rule: The Reality: Duomatic requires ALL shareholders with voting rights to consent with full knowledge of the transaction. Here that condition is met. However, the principle cannot authorise what is in substance an unlawful distribution or breach of director's duty without proper board approval. A director cannot use Duomatic as a substitute for the board resolution required to approve their own remuneration. The payment may also engage s.172 (duty to promote success) and s.175 (conflicts) — shareholder ratification does not automatically cure a breach of duty where the director is also a shareholder.
Written Resolutions.application: Why the Distractor Fails: The trap lies in treating shareholder consent as sufficient to authorise any act. Duomatic is a procedural shortcut for shareholder decisions — it does not replace the requirement for a valid board decision, nor does it ratify director conduct that is otherwise in breach of duty. Beth's informal consent gets Aaron past the shareholder approval hurdle but not the director duty hurdle.
Written Resolutions.conclusion: The Key Rule: Duomatic validates the shareholder consent element only. It does not cure breaches of director's duties or substitute for required board resolutions. Always ask: (1) does Duomatic satisfy the shareholder approval requirement? AND (2) is there a separate director-level obligation that still needs to be met?
Written Resolutions.summary_line: DUOMATIC · SHAREHOLDER CONSENT ONLY · DOES NOT CURE DIRECTOR DUTY BREACH · BOARD RESOLUTION STILL REQUIRED

Dividends.front: When can a company lawfully pay a dividend?
Dividends.answer: A company may only pay a dividend out of distributable profits — its accumulated realised profits less its accumulated realised losses (CA 2006 s.830). A public company has an additional restriction: it may not distribute if the distribution would reduce its net assets below the aggregate of its called-up share capital and undistributable reserves (s.831). Dividends are declared by shareholders at a general meeting (final dividend) or paid by directors (interim dividend) — both must satisfy the distributable profits test. An unlawful dividend is recoverable from the shareholder if they knew or had reasonable grounds to believe it was unlawful.
Dividends.summary_line: CA 2006 S.830 · DISTRIBUTABLE PROFITS ONLY · ACCUMULATED REALISED PROFITS - LOSSES · UNLAWFUL DIVIDEND RECOVERABLE

Dividends.front:
Upton Ltd made a profit of £80,000 in its most recent financial year. It has accumulated losses of £120,000 from previous years. The directors want to declare a dividend of £30,000 to the shareholders.

Can the company lawfully pay this dividend?
Dividends.answer:
No. The dividend would be unlawful.

Under CA 2006 s.830, a company may only make a distribution out of profits available for the purpose — defined as accumulated realised profits less accumulated realised losses. This is a cumulative test: it is not sufficient to look only at this year's profit in isolation.

Applying the test: £80,000 (this year's realised profit) less £120,000 (accumulated realised losses from prior years) = net deficit of £40,000. There are no distributable profits. The company cannot lawfully pay any dividend.

If the dividend were paid in these circumstances: (1) it would be an unlawful distribution; (2) shareholders who received it knowing it was unlawful (or who had reasonable grounds to know) would be required to repay it; (3) directors who authorised it could face liability for misfeasance.

The company needs to generate further profits in future years to eliminate the accumulated deficit before dividends can lawfully be paid.
Dividends.summary_line: CA 2006 S.830 · DISTRIBUTABLE PROFITS · CUMULATIVE TEST · ACCUMULATED LOSSES · NO DISTRIBUTION POSSIBLE · UNLAWFUL DIVIDEND RECOVERABLE

New Share Allotment.front: What authority must directors have to allot shares and how is it obtained?
New Share Allotment.answer: Directors of a company with more than one class of shares must have authority to allot shares (CA 2006 s.551). This authority can be conferred: (1) by an ordinary resolution of shareholders; or (2) by the articles of association. The resolution must state the maximum amount that may be allotted and an expiry date (not more than 5 years). Private companies with only one class of share have an automatic authority to allot shares under s.550 without shareholder approval. Public companies always need shareholder authority.
New Share Allotment.summary_line: CA 2006 S.551 · ORDINARY RESOLUTION OR ARTICLES · MAX AMOUNT + EXPIRY · PRIVATE ONE-CLASS = AUTO AUTHORITY S.550

New Share Allotment.front: What is a share premium and how must it be treated?
New Share Allotment.answer: A share premium arises when shares are issued at a price above their nominal value. The excess over nominal value must be credited to the share premium account (CA 2006 s.610). The share premium account is treated as share capital for most purposes — it is subject to the capital maintenance rules and generally cannot be distributed to shareholders as dividends. It can be used for: issuing bonus shares; writing off formation expenses; writing off share issue expenses; and (with court approval) reduction of capital.
New Share Allotment.summary_line: PREMIUM ABOVE NOMINAL VALUE · SHARE PREMIUM ACCOUNT · CA 2006 S.610 · TREATED AS CAPITAL · CANNOT DISTRIBUTE

New Share Allotment.front: What is the difference between ordinary shares and preference shares?
New Share Allotment.answer: Ordinary shares typically carry: voting rights at general meetings; a right to dividends (if declared); and a right to a return of capital on winding up — all after preference shareholders have been satisfied. Preference shares typically carry: a right to a fixed dividend (often cumulative — unpaid dividends accrue) in priority to ordinary shareholders; and a priority return of capital on winding up. Preference shares may or may not carry voting rights (usually limited). The precise rights depend on the articles/terms of issue. Cumulative preference dividends — if not paid — accumulate and must be paid before any ordinary dividend.
New Share Allotment.summary_line: ORDINARY = VOTES + RESIDUAL RIGHTS · PREFERENCE = FIXED PRIORITY DIVIDEND + CAPITAL PRIORITY · CUMULATIVE

New Share Allotment.front: Explain the difference between the directors' authority to allot shares under CA 2006 s.550 and s.551. When does each apply?
New Share Allotment.answer:
S.550 — AUTOMATIC AUTHORITY (PRIVATE COMPANIES, ONE CLASS OF SHARES)
Where a private company has only one class of shares, the directors may allot shares of that class without needing shareholder authorisation, unless the articles prohibit or restrict this. This automatic authority under s.550 avoids the need for a shareholder resolution every time the directors wish to issue shares — simplifying the process for straightforward single-class companies.

Conditions for s.550 to apply:
• Private company only (public companies cannot use s.550)
• Company has only one class of shares at the time of allotment
• Articles do not restrict or prohibit the authority

S.551 — SHAREHOLDER AUTHORISATION REQUIRED
In all other cases (public companies; private companies with more than one class of shares; or where the articles remove s.550 authority), directors need authorisation from the shareholders by ordinary resolution (or in the articles) to allot shares. The resolution must state:
• The maximum number / nominal amount of shares that may be allotted; and
• The expiry date of the authority (maximum 5 years; renewable).

Note: In both cases, statutory pre-emption rights (s.561) apply separately to cash allotments and must be complied with or disapplied, regardless of whether authority arises under s.550 or s.551.
New Share Allotment.summary_line: S.550 AUTOMATIC AUTHORITY · PRIVATE + ONE CLASS · NO ARTICLES RESTRICTION · S.551 ORDINARY RESOLUTION · PUBLIC / MULTI-CLASS · MAX 5 YEARS · PRE-EMPTION SEPARATE

New Share Allotment.front: What are statutory pre-emption rights on allotment under CA 2006 s.561, and what are the procedures for disapplying them?
New Share Allotment.answer:
STATUTORY PRE-EMPTION RIGHTS (S.561)
Where a company proposes to allot 'equity securities' (broadly, ordinary shares or rights to subscribe for them) wholly for cash, it must first offer those securities to existing shareholders in proportion to their existing holdings (pro rata). The offer must:
• Be made on the same or more favourable terms than those offered to the outside investor
• Remain open for acceptance for at least 21 days
• Be made in writing

Only if shareholders decline or the offer period expires without full takeup can the remaining shares be offered to outsiders.

DISAPPLICATION OF PRE-EMPTION RIGHTS
Pre-emption rights can be disapplied in two ways:

1. By the articles (s.568): The articles may exclude or modify s.561. This applies to all future allotments unless and until the articles are amended.

2. By special resolution (s.570): Shareholders can pass a special resolution (75% majority) disapplying s.561 for a specific allotment or generally for a period (maximum 5 years). Must be accompanied by a directors' written statement of reasons (for listed companies, more detailed disclosure is required).

NOTE: Pre-emption rights do not apply to: non-cash allotments; allotments of shares under employee share schemes; bonus shares. They apply only to equity securities allotted for cash.
New Share Allotment.summary_line: S.561 PRE-EMPTION · PRO RATA OFFER · 21 DAYS · CASH ONLY · DISAPPLICATION S.568 (ARTICLES) OR S.570 (SPECIAL RESOLUTION) · BONUS / NON-CASH EXCLUDED

New Share Allotment.front:
The board of Apex Ltd wants to raise £200,000 by allotting new shares to an external investor for cash. Apex is a private company with two classes of shares and three existing shareholders. The articles do not grant the directors any authority to allot.

What steps must the company take before the allotment can proceed?
New Share Allotment.answer:
Three steps are required:

1. Authority to allot (CA 2006 s.551): Apex has more than one class of shares, so the automatic authority in s.550 (for single-class private companies) does not apply. The directors need authority from the shareholders by ordinary resolution, specifying the maximum amount that may be allotted and an expiry date (maximum 5 years). This is passed by a simple majority (>50%).

2. Disapplication of pre-emption rights (CA 2006 ss.561, 570): Because the new shares are being allotted for cash, existing shareholders have statutory pre-emption rights — the right to be offered the shares pro rata before the external investor. To allot to the external investor directly, these rights must be disapplied by special resolution (75% majority) or under a provision in the articles (none here). Alternatively, the company could offer the shares to existing shareholders first and only allot to the investor any shares they decline.

3. Allotment and registration: Once authority and disapplication are in place, the directors can pass a board resolution to allot, issue share certificates, and update the register of members. A return of allotment (SH01) must be filed at Companies House within one month (CA 2006 s.555).
New Share Allotment.summary_line: AUTHORITY TO ALLOT S.551 · ORDINARY RESOLUTION · DISAPPLICATION S.570 SPECIAL RESOLUTION · PRE-EMPTION S.561 · SH01 WITHIN ONE MONTH

Preference Shares.front:
Bluebell Ltd has 1,000 ordinary shares and 500 cumulative preference shares carrying a 5% fixed annual dividend on their nominal value of £1 each. The company paid no dividend in Year 1 or Year 2. In Year 3 it has distributable profits of £200,000. The board declares a dividend of £100,000 to ordinary shareholders only.

Advise the preference shareholders.
Preference Shares.answer:
The preference shareholders have priority over ordinary shareholders for their dividend and can challenge this distribution.

Rights of cumulative preference shareholders: Preference shares carry a fixed dividend (here 5% of £1 nominal = 5p per share per year). 'Cumulative' means that if the dividend is not paid in any year, the arrears accumulate and must be paid before any dividend can be declared on ordinary shares.

Arrears calculation: Year 1 unpaid: 500 × 5p = £25. Year 2 unpaid: £25. Year 3 current: £25. Total arrears and current preference dividend = £75.

Priority: Before any ordinary dividend can be paid, the accumulated preference dividend of £75 (covering all three years) must be satisfied. The board's declaration of £100,000 to ordinary shareholders without first paying the £75 arrears to preference shareholders is a breach of the preference shareholders' rights as set out in the articles/terms of issue.

The preference shareholders can bring a claim to enforce their contractual right to priority payment. The £100,000 distribution is not unlawful under s.830 (there are ample distributable profits) — the issue is the order of payment. The ordinary shareholders should be required to return the £75 portion that should have gone to preference shareholders first.
Preference Shares.summary_line: CUMULATIVE PREFERENCE SHARES · ARREARS ACCUMULATE · PRIORITY OVER ORDINARY DIVIDEND · BREACH OF RIGHTS · DISTRIBUTABLE PROFITS SEPARATE ISSUE

Share Transfer.front: How are shares in a private company transferred and what restrictions commonly apply?
Share Transfer.answer: Shares are transferred using a stock transfer form (signed by the transferor), delivered with the share certificate to the company. The directors of a private company typically have a discretion to refuse to register a transfer under the articles (the Model Articles give directors a broad discretion). Common restrictions in private company articles include: right of first refusal (pre-emption on transfer) — existing shareholders must be offered shares before sale to a third party; and director discretion to refuse registration. Stamp duty at 0.5% of the consideration is payable by the buyer on transfers above £1,000. ⚠️ Verify current stamp duty threshold.
Share Transfer.summary_line: STOCK TRANSFER FORM · DIRECTOR DISCRETION · PRE-EMPTION ON TRANSFER · STAMP DUTY 0.5%

Share Transfer.front:
Victor wants to transfer his 25% shareholding in Widget Ltd to his daughter, Wendy. The company's articles give the directors an absolute discretion to refuse to register a transfer. Victor completes a stock transfer form and submits it with the share certificate. The directors refuse to register the transfer without giving any reason.

Advise Victor and Wendy.
Share Transfer.answer:
The directors have power under the articles to refuse to register the transfer, but that power must be exercised in accordance with their duties.

Under the articles, the discretion to refuse is described as 'absolute'. However, directors exercising such a discretion must act bona fide in what they consider to be the interests of the company (Re Smith & Fawcett [1942]). An unexplained refusal may be challenged if it was exercised for an improper purpose or in bad faith.

Procedure: Once the stock transfer form is received, the company must either register the transfer or give notice of refusal within two months (CA 2006 s.771). If it refuses, it must notify the transferee. The company does not have to give reasons for refusal (unless the articles require it). However, if no notice of refusal is given within two months, the company is in default (though the transfer is not automatically registered).

Victor's options: He can challenge the refusal by seeking a court order under CA 2006 s.771 that the register be rectified. He would need to show the refusal was improper. Alternatively, if the refusal constitutes part of a pattern of unfairly prejudicial conduct, Victor could petition under s.994.

Stamp duty: Wendy (as transferee) must pay stamp duty at 0.5% on any consideration paid — if this is a gift, no stamp duty arises.
Share Transfer.summary_line: DIRECTOR DISCRETION TO REFUSE · BONA FIDE REQUIREMENT · CA 2006 S.771 · TWO MONTH NOTICE · COURT RECTIFICATION · STAMP DUTY 0.5%

Crystallisation of Floating Charge.front:
Falcon Ltd has a floating charge over all its current assets in favour of a lender. The company enters administration. At the date of administration, Falcon's stock is worth £200,000. One week before administration, Falcon sold £50,000 worth of stock to a customer in the ordinary course of business.

What is the position of the lender with respect to the stock?
Crystallisation of Floating Charge.answer:
The lender is entitled to the £200,000 of stock remaining at the date of administration, but cannot claim the £50,000 worth of stock already sold.

Nature of floating charge: A floating charge hovers over a class of assets as they exist from time to time. Until crystallisation, the company can deal freely with those assets in the ordinary course of business — buying, selling, and replacing stock without needing the chargee's consent.

The £50,000 stock sale: This occurred one week before administration, in the ordinary course of Falcon's business. Because the floating charge had not yet crystallised at that point, the sale was entirely valid and the customer takes good title free of the charge. The lender has no claim against the customer or the stock sold.

Crystallisation on administration: The appointment of an administrator is a crystallisation event. The floating charge crystallises automatically at that moment — it becomes a fixed charge over all the current assets then owned by Falcon (i.e. the remaining £200,000 of stock). The lender's security now attaches to those specific assets.

In the administration, the lender ranks as a floating chargee. The prescribed part (IA 1986 s.176A) and preferential creditors take priority from the floating charge realisations before the lender receives anything.
Crystallisation of Floating Charge.summary_line: FLOATING CHARGE · FREE DEALING PRE-CRYSTALLISATION · CRYSTALLISES ON ADMINISTRATION · FIXED ON ASSETS AT DATE · PRESCRIBED PART S.176A

Fixed vs Floating Charge.front: What is the difference between a fixed charge and a floating charge?
Fixed vs Floating Charge.answer: A fixed charge attaches immediately to a specific identified asset — the chargor cannot deal with the asset without the chargee's consent (e.g. a mortgage over land). A floating charge 'floats' over a class of assets (e.g. stock, book debts) as they exist from time to time — the company can deal freely with those assets in the ordinary course of business until the charge crystallises. Crystallisation occurs when: the company goes into liquidation or administration; the chargee appoints a receiver; or a crystallisation event specified in the charge occurs. On crystallisation, the floating charge becomes fixed on the assets then owned.
Fixed vs Floating Charge.summary_line: FIXED = SPECIFIC ASSET IMMEDIATELY · FLOATING = CLASS OF ASSETS · CRYSTALLISATION · FREE DEALING UNTIL CRYSTALLISED

Fixed vs Floating Charge.front: What are the rules on registration of charges created by a company?
Fixed vs Floating Charge.answer: A charge created by a company must be registered with Companies House within 21 days of creation (CA 2006 s.859A). If not registered, the charge is void against a liquidator, administrator, or any creditor of the company — though the underlying debt remains valid and payable on demand. The charge is registered by filing a statement of particulars (MR01). The company or the chargee may file. Late registration is possible with court permission. A company must also keep a register of charges at its registered office or the SAIL address.
Fixed vs Floating Charge.summary_line: CA 2006 S.859A · 21 DAYS · VOID AGAINST LIQUIDATOR/ADMINISTRATOR IF NOT REGISTERED · MR01 FORM

Fixed vs Floating Charge.front: What is the order of priority between charges over the same asset?
Fixed vs Floating Charge.answer: Generally: (1) fixed charges rank in order of creation (first in time prevails) unless the later chargee takes without notice of the earlier charge; (2) a fixed charge has priority over a floating charge over the same asset, even if the floating charge was created first — unless the floating charge contains a negative pledge clause and the fixed chargee had notice of it; (3) floating charges rank in order of creation among themselves. Registered charges give constructive notice to subsequent chargees. The negative pledge in a floating charge can restrict the company from granting a fixed charge on the same assets.
Fixed vs Floating Charge.summary_line: FIXED BEFORE FLOATING · FIRST IN TIME · NEGATIVE PLEDGE · CONSTRUCTIVE NOTICE FROM REGISTRATION

Fixed vs Floating Charge.front:
Classify each of the following as a fixed charge or a floating charge, giving reasons:

(a) A charge over a company's freehold factory
(b) A charge described as 'fixed' over a company's book debts, where the company is free to collect the debts and pay the proceeds into its general bank account
(c) A charge over all the company's stock in trade as it exists from time to time
Fixed vs Floating Charge.answer:
(a) Fixed charge. The factory is a specific, identified asset. The company cannot deal with it freely — it would need the chargee's consent to sell or further charge it. This is the paradigm case of a fixed charge.

(b) Floating charge, despite the 'fixed' label. In Re Spectrum Plus [2005], the House of Lords held that for a charge over book debts to be truly fixed, the company must be required to pay the collected proceeds into a blocked account from which it cannot withdraw without the chargee's consent. If the company can collect debts and use the proceeds freely, the charge is a floating charge regardless of how it is labelled. Labels are not determinative.

(c) Floating charge. Stock in trade is a constantly changing class of assets. The company must be able to deal with it freely in the ordinary course of business — buying and selling stock. This is the classic subject matter of a floating charge.
Fixed vs Floating Charge.summary_line: FIXED vs FLOATING · RE SPECTRUM PLUS [2005] · BOOK DEBTS · BLOCKED ACCOUNT · LABEL NOT DETERMINATIVE · STOCK = FLOATING

Fixed vs Floating Charge.issue: Whether a charge over book debts, where the chargor was required to pay collected proceeds into a specific account but could then draw freely on that account, was a fixed or floating charge.
Fixed vs Floating Charge.rule: For a charge over book debts to be a fixed charge, the chargor must be prohibited from dealing with the debts as they are collected — specifically, the proceeds must be paid into a blocked account from which the chargor cannot withdraw without the chargee's consent. If the chargor is free to use the proceeds in the ordinary course of business, the charge is a floating charge regardless of the label used in the debenture.
Fixed vs Floating Charge.application: Spectrum had a debenture requiring it to pay book debt proceeds into its account with NatWest but giving it free access to those funds. The House of Lords overruled Siebe Gorman v Barclays Bank (which had been relied on for two decades) and held the charge was floating, not fixed. The label 'fixed charge' in the document was not determinative.
Fixed vs Floating Charge.conclusion: The definitive authority on the fixed/floating distinction for book debts. Chargees must impose a genuine restriction on the use of collected proceeds to achieve fixed charge status. The decision also confirmed that a floating charge is one that 'hovers' over assets until crystallisation — the freedom to deal is the key distinguishing characteristic.
Fixed vs Floating Charge.summary_line: FIXED vs FLOATING · BOOK DEBTS · BLOCKED ACCOUNT REQUIRED · LABEL NOT DETERMINATIVE · OVERRULED SIEBE GORMAN · HOUSE OF LORDS [2005]
