Fixed vs Floating Charge.front:
A bank takes a debenture over Delta Ltd's assets described as:
(a) 'A fixed charge over Delta's book debts' — the debenture requires Delta to pay all collected book debt proceeds into a designated account at the bank, but Delta can draw on that account freely for business purposes.
(b) 'A fixed charge over Delta's freehold warehouse' — Delta cannot sell or further charge the warehouse without the bank's consent.

Classify each charge.
Fixed vs Floating Charge.answer:
(a) Is a floating charge, despite the 'fixed' label.

Under Re Spectrum Plus [2005] (House of Lords), for a charge over book debts to be truly fixed, the proceeds collected must be paid into a blocked account from which Delta cannot withdraw without the bank's consent. If Delta can draw freely on the designated account, there is no genuine restriction on dealing with the proceeds. The label 'fixed charge' is not determinative. The charge is therefore a floating charge — it hovers over the book debts as they come in and go out, with Delta free to use the proceeds in its business. On crystallisation (e.g. administration or liquidation), it becomes fixed on whatever debts then exist.

This has significant practical consequences: a floating charge ranks behind fixed charges, liquidation expenses, and preferential creditors; and part of the realisations must go into the prescribed part fund for unsecured creditors (IA 1986 s.176A).

(b) Is a genuine fixed charge.

The warehouse is a specific, identified asset. Delta cannot deal with it without the bank's consent. There is no freedom to substitute or dispose. This is the classic fixed charge. The bank's security interest attaches immediately to the asset and is not affected by what happens to the rest of Delta's assets.
Fixed vs Floating Charge.summary_line: RE SPECTRUM PLUS · FIXED vs FLOATING · BLOCKED ACCOUNT REQUIRED · LABEL NOT DETERMINATIVE · PRESCRIBED PART S.176A

Fixed vs Floating Charge.front: Going deeper: Is the distinction between fixed and floating charges — and in particular the Re Spectrum Plus [2005] test for book debts — commercially workable, or does it produce arbitrary results that undermine secured lending?
Fixed vs Floating Charge.answer:
THE SPECTRUM PLUS RULE
Re Spectrum Plus [2005] (HL) overruled two decades of commercial practice built on Siebe Gorman v Barclays Bank [1979]. Slade J in Siebe Gorman had held that a charge over book debts requiring proceeds to be paid into the company's bank account (even if the account could be drawn on freely) could be a fixed charge. Spectrum Plus corrected this: a true fixed charge requires a blocked account — the chargor cannot access the proceeds without the chargee's consent.

COMMERCIAL IMPACT
The immediate effect was that vast swathes of 'fixed charge' debentures entered into before 2005 were retrospectively reclassified as floating charges. This reduced lenders' priority (floating charges are subordinated to preferential creditors and the prescribed part under s.176A IA 1986) and increased their exposure. The banking industry lobbied for prospective-only overruling, but the House of Lords declined — applying Spectrum Plus retrospectively.

THE ACADEMIC CRITIQUE
Goode ('The Exodus of the Fixed Charge') argued that the rigid blocked-account requirement is commercially impractical: requiring all collected receivables to sit in a blocked account disrupts everyday cash management and increases financing costs. The distinction between fixed and floating, he argued, should be reconsidered as an asset-monitoring concept rather than a dealing-freedom concept. Worthington ('Fixed Charges over Book Debts' [2004]) had pre-empted Spectrum, arguing the categorisation was incoherent.

DEFENDERS OF THE RULE
McCormack and others argue the clarity of the Spectrum rule is valuable: lenders now know exactly what they must do to achieve fixed charge status (impose a genuine blocked account). The rule may have increased financing costs, but it produced a definite, predictable standard. The commercial community has adapted.

COMPARATIVE AND REFORM NOTE
Article 9 of the US Uniform Commercial Code treats all security interests in receivables similarly, focusing on perfection and priority rather than the fixed/floating distinction. The Law Commission's 2002 Report on Company Security Interests proposed replacing the fixed/floating distinction with a unitary registration regime — a proposal the Government has never implemented. ⚠️ Verify whether any reform has occurred since 2026.

OPEN QUESTION
Should England abolish the fixed/floating distinction and adopt a UCC-style registration-based priority system? The Law Commission thought yes in 2002. The economic case is strong — but the political will has been absent.
Fixed vs Floating Charge.summary_line: RE SPECTRUM PLUS · SIEBE GORMAN OVERRULED · BLOCKED ACCOUNT · RETROSPECTIVE · PRESCRIBED PART · GOODE · WORTHINGTON · LAW COMMISSION 2002 · UCC COMPARISON

Negative Pledge.front:
Eagle Ltd has a floating charge over all its assets in favour of Bank X. The floating charge debenture contains a negative pledge clause prohibiting Eagle from creating any further charges without Bank X's consent. Eagle later creates a fixed charge over its freehold factory in favour of Bank Y, without telling Bank X. Bank Y is aware of the floating charge but does not search for negative pledge clauses.

What is the priority position between Bank X and Bank Y?
Negative Pledge.answer:
This turns on whether Bank Y had notice of Bank X's negative pledge clause.

General rule: A fixed charge takes priority over a pre-existing floating charge over the same assets — because the floating charge only attaches on crystallisation, while the fixed charge attaches immediately. Bank Y's fixed charge would normally take priority.

Negative pledge exception: Where a floating charge contains a negative pledge and the later chargee (Bank Y) takes with notice of it, the floating charge retains priority over the later fixed charge. The rationale is that Bank Y cannot claim to be a bona fide purchaser if it knew the chargor was not permitted to grant further charges.

Registration: The floating charge is registered at Companies House, which gives constructive notice of the charge itself. However, registration of a charge does not give constructive notice of its terms (including any negative pledge clause). Bank Y would need actual notice of the negative pledge for the exception to apply.

On the facts: Bank Y knew of the floating charge (actual notice) but did not search for negative pledge clauses. Whether this constitutes notice of the negative pledge is debatable. In practice, banks conducting proper due diligence should search for negative pledge clauses. If a court finds Bank Y ought to have known, Bank X may retain priority. ⚠️ This area involves nuanced case law — recommend further research.
Negative Pledge.summary_line: NEGATIVE PLEDGE · FIXED BEFORE FLOATING GENERAL RULE · NOTICE OF NEGATIVE PLEDGE EXCEPTION · REGISTRATION ≠ CONSTRUCTIVE NOTICE OF TERMS

Registration of Charges.front:
Cedar Ltd creates a fixed charge over its freehold property in favour of Bank A on 1 March. Bank A fails to register the charge at Companies House. On 20 March, Cedar Ltd creates a floating charge over all its assets in favour of Bank B, which Bank B registers promptly. Cedar Ltd enters insolvent liquidation on 30 April.

What is the position of Bank A and Bank B?
Registration of Charges.answer:
Bank A's fixed charge is void against the liquidator and against Bank B.

Registration requirement (CA 2006 s.859A): A charge created by a company must be registered with Companies House within 21 days of creation. Bank A's charge was created on 1 March. The 21-day period expired on 22 March. Bank A failed to register. Under s.859H, an unregistered charge is void against the liquidator, administrator, and any creditor of the company. This means Bank A cannot enforce the fixed charge against Cedar's estate in the liquidation. The underlying debt remains valid and becomes immediately payable — but Bank A ranks as an unsecured creditor only.

Bank B's floating charge was validly registered. Bank B therefore takes priority over Bank A's unregistered charge. In the liquidation, Bank B (as a floating chargee) will benefit from the prescribed part regime (IA 1986 s.176A) and rank after liquidation expenses and preferential creditors but ahead of unsecured creditors for the net floating charge realisations.

Note: Even if Bank A had registered, the fixed charge (created first) would generally have priority over the later floating charge — but Bank A's failure to register has cost it that priority entirely.
Registration of Charges.summary_line: CA 2006 S.859A · 21 DAYS · UNREGISTERED = VOID AGAINST LIQUIDATOR · DEBT SURVIVES · BANK A UNSECURED · BANK B PRIORITY

Capital Allowances.front:
Juniper Ltd buys new manufacturing machinery for £300,000 in its accounting year. The machinery qualifies for the Annual Investment Allowance (AIA). The company's trading profit before capital allowances is £450,000.

Explain how capital allowances affect the company's taxable profit. ⚠️ Verify the current AIA limit.
Capital Allowances.answer:
⚠️ The AIA limit changes — verify the current limit against HMRC before advising.

Capital allowances replace depreciation as the tax deduction for capital expenditure on qualifying plant and machinery. Depreciation charged in the accounts is added back for tax purposes (not deductible), and capital allowances are deducted instead.

AIA: The AIA provides a 100% first-year deduction for qualifying plant and machinery up to the AIA limit. Assuming the current AIA limit is at least £300,000 (⚠️ verify), Juniper can claim the full £300,000 as an AIA deduction in the year of purchase.

Taxable profit calculation:
Trading profit before allowances:  £450,000
Less: AIA on machinery:           (£300,000)
Taxable trading profit:            £150,000

This significantly reduces the corporation tax bill. At the main rate of 25% (⚠️ verify), the tax on £150,000 is £37,500 rather than £112,500 on the full £450,000. The timing advantage is the key benefit of the AIA — full relief in year one rather than written down over many years at 18% or 6%.
Capital Allowances.summary_line: AIA · 100% FIRST YEAR · REPLACES DEPRECIATION · REDUCES TAXABLE PROFIT · ⚠️ VERIFY AIA LIMIT AND CT RATE

Capital Gains Tax.front: What is Business Asset Disposal Relief (BADR) and what conditions must be met?
Capital Gains Tax.answer: ⚠️ Verify the current BADR rate and conditions against HMRC guidance — these have been subject to change. Business Asset Disposal Relief (formerly Entrepreneurs' Relief) reduces the CGT rate on qualifying gains to a lower rate (⚠️ confirm current rate). Key conditions for a sole trader/partner: (1) the business must have been owned and operated for at least 2 years ending with the disposal; (2) BADR applies to gains on disposal of the whole or part of a business, or assets used in the business at the time it ceases. For company shares: the individual must hold at least 5% of ordinary shares and voting rights, and be an employee/officer, for at least 2 years. A lifetime limit on qualifying gains applies (⚠️ verify limit).
Capital Gains Tax.summary_line: BADR · FORMERLY ENTREPRENEURS' RELIEF · LOWER CGT RATE · 2 YEARS OWNERSHIP · 5% SHARES · LIFETIME LIMIT · ⚠️ VERIFY RATE

Capital Gains Tax.front: What are the qualifying conditions for Business Asset Disposal Relief (BADR) and what CGT rate applies? ⚠️ Verify current conditions and rate.
Capital Gains Tax.answer:
⚠️ BADR conditions and rates are subject to change in Finance Acts — always verify against current HMRC guidance before advising a client.

BUSINESS ASSET DISPOSAL RELIEF (formerly Entrepreneurs' Relief)

BADR reduces the CGT rate to 10% on qualifying gains (up to the lifetime limit).

Qualifying conditions (for disposal of shares in a personal company):
Throughout the 2-year period ending on the date of disposal, the individual must:
1. Hold at least 5% of the ordinary share capital of the company;
2. Hold at least 5% of the voting rights; AND
3. Be an employee or officer (director) of the company.

For sole traders / partnerships: the business must have been owned for at least 2 years and be a trading business (not investment).

Lifetime limit: £1,000,000 of qualifying gains (reduced from £10m in 2020). ⚠️ Verify current limit.

Rate: 10% on qualifying gains (vs 20% standard CGT rate for higher rate taxpayers on most assets). ⚠️ CGT rates changed in October 2024 Budget — verify the current rate carefully.

BADR is not available on: investment companies, non-trading companies, or where the 2-year holding / employment condition is not met at the relevant date.
Capital Gains Tax.summary_line: BADR · 10% CGT RATE · ⚠️ VERIFY · 2-YEAR HOLDING · 5% SHARES + VOTES · EMPLOYEE/OFFICER · £1M LIFETIME LIMIT · TRADING ONLY

Capital Gains Tax.front: How does CGT apply on the disposal of a sole trader business or partnership share? How does this differ from a share disposal by a shareholder?
Capital Gains Tax.answer:
SOLE TRADER / PARTNERSHIP DISPOSAL
On cessation or sale of a sole trader business or a partner's share, CGT is charged on chargeable gains arising on disposal of the underlying business assets (goodwill, property, plant and machinery if above cost). The gain = disposal proceeds − base cost − allowable expenditure.
BADR may apply if: the business has been owned for 2+ years and is a qualifying trading business (not investment). The individual must have been running the business throughout. ⚠️ Verify conditions.

SHARE DISPOSAL BY SHAREHOLDER
A shareholder selling shares in a company realises a capital gain (or loss) on the difference between the sale proceeds and the cost of the shares. CGT is charged on the individual shareholder, not the company.
BADR may apply if: the individual holds 5%+ shares and votes and has been an employee/officer for 2+ years.

KEY CONTRASTS:
• Asset sale: SDLT on land; VAT on non-TOGC assets; TUPE on employees
• Share sale: stamp duty (0.5%); no VAT; no TUPE (employees stay in company)
• Tax on gain: both subject to CGT; BADR rate (10%) may apply to both ⚠️
• MVL distributions may be treated as capital (CGT) rather than income — potentially qualifying for BADR ⚠️ Verify — HMRC has challenged some uses.

Income tax applies (not CGT) where the disposal is treated as trading income (e.g. property dealer) or where dividends are extracted rather than capital distributions made.
Capital Gains Tax.summary_line: CGT ON BUSINESS DISPOSAL · SOLE TRADER / PARTNERSHIP / SHARES · BADR 10% ⚠️ · ASSET SALE vs SHARE SALE · SDLT · STAMP DUTY · TUPE · MVL DISTRIBUTIONS

Corporation Tax.front: What is the basis on which a company pays corporation tax?
Corporation Tax.answer: A UK-resident company pays corporation tax on its total profits for each accounting period. Total profits include: trading income (adjusted for capital allowances and disallowable expenditure); property income; investment income; and chargeable gains (less allowable losses). The accounting period for corporation tax purposes is usually the company's financial year but is capped at 12 months. Corporation tax is self-assessed and must be paid nine months and one day after the end of the accounting period (for companies not paying by instalments). ⚠️ Verify the current CT rate — it has been subject to change. From April 2023 the main rate is 25% with a small profits rate.
Corporation Tax.summary_line: CT ON TOTAL PROFITS · ACCOUNTING PERIOD · SELF-ASSESSED · 9 MONTHS + 1 DAY · ⚠️ VERIFY CURRENT RATE

Corporation Tax.front: What is the small profits rate of corporation tax and how does it apply?
Corporation Tax.answer: ⚠️ Verify against current HMRC rates before relying on this — CT rates are subject to change and have been amended in recent Finance Acts. From April 2023: the main rate of corporation tax is 25%. The small profits rate of 19% applies to companies with profits of £50,000 or less per year. Companies with profits between £50,000 and £250,000 pay a tapered rate (marginal relief applies). The profit limits are apportioned for short accounting periods and divided by the number of associated companies. Companies with profits above £250,000 pay the full 25% main rate. ⚠️ The associated companies rules are complex — verify against current HMRC guidance.
Corporation Tax.summary_line: ⚠️ SMALL PROFITS RATE 19% (≤£50K) · MARGINAL RELIEF (£50K–£250K) · MAIN RATE 25% (>£250K) · ASSOCIATED COMPANIES

Corporation Tax.front: What are capital allowances and how do they reduce a company's taxable profit?
Corporation Tax.answer: Capital allowances allow a company to deduct the cost of capital expenditure on plant and machinery (and other qualifying assets) against taxable profits. Depreciation charged in the accounts is not tax-deductible; capital allowances are the tax equivalent. The main rates are: (1) Annual Investment Allowance (AIA) — 100% deduction up to a specified annual limit for qualifying plant and machinery (⚠️ verify current AIA limit); (2) Main pool — 18% writing-down allowance (WDA) on a reducing balance; (3) Special rate pool — 6% WDA (e.g. integral features). First-year allowances (FYAs) at 100% are available for certain energy-efficient plant.
Corporation Tax.summary_line: CAPITAL ALLOWANCES · AIA · MAIN POOL 18% WDA · SPECIAL RATE 6% WDA · NOT DEPRECIATION · ⚠️ VERIFY AIA LIMIT

Corporation Tax.front: How is a company's Total Taxable Profits (TTP) calculated for corporation tax purposes? Outline the key components and adjustments.
Corporation Tax.answer:
Corporation tax is charged on a company's Total Taxable Profits (TTP), calculated as follows:

1. Trading profits: Start with accounting profit, then:
   + Add back: depreciation, entertaining (non-deductible), capital expenditure
   − Deduct: capital allowances (AIA / writing-down allowances), qualifying    revenue expenditure
   = Adjusted trading profit

2. Add: Non-trading income (e.g. rental income, interest received if non-trading)

3. Add: Chargeable gains (capital gains on disposal of chargeable assets, net of indexation where applicable — ⚠️ indexation allowance frozen for companies; verify current position)

4. Less: Qualifying charitable donations (Gift Aid)

5. Less: Losses brought forward (trading losses, capital losses on capital gains only)

= TOTAL TAXABLE PROFITS (TTP)

Corporation tax rate then applies to TTP:
• Small profits rate (19%): profits ≤ £50,000 ⚠️
• Main rate (25%): profits ≥ £250,000 ⚠️
• Marginal relief: between £50,000–£250,000 ⚠️
• Thresholds divided by number of associated companies ⚠️

⚠️ Verify all rates, thresholds and associated company rules against current HMRC guidance — these change frequently.
Corporation Tax.summary_line: TTP = TRADING PROFIT ± ADJUSTMENTS + GAINS + NON-TRADING INCOME − LOSSES · ADD BACK DEPRECIATION · CAPITAL ALLOWANCES · ⚠️ VERIFY RATES/THRESHOLDS

Corporation Tax.front:
Gable Ltd makes a trading profit of £40,000 in its accounting year ending 31 December 2024. It has no other income. When must corporation tax be paid, and at what rate?

⚠️ Verify rates against current HMRC guidance.
Corporation Tax.answer:
⚠️ These figures reflect the position from April 2023 — verify against current HMRC guidance before advising a client.

Rate: Gable's profits of £40,000 fall below the small profits threshold of £50,000. The small profits rate of corporation tax (19%) therefore applies. Corporation tax liability = £40,000 × 19% = £7,600.

Payment deadline: For companies that do not pay by instalments (generally those with profits below £1.5m — the instalment threshold, divided by associated companies), corporation tax is due nine months and one day after the end of the accounting period. The accounting year ends 31 December 2024, so corporation tax is due by 1 October 2025.

CT return: A company tax return (CT600) must also be filed with HMRC within 12 months of the end of the accounting period — by 31 December 2025 here.

Note: If Gable has associated companies, the £50,000 threshold is divided by the number of associated companies, which may push it into the marginal relief band.
Corporation Tax.summary_line: SMALL PROFITS RATE 19% · ≤£50K · 9 MONTHS + 1 DAY PAYMENT · CT600 WITHIN 12 MONTHS · ⚠️ VERIFY RATES AND THRESHOLDS

Income Tax for Unincorporated Businesses.front: How are the profits of an ordinary partnership taxed?
Income Tax for Unincorporated Businesses.answer: A partnership is not itself a taxable entity. Each partner is taxed individually on their share of the partnership's profits (income tax for individual partners; corporation tax if a corporate partner). Each partner's share of profits is determined by the profit-sharing agreement and assessed in the tax year in which the partnership's accounting period ends. Partners also pay Class 2 NIC and Class 4 NIC on their share of trading profits. The partnership must file a partnership tax return (to notify HMRC of the profit split) but does not pay tax directly.
Income Tax for Unincorporated Businesses.summary_line: LOOK-THROUGH · EACH PARTNER TAXED ON SHARE · INCOME TAX · CLASS 2 + 4 NIC · PARTNERSHIP RETURN FILED

Income Tax for Unincorporated Businesses.front: Compare the tax treatment of a sole trader with that of a shareholder-director of a private limited company. What are the key advantages and disadvantages of each structure from a tax perspective? ⚠️ Verify all rates.
Income Tax for Unincorporated Businesses.answer:
⚠️ Tax rates and thresholds change frequently — always verify before advising.

SOLE TRADER
+ Simple: all profits taxed as income; self-assessment return only
+ Trading losses can be offset against other income in the same or previous year
− Income tax at 20% / 40% / 45% on profits above thresholds
− Class 2 and Class 4 NIC on profits (Class 2 effectively abolished ⚠️ verify)
− No ability to retain profits in a lower-rate environment
− Effective tax rate at higher profits can exceed 45%+ when NIC included

PRIVATE LIMITED COMPANY (Shareholder-Director)
+ Corporation tax on profits (19% / 25% depending on profit level) ⚠️
+ Efficient extraction: salary up to NIC threshold (no NIC on salary below threshold); remainder as dividends (taxed at lower rates, no NIC)
+ Can retain profits in company at CT rates (useful if director does not need all cash immediately — defers personal tax)
+ BADR on exit via share sale (if conditions met) ⚠️
− Admin: annual accounts, CT return, payroll, Companies House filings
− Salary and dividends subject to their own tax rates ⚠️
− IR35 risk for personal service companies
− Extracting retained profits on wind-up may trigger CGT (or income tax if anti-avoidance applies — s.396B ITTOIA 2005 'Phoenix' provisions) ⚠️

SQE EXAM FOCUS: Know the structure of the comparison; identify the salary/dividend extraction strategy; flag BADR and IR35 as risks; recommend specialist tax advice for any specific figures.
Income Tax for Unincorporated Businesses.summary_line: SOLE TRADER = INCOME TAX + NIC · COMPANY = CT + SALARY/DIVIDEND · BADR ON EXIT · IR35 · RETENTION OF PROFITS · ⚠️ VERIFY ALL RATES · PHOENIX RISK

Income Tax for Unincorporated Businesses.front:
Ingrid runs a profitable consultancy. She is deciding whether to operate as a sole trader or through a personal service company (PSC). Her projected annual profit is £120,000. Assume she will extract all profit from the company if incorporating.

Outline the key tax differences between the two structures. ⚠️ Advise on seeking specialist tax advice.
Income Tax for Unincorporated Businesses.answer:
⚠️ Tax rates and thresholds change frequently — this is an outline only. Ingrid must take specialist tax advice before making any decision.

Sole trader: Ingrid pays income tax on all profits (£120,000). At this level, she will pay 20% on income up to the basic rate band limit, 40% on income above that (up to £125,140 adjusted), and potentially 45% above. She also pays Class 4 NIC on profits. Total effective tax rate will be high — approaching 50%+ on income in the higher-rate band when NIC is included.

Personal service company: The company pays corporation tax on profits (25% main rate above £250,000; lower rates on smaller profits — ⚠️ verify). Ingrid then extracts profit via: (a) a salary up to the NIC threshold (saving NIC while remaining eligible for state benefits); and (b) dividends on remaining profits. Dividends are taxed at lower rates than employment income (basic/higher/additional rate dividend rates — ⚠️ verify) and are not subject to NIC. This can produce a meaningful tax saving.

IR35 risk: If Ingrid's work resembles disguised employment, HMRC's IR35 rules may require the PSC to operate PAYE as if Ingrid were directly employed, eliminating most tax advantages. This is a significant risk for personal service consultancies. ⚠️ IR35 status must be assessed carefully.
Income Tax for Unincorporated Businesses.summary_line: SOLE TRADER = INCOME TAX + NIC · PSC = CT + SALARY/DIVIDEND EXTRACTION · LOWER NIC · IR35 RISK · ⚠️ SPECIALIST ADVICE ESSENTIAL

National Insurance Contributions.front: What are the classes of National Insurance Contributions (NIC) and who pays each?
National Insurance Contributions.answer: ⚠️ Verify current rates against HMRC. The main classes are: Class 1 NIC — paid by employees (primary, deducted from salary) and employers (secondary, on top of wages) on employment income. Class 2 NIC — paid by self-employed individuals (flat weekly rate, if profits exceed small profits threshold; from April 2024, Class 2 is effectively abolished for most purposes — ⚠️ verify). Class 4 NIC — paid by self-employed individuals on trading profits between the lower and upper profits limits (percentage rates apply). Directors of companies take salary (Class 1) and dividends (not subject to NIC), which is often a tax-efficient structure.
National Insurance Contributions.summary_line: CLASS 1 = EMPLOYED · CLASS 2 = SELF-EMPLOYED FLAT RATE · CLASS 4 = SELF-EMPLOYED ON PROFITS · ⚠️ VERIFY RATES

SDLT.front: When does Stamp Duty Land Tax (SDLT) arise in a business context?
SDLT.answer: SDLT is payable by a buyer on acquisitions of interests in land and buildings in England and Northern Ireland (FA 2003). It is triggered by a 'land transaction' — any acquisition of a chargeable interest in land. In a business context, SDLT arises on: (1) purchase of commercial property (different rates from residential); (2) grant of a new commercial lease (SDLT on both premium and net present value of rent); (3) assignment of a lease. ⚠️ Verify current SDLT rates for commercial property against HMRC guidance. SDLT must be notified and paid within 14 days of completion. SDLT does not apply to transfers of shares (stamp duty at 0.5% applies instead).
SDLT.summary_line: FA 2003 · LAND TRANSACTIONS IN ENGLAND · COMMERCIAL PROPERTY · LEASES · 14 DAYS TO FILE · ⚠️ VERIFY RATES

SDLT.front:
Kite Ltd is purchasing a commercial property for £800,000 and taking a lease on a second commercial property at £40,000 per annum rent (no premium), both in England. Advise on Stamp Duty Land Tax.

⚠️ Verify current SDLT rates for commercial property.
SDLT.answer:
⚠️ SDLT rates for commercial property should be verified against HMRC — rates and thresholds change in Finance Acts.

Purchase at £800,000 (non-residential): SDLT applies on a slice basis to non-residential property in England. As a general outline (⚠️ verify):
- 0% on the first £150,000
- 2% on £150,001–£250,000 = £2,000
- 5% on £250,001–£800,000 = £27,500
Total SDLT on purchase ≈ £29,500 (⚠️ verify with current rates).

Kite Ltd must file an SDLT return and pay the tax within 14 days of completion.

New lease at £40,000 per annum: SDLT on leases is charged on both any premium paid (none here) and on the net present value (NPV) of the rent. The NPV is calculated using a prescribed discount rate over the term of the lease. If the NPV exceeds £150,000, SDLT is charged at 1% on the excess. ⚠️ The calculation is complex — specialist advice should be taken.

SDLT does not apply to share acquisitions (stamp duty at 0.5% applies instead). SDLT is the liability of the buyer/tenant.
SDLT.summary_line: SDLT NON-RESIDENTIAL · SLICE BASIS · 14 DAYS TO FILE · LEASE = NPV OF RENT · ⚠️ VERIFY CURRENT RATES · BUYER PAYS

VAT.front: When must a business register for VAT and what are the consequences of late registration?
VAT.answer: A business must register for VAT when its taxable turnover in the previous 12 months exceeds the registration threshold, or when there are reasonable grounds to believe taxable turnover in the next 30 days alone will exceed the threshold (VATA 1994). ⚠️ Verify the current VAT registration threshold against HMRC guidance — it changes in Finance Acts. On registration, the business must charge VAT on taxable supplies. Late registration can result in a penalty based on the VAT that should have been paid from the date registration was required. Voluntary registration is possible if turnover is below the threshold.
VAT.summary_line: VATA 1994 · COMPULSORY REGISTRATION WHEN THRESHOLD EXCEEDED · ⚠️ VERIFY THRESHOLD · LATE REGISTRATION PENALTY

VAT.front: What are the three rates of VAT and give an example of a supply within each?
VAT.answer: The three rates are: (1) Standard rate (20%) — applies to most taxable supplies of goods and services (e.g. professional services, most goods); (2) Reduced rate (5%) — applies to specified supplies (e.g. domestic fuel and power, installation of energy-saving materials, children's car seats); (3) Zero rate (0%) — applies to specified supplies that are taxable but VAT-free (e.g. most food, children's clothing, books, newspapers, exported goods, new residential construction). A fourth category — exempt supplies (e.g. financial services, insurance, education, health) — is not subject to VAT, but the supplier cannot reclaim input VAT attributable to exempt supplies.
VAT.summary_line: STANDARD 20% · REDUCED 5% · ZERO 0% · EXEMPT (NO INPUT VAT RECOVERY) · ⚠️ CHECK CATEGORIES

VAT.front: What is input VAT and output VAT, and how does a VAT-registered business account for VAT?
VAT.answer: Output VAT is the VAT a business charges on its taxable supplies (sales). Input VAT is the VAT a business pays on goods and services it purchases for use in its business. A VAT-registered business accounts for VAT by filing quarterly VAT returns, paying HMRC the difference where output VAT exceeds input VAT, or claiming a repayment where input VAT exceeds output VAT. Input VAT can only be reclaimed if the purchases relate to taxable (not exempt) supplies. Partial exemption rules apply where a business makes both taxable and exempt supplies.
VAT.summary_line: OUTPUT VAT = CHARGED ON SALES · INPUT VAT = PAID ON PURCHASES · QUARTERLY RETURN · PARTIAL EXEMPTION

VAT.front:
Hugo runs a catering business as a sole trader. His taxable turnover for the past 12 months has just exceeded the VAT registration threshold for the first time. He has been charging clients without adding VAT.

What must Hugo do now, and what are the consequences of late registration? ⚠️ Verify the current threshold.
VAT.answer:
⚠️ Verify the current VAT registration threshold against HMRC — it changes in Finance Acts and this answer should not be relied upon without checking.

Obligation: Hugo must notify HMRC of his liability to register for VAT within 30 days of the end of the month in which his taxable turnover first exceeded the threshold (VATA 1994). He must register and begin charging VAT on all taxable supplies from the effective date of registration.

Late registration penalty: If Hugo fails to register on time, HMRC will register him with effect from when he should have been registered. HMRC will assess him for the VAT that should have been charged from that date — even though he did not collect it from clients. He will owe this VAT personally. A penalty based on the net tax due may also be charged. The penalty percentage increases with the length of delay.

Practical issues: Hugo must issue VAT invoices to his VAT-registered clients going back to the effective registration date if possible (or absorb the VAT cost himself). If his clients are businesses, they can claim input VAT — so re-billing may be possible. Consumer clients are less likely to pay retrospective VAT without dispute.

Hugo should register immediately and take advice on recovering VAT from clients.
VAT.summary_line: VATA 1994 · REGISTER WITHIN 30 DAYS · LATE REGISTRATION PENALTY · VAT FROM EFFECTIVE DATE · ⚠️ VERIFY THRESHOLD

Administration.front: What is the purpose of administration and what statutory objectives must the administrator pursue?
Administration.answer: Administration is a rescue and restructuring procedure under IA 1986 Sch.B1. An administrator is an officer of the court and must act in the interests of all creditors. The administrator must pursue the statutory objectives in order of priority: (1) rescuing the company as a going concern; (2) if that is not reasonably practicable, achieving a better result for creditors as a whole than winding up; (3) if that is also not reasonably practicable, realising property to pay secured or preferential creditors. An administration gives the company a moratorium — all legal proceedings and enforcement action are stayed without the administrator's or court's consent.
Administration.summary_line: IA 1986 SCH.B1 · THREE OBJECTIVES IN ORDER · MORATORIUM · OFFICER OF COURT · ALL CREDITORS

Administration.answer: Administration can be commenced in three ways: (1) Court order — on application by the company, its directors, or a creditor; (2) Out of court appointment by the holder of a qualifying floating charge (QFC holder) — by filing documents at court; (3) Out of court appointment by the company or its directors. The out-of-court routes (2 and (3)) are quicker and cheaper. A QFC holder must give 5 business days' written notice to any prior-ranking QFC holder before appointing. The moratorium takes effect from the moment of appointment.
Administration.summary_line: COURT ORDER · QFC HOLDER OUT OF COURT · COMPANY/DIRECTORS OUT OF COURT · MORATORIUM FROM APPOINTMENT

Administration.front:
Nova Engineering Ltd is struggling with cash flow but has a profitable order book. Its main lender holds a qualifying floating charge. A key creditor is threatening to issue a winding-up petition next week.

Advise the directors on whether administration might be appropriate and how it could be commenced quickly.
Administration.answer:
Administration may be appropriate and can be commenced quickly by the directors using the out-of-court route.

Purpose: Administration under IA 1986 Sch.B1 is designed for potentially viable businesses. The primary objective is rescuing the company as a going concern. Given Nova's profitable order book, there is a plausible rescue case. If rescue is not achievable, the secondary objective — a better result for creditors than liquidation — may be met by selling the business as a going concern.

Moratorium: From the moment of appointment, a moratorium takes effect — all legal proceedings are stayed and no enforcement action (including winding-up petitions) can proceed without the administrator's or court's consent. This immediately stops the threatened winding-up petition.

How to appoint quickly: The directors can file a Notice of Intention to Appoint (NOITA) at court, which gives them a 10-business-day moratorium while an administrator is lined up. However, the qualifying floating chargeholder (the main lender) must be given at least 5 business days' written notice before the directors make the out-of-court appointment, allowing the lender to appoint its own administrator instead. The lender may prefer to appoint — its administrator takes precedence.

Practical step: The directors should immediately contact the lender and an insolvency practitioner. Speed is essential.
Administration.summary_line: IA 1986 SCH.B1 · ADMINISTRATION · MORATORIUM · OUT-OF-COURT APPOINTMENT · QFC HOLDER 5 DAYS NOTICE · RESCUE OBJECTIVE

Antecedent Transactions.front: Contrast the three main antecedent transaction provisions available to a liquidator: s.238 (transaction at undervalue), s.239 (preference) and s.245 (avoidance of floating charges). What must be shown for each?
Antecedent Transactions.answer:
S.238 — TRANSACTION AT UNDERVALUE
What: Company gave away assets or received significantly less than market value.
Relevant time: 2 years (all parties).
Insolvency condition: Yes — insolvent at time or caused by transaction (connected persons presumed).
Mental element: None — focus is on the objective value of what was received.
Defence: Good faith + reasonable belief transaction benefited the company.

S.239 — PREFERENCE
What: Creditor or surety put in a better position than they would have been on insolvent liquidation.
Relevant time: 6 months (non-connected) / 2 years (connected).
Insolvency condition: Yes.
Mental element: Company must have been influenced by a desire to prefer. Presumed for connected persons; must be proved for non-connected.
Defence: No equivalent to TUV good faith defence — but genuine commercial pressure (e.g. creditor threatening to withdraw supply) can negate the desire.

S.245 — AVOIDANCE OF FLOATING CHARGES
What: A floating charge created within the relevant time is invalid to the extent it secures past debt (existing indebtedness), unless 'new money' (cash or assets) was provided at the time of or after creation.
Relevant time: 12 months (non-connected) / 2 years (connected).
Insolvency condition: Yes for non-connected; no condition for connected.
Mental element: None — purely objective.
Effect: Charge is invalid to the extent of past debt; valid for new money.
Antecedent Transactions.summary_line: S.238 TUV · S.239 PREFERENCE · S.245 FLOATING CHARGE · RELEVANT TIMES: 2YR / 6MTH+2YR / 12MTH+2YR · DESIRE TO PREFER · GOOD FAITH · NEW MONEY

Director Liability in Insolvency.front: What is wrongful trading under the Insolvency Act 1986 s.214 and what are the consequences?
Director Liability in Insolvency.answer: Wrongful trading under IA 1986 s.214 applies where: (1) the company has gone into insolvent liquidation; and (2) at some point before the winding up, a director knew or ought to have concluded that there was no reasonable prospect of the company avoiding insolvent liquidation; and (3) the director failed to take every step to minimise potential loss to creditors. The court can order the director to contribute to the company's assets (compensation, not a fine). The standard is objective: what a reasonably diligent person with the director's functions and actual knowledge would have concluded.
Director Liability in Insolvency.summary_line: IA 1986 S.214 · INSOLVENT LIQUIDATION · NO REASONABLE PROSPECT · CONTRIBUTION ORDER · OBJECTIVE STANDARD

Director Liability in Insolvency.front: What is the difference between wrongful trading (s.214) and fraudulent trading (s.213) under the Insolvency Act 1986?
Director Liability in Insolvency.answer: Wrongful trading (s.214): civil liability only; no dishonesty required — the test is objective (what a reasonably diligent director would have known/done); applies only on insolvent liquidation; director ordered to contribute to assets. Fraudulent trading (s.213): requires actual dishonesty — carrying on business with intent to defraud creditors or for any fraudulent purpose; can involve any party (not just directors); criminal offence under CA 2006 s.993 (separate provision); civil remedy under s.213 in liquidation — contribution order. Fraudulent trading is harder to establish due to the dishonesty requirement.
Director Liability in Insolvency.summary_line: WRONGFUL S.214 = NO DISHONESTY · OBJECTIVE · CIVIL ONLY · FRAUDULENT S.213 = DISHONESTY · CRIMINAL + CIVIL · HARDER TO PROVE

Director Liability in Insolvency.front: What is a preference under the Insolvency Act 1986 s.239 and when can it be set aside?
Director Liability in Insolvency.answer: A preference occurs when a company does something or allows something to happen that puts a creditor or surety in a better position in the event of the company's insolvent liquidation than they would otherwise have been in. The liquidator can apply to set aside a preference that occurred within 6 months of the onset of insolvency (2 years if the preferred person is a connected person). The company must have been influenced by a desire to prefer — this is presumed if the preferred person is connected. If set aside, the transaction is reversed and the asset/money returns to the estate.
Director Liability in Insolvency.summary_line: IA 1986 S.239 · BETTER POSITION IN INSOLVENCY · 6 MONTHS (2 YEARS CONNECTED) · DESIRE TO PREFER · REVERSAL

Director Liability in Insolvency.front: What is a transaction at an undervalue under the Insolvency Act 1986 s.238?
Director Liability in Insolvency.answer: A transaction at an undervalue occurs where a company enters into a transaction with any person for no consideration, or for consideration significantly less than the value given by the company. The liquidator can set aside a transaction at undervalue made within 2 years before the onset of insolvency, provided the company was insolvent at the time or became insolvent as a result. The court will not set aside a transaction if entered into in good faith for the purpose of carrying on business and at a time when there were reasonable grounds to believe it would benefit the company.
Director Liability in Insolvency.summary_line: IA 1986 S.238 · NO/UNDERVALUE CONSIDERATION · 2 YEARS · INSOLVENCY AT TIME · GOOD FAITH DEFENCE

Liquidation.front: What are the main grounds for a compulsory winding-up order under the Insolvency Act 1986?
Liquidation.answer: A court may order compulsory winding up under IA 1986 s.122 on various grounds. The most commonly used ground in practice is that the company is unable to pay its debts (s.122(1)(f)). The petitioner (often a creditor) must demonstrate insolvency by either: (1) a statutory demand for a debt exceeding £750 that remains unpaid for 21 days (s.123(1)(a)); or (2) proving that the company's assets are insufficient to meet its liabilities (balance sheet test, s.123(2)). Other grounds include: just and equitable (s.122(1)(g)); failure to hold a statutory meeting.
Liquidation.summary_line: IA 1986 S.122 · UNABLE TO PAY DEBTS S.122(1)(F) · STATUTORY DEMAND £750 + 21 DAYS · BALANCE SHEET TEST

Liquidation.front: What is the difference between a Members' Voluntary Liquidation (MVL) and a Creditors' Voluntary Liquidation (CVL)?
Liquidation.answer: An MVL is a solvent liquidation — directors make a statutory declaration of solvency confirming the company can pay all debts within 12 months. Shareholders resolve to wind up (special resolution) and appoint a liquidator. Surplus assets are distributed to shareholders. A CVL is an insolvent liquidation — no declaration of solvency is possible. Shareholders pass a special resolution to wind up; creditors have the right to nominate their own liquidator (which takes precedence). In a CVL, the liquidator collects assets, pays creditors in the statutory order, and any surplus goes to shareholders (in practice, there is usually no surplus).
Liquidation.summary_line: MVL = SOLVENT · DECLARATION OF SOLVENCY · CVL = INSOLVENT · CREDITORS NOMINATE LIQUIDATOR · SPECIAL RESOLUTION BOTH

Liquidation.front: In what order are debts paid on the insolvency of a company?
Liquidation.answer: The statutory order of priority in a corporate insolvency (broadly) is: (1) costs and expenses of the liquidation; (2) preferential creditors (e.g. certain employee claims — unpaid wages up to £800, holiday pay; HMRC secondary preferential creditor status for certain tax debts — ⚠️ verify); (3) prescribed part — a ring-fenced fund from floating charge realisations for unsecured creditors (IA 1986 s.176A); (4) holder of a qualifying floating charge; (5) unsecured creditors (pari passu — equally); (6) deferred debts; (7) shareholders. Fixed charge holders are paid from the proceeds of their security and rank outside this waterfall.
Liquidation.summary_line: LIQUIDATION EXPENSES · PREFERENTIAL · PRESCRIBED PART · FLOATING CHARGE · UNSECURED · SHAREHOLDERS · FIXED OUTSIDE WATERFALL

MVL vs CVL.front:
The shareholders of Quest Ltd have decided to close the business. The company has settled all its debts and has £80,000 in remaining assets to distribute to shareholders. No creditors will be prejudiced by the closure.

What is the appropriate winding-up procedure?
MVL vs CVL.answer:
A Members' Voluntary Liquidation (MVL) is the appropriate procedure.

An MVL is available where a company is solvent — that is, the directors can make a statutory declaration of solvency confirming they have conducted a full inquiry into the company's affairs and have formed the opinion that the company will be able to pay all its debts in full within 12 months of the commencement of winding up (IA 1986 s.89). On the facts, all debts are already settled, so the declaration can be made.

Procedure: (1) Directors make the statutory declaration of solvency (within 5 weeks before the shareholders' resolution); (2) shareholders pass a special resolution to wind up (75% majority); (3) shareholders appoint a liquidator; (4) the liquidator collects remaining assets, pays any outstanding debts, and distributes the surplus to shareholders.

The £80,000 will be distributed to shareholders in proportion to their shareholdings (after liquidator's costs).

Tax note: Distributions in an MVL may be treated as capital distributions rather than income — potentially qualifying for Business Asset Disposal Relief (BADR) at a lower CGT rate. ⚠️ The tax treatment of MVL distributions has been subject to HMRC challenge — specialist advice should be taken.
MVL vs CVL.summary_line: MVL · SOLVENT LIQUIDATION · DECLARATION OF SOLVENCY S.89 · SPECIAL RESOLUTION · SURPLUS TO SHAREHOLDERS · ⚠️ CGT / BADR

Personal Insolvency.front: What is bankruptcy and what are its main consequences for the bankrupt individual?
Personal Insolvency.answer: Bankruptcy is a personal insolvency procedure under IA 1986. A bankruptcy order can be made by the court on a debtor's petition or a creditor's petition (for a debt of at least £5,000). On making the order, the bankrupt's estate vests automatically in the trustee in bankruptcy, who distributes assets to creditors. Key consequences: the bankrupt cannot act as a director or be involved in company management without court permission; cannot obtain credit above a prescribed amount without disclosing bankruptcy; professional licences may be suspended. A discharge usually occurs automatically after 12 months (or less in some cases). ⚠️ Verify the current creditor petition threshold.
Personal Insolvency.summary_line: IA 1986 · BANKRUPTCY ORDER · ESTATE TO TRUSTEE · DIRECTOR DISQUALIFICATION · DISCHARGE 12 MONTHS · ⚠️ VERIFY THRESHOLD

Preferences.front: What is the 'relevant time' for challenging a preference under IA 1986 s.239, and how does it differ depending on whether the recipient is a connected person?
Preferences.answer:
Under IA 1986 s.239, a preference can only be challenged by a liquidator or administrator if it was given at a 'relevant time' — defined in s.240.

The relevant time periods are:
• 6 months before the onset of insolvency: applies to preferences given to non-connected persons (i.e. arm's length creditors)
• 2 years before the onset of insolvency: applies to preferences given to connected persons (directors, shadow directors, associates — defined in s.249 IA 1986 / s.252 CA 2006)

In both cases, the company must also have been insolvent at the time of the preference, or have become insolvent as a result of it (s.240(2)).

Desire to prefer: For a preference to be voidable, the company must have been influenced by a desire to improve the creditor's position on insolvency (s.239(5)). For connected persons, this desire is presumed (s.239(6)) — the burden shifts to the connected person to rebut it. For non-connected persons, the liquidator must prove the desire to prefer positively.

Onset of insolvency: typically the date of the winding-up petition or the commencement of administration (s.240(3)).
Preferences.summary_line: S.239 PREFERENCE · 6 MONTHS (NON-CONNECTED) · 2 YEARS (CONNECTED) · INSOLVENCY CONDITION · DESIRE TO PREFER · PRESUMED FOR CONNECTED PERSONS

Preferences.front:
Lotus Ltd is insolvent. Six weeks before entering liquidation, it repays a £20,000 loan from its sole director and majority shareholder, Mark, in full. Other unsecured creditors remain unpaid. Mark argues the repayment was simply a legitimate debt repayment.

Advise the liquidator.
Preferences.answer:
The liquidator should challenge this payment as a preference under IA 1986 s.239.

A preference occurs where the company, at a time when it was insolvent, did something that put a creditor in a better position on insolvent liquidation than they would otherwise have been. Repaying Mark's loan in full when other creditors will receive nothing plainly satisfies this.

Time limit: A preference given to a connected person (CA 2006 s.252 — Mark is a director and majority shareholder, clearly connected) can be challenged if it occurred within two years before the onset of insolvency. Six weeks falls well within this window.

Desire to prefer: Where a connected person receives a preference, there is a statutory presumption that the company was influenced by a desire to prefer them (IA 1986 s.239(6)). Mark's argument that this was a 'legitimate debt repayment' does not rebut that presumption — the desire to prefer is presumed unless evidence shows the repayment was, in reality, made for other genuine commercial reasons without any desire to improve Mark's position. This is very hard to show on these facts.

Remedy: If the court sets aside the preference, Mark must repay the £20,000 to the company's estate for distribution to all creditors. The court can make any order it thinks fit to restore the position.
Preferences.summary_line: IA 1986 S.239 · PREFERENCE · CONNECTED PERSON · 2-YEAR WINDOW · DESIRE TO PREFER PRESUMED · REPAYMENT TO ESTATE

Priority of Creditors.front:
Orbit Ltd enters insolvent liquidation. At the date of liquidation the following claims exist:
- Liquidator's fees and expenses: £15,000
- Employees' unpaid wages (last 4 months, £200 per employee for 5 employees): £4,000
- Bank (fixed charge over freehold, realised value £100,000, debt £120,000)
- Bank (floating charge over stock, realised value £60,000, debt £80,000)
- HMRC (unsecured): £25,000
- Trade creditors (unsecured): £50,000

In what order are these paid? (Assume the prescribed part is £10,000.)
Priority of Creditors.answer:
Payments are made in the following order:

1. Fixed chargeholder (Bank, from fixed charge realisations): The bank is paid first from the £100,000 proceeds of the freehold — outside the general insolvency waterfall. Bank recovers £100,000, leaving £20,000 of its debt unsatisfied (ranks as unsecured for the balance).

2. From the remaining estate (floating charge realisations + any other assets):

   a. Liquidation expenses (£15,000): Paid first from the general estate.

   b. Preferential creditors — employees' unpaid wages: Employees are preferential    creditors for unpaid wages up to £800 per employee for the period before    liquidation. Here 5 employees × £800 = £4,000 maximum. ⚠️ Verify the current    preferential limit.

   c. Prescribed part (IA 1986 s.176A): A ring-fenced fund carved out of floating    charge realisations for unsecured creditors. Here assumed to be £10,000.

   d. Floating chargeholder (Bank): After expenses, preferential creditors, and    prescribed part are deducted from the £60,000 floating charge realisations:    £60,000 − £15,000 − £4,000 − £10,000 = £31,000 to the bank. Balance of    floating charge debt unsatisfied: £80,000 − £31,000 = £49,000 (ranks unsecured).

   e. Unsecured creditors (pari passu from prescribed part + any surplus):    £10,000 prescribed part shared among: HMRC £25,000, trade creditors £50,000,    bank balance £20,000 (fixed) + £49,000 (floating) = total unsecured £144,000.    Each receives approximately 6.9p in the pound from the prescribed part.

   f. Shareholders: Nothing — company is insolvent.
Priority of Creditors.summary_line: FIXED CHARGE FIRST · LIQUIDATION EXPENSES · PREFERENTIAL CREDITORS · PRESCRIBED PART S.176A · FLOATING CHARGE · UNSECURED PARI PASSU · SHAREHOLDERS LAST

The Prescribed Part and Creditor Priorit.subtopic: The Prescribed Part and Creditor Priority
The Prescribed Part and Creditor Priorit.front: Going deeper: Does the prescribed part regime under IA 1986 s.176A strike the right balance between secured and unsecured creditors, or does it undermine the efficiency of secured lending without meaningfully benefiting unsecured creditors?
The Prescribed Part and Creditor Priorit.answer:
THE PRESCRIBED PART
IA 1986 s.176A (inserted by the Enterprise Act 2002) requires that a proportion of net floating charge realisations be set aside for unsecured creditors — the 'prescribed part'. The prescribed part is calculated as: 50% of the first £10,000 of net floating charge property, plus 20% of the remainder, up to a maximum of £800,000 (⚠️ verify current cap). The maximum was increased from £600,000 in 2020.

THE POLICY RATIONALE
The Enterprise Act 2002 abolished Crown preference (HMRC's preferential creditor status for most tax debts) and introduced the prescribed part as a partial compensation for unsecured creditors. The theory: floating charges had historically captured assets that might otherwise have been available to unsecured creditors who extended trade credit on the assumption that company assets were unencumbered.

THE CRITIQUE
Goode, Mokal, and others argue the prescribed part is an economically inefficient compromise. Secured lenders responded to its introduction by: (a) seeking more fixed charge security to reduce floating charge pools; (b) increasing lending costs (the prescribed part acts as a tax on floating charge lending). Empirical research (Armour, Frisby & Hicks, 'Debenture Holders and Administrative Receivers' [2000]) suggested that the practical benefit to unsecured creditors is modest — the sums recovered from the prescribed part are often small relative to total unsecured debt.

THE HMRC 'SECONDARY PREFERENCE' COMPLICATION
HMRC regained a secondary preferential creditor status from December 2020 under the Finance Act 2020 (⚠️ verify current scope). This partially reverses the Enterprise Act 2002 reform and reduces the floating charge pool available for both floating chargeholders and the prescribed part. Critics argue this creates conflicting signals — policymakers cannot decide whether secured or unsecured creditors (including HMRC) deserve priority.

OPEN QUESTION
Should the prescribed part be abolished, retained, or significantly increased? The Insolvency Service has conducted periodic reviews. The tension between encouraging access to secured credit (which drives business lending) and protecting unsecured trade creditors (which is also commercially important) has not been resolved.
The Prescribed Part and Creditor Priorit.summary_line: S.176A · PRESCRIBED PART · ENTERPRISE ACT 2002 · CROWN PREFERENCE ABOLISHED · HMRC SECONDARY PREFERENCE · GOODE · MOKAL · ARMOUR · REFORM DEBATE

Transaction at Undervalue.front: What is a transaction at an undervalue under IA 1986 s.238, and how does it differ from a preference under s.239?
Transaction at Undervalue.answer:
A transaction at an undervalue (TUV) under IA 1986 s.238 occurs where a company in insolvent liquidation or administration entered into a transaction:
(a) as a gift (no consideration); or
(b) for a consideration significantly less in money or money's worth than the value of what the company provided.

Relevant time: 2 years before the onset of insolvency (s.240). For connected persons, insolvency at the time is presumed. For non-connected, the company must have been insolvent at the time or become insolvent as a result.

Good faith defence (s.238(5)): The court will not set aside the transaction if it was entered into in good faith for the purpose of carrying on the company's business, and there were reasonable grounds to believe it would benefit the company. This defence is not available where the consideration is grossly inadequate (e.g. transfer for £1).

Key contrasts with preferences (s.239):
• TUV: no need to prove desire to prefer — focus is on what value was received
• Preference: must show desire to improve creditor's position on insolvency
• TUV relevant time: 2 years (both connected and non-connected)
• Preference relevant time: 6 months (non-connected) / 2 years (connected)
• TUV: good faith/benefit defence available; Preference: no equivalent defence

Both provisions allow the court to make such order as it thinks fit to restore the position as if the transaction had not been entered into.
Transaction at Undervalue.summary_line: S.238 TUV · 2 YEARS · GIFT OR UNDERVALUE · GOOD FAITH DEFENCE · CONTRAST S.239 PREFERENCE · 6 MONTHS / 2 YEARS · DESIRE TO PREFER NOT NEEDED

Transaction at Undervalue.front:
Mango Ltd transfers its delivery fleet (worth £150,000) to its director's wife for £1 eighteen months before entering insolvent liquidation. At the time of the transfer, Mango was already insolvent.

Advise the liquidator on whether this can be challenged.
Transaction at Undervalue.answer:
The liquidator has a strong claim to set aside this transfer under IA 1986 s.238 (transaction at an undervalue).

A transaction at an undervalue occurs where a company enters into a transaction for consideration significantly less than the value it provides. Transferring assets worth £150,000 for £1 is an extreme undervalue (essentially a gift).

Time limit: The transaction must have occurred within two years before the onset of insolvency. Eighteen months falls within this window.

Insolvency condition: The company must have been insolvent at the time of the transaction, or have become insolvent as a result of it. On the facts, Mango was already insolvent when the transfer was made — this condition is satisfied.

Good faith defence: The court will not set aside a transaction at an undervalue if it was entered into in good faith for the purposes of carrying on the company's business, and there were reasonable grounds to believe it would benefit the company. Transferring a major asset for £1 to the director's wife plainly does not meet this test. The recipient is also a connected person (director's wife — s.249), raising a presumption of insolvency at the time.

Remedy: The court will make an order restoring the position — requiring the wife to return the fleet (or pay its value) to the estate.
Transaction at Undervalue.summary_line: IA 1986 S.238 · TRANSACTION AT UNDERVALUE · 2 YEARS · INSOLVENT AT TIME · NO GOOD FAITH DEFENCE · RESTORE POSITION

Wrongful Trading.front: State the test for wrongful trading under IA 1986 s.214 and the standard against which a director's conduct is measured.
Wrongful Trading.answer:
Under IA 1986 s.214(2), a director is liable for wrongful trading if:

1. The company has gone into insolvent liquidation; and
2. At some point before the commencement of winding up, the director knew or ought to have concluded that there was no reasonable prospect of the company avoiding insolvent liquidation; and
3. The director failed to take every step with a view to minimising the potential loss to the company's creditors.

Standard of knowledge (s.214(4) — dual objective/subjective test):
The facts which a director 'ought to know or ascertain', the conclusions they 'ought to reach', and the steps they 'ought to take' are assessed against the standard of a reasonably diligent person with:
(a) the general knowledge, skill and experience reasonably expected of a person carrying out the same functions as the director (objective floor); AND
(b) the actual knowledge, skill and experience of that particular director (subjective ceiling — whichever is higher applies).

Key case: Re Produce Marketing Consortium [1989] — directors who buried their heads in the sand and failed to review management accounts were held to the objective standard.

The court may order the director to contribute to the company's assets. Section 214 also applies to shadow directors (s.214(7)).

Defence: Taking every step to minimise creditor loss from the critical date (e.g. seeking insolvency advice, stopping new credit) provides a full defence.
Wrongful Trading.summary_line: S.214 · KNEW OR OUGHT TO CONCLUDE · DUAL TEST · OBJECTIVE FLOOR · SUBJECTIVE CEILING · EVERY STEP DEFENCE · RE PRODUCE MARKETING · SHADOW DIRECTORS

Wrongful Trading.issue: How the wrongful trading provision under IA 1986 s.214 should be applied, including the standard of knowledge required and the basis for calculating a director's contribution.
Wrongful Trading.rule: Under IA 1986 s.214, a director is liable to contribute to the company's assets if: (1) the company has gone into insolvent liquidation; (2) at some time before commencement of winding up, the director knew or ought to have concluded that there was no reasonable prospect of avoiding insolvent liquidation; and (3) the director did not take every step to minimise potential loss to creditors. The objective standard (what the director ought to have known) can be more demanding than the director's actual knowledge.
Wrongful Trading.application: Two directors of a fruit-importing company continued to trade after it was clear (or should have been clear) that insolvent liquidation was inevitable. Knox J held both directors liable under s.214. The contribution was assessed by reference to the increase in the net deficiency caused by the continued trading after the date when they ought to have recognised the position.
Wrongful Trading.conclusion: The first significant application of the wrongful trading provisions. Establishes that the objective element of s.214 can expose directors who bury their heads in the sand. The quantum of contribution is assessed by reference to the additional loss to creditors caused by the continued trading.
Wrongful Trading.summary_line: WRONGFUL TRADING S.214 · OBJECTIVE STANDARD · NET DEFICIENCY INCREASE · HEADS IN SAND · INSOLVENT LIQUIDATION INEVITABLE · KNOX J

Wrongful Trading.front:
Pearl Ltd has been loss-making for two years. In January, the directors receive management accounts showing the company is balance-sheet insolvent with a £200,000 net deficit. Rather than seeking advice, the directors continue trading, hoping a large contract will materialise. The contract falls through in June. By the time Pearl enters liquidation in August, the net deficit has grown to £350,000.

Advise the liquidator on a wrongful trading claim.
Wrongful Trading.answer:
The liquidator has a strong wrongful trading claim under IA 1986 s.214.

Test: The directors are liable if, at some point before the commencement of winding up, they knew or ought to have concluded that there was no reasonable prospect of the company avoiding insolvent liquidation, and failed to take every step to minimise the potential loss to creditors.

When did they know (or ought they to have known)?: The January management accounts showed balance-sheet insolvency and a £200,000 deficit. This is strong evidence that a reasonably diligent director ought to have concluded by January that insolvent liquidation was, at minimum, a real risk — and probably probable. Continuing to trade without seeking insolvency advice (hoping a contract would save the company) is precisely the 'ostrich' behaviour that s.214 targets (Re Produce Marketing Consortium [1989]).

Contribution: The quantum of any contribution order is the increase in the net deficiency caused by the continued trading from the date when they ought to have recognised the position. If that date is January: £350,000 − £200,000 = £150,000. The directors could be ordered to contribute £150,000 to the company's assets.

Defence: The directors could avoid liability by showing they took every step to minimise creditor losses from the critical date — e.g. seeking insolvency advice, halting new credit, reducing expenditure. Doing nothing does not satisfy this.
Wrongful Trading.summary_line: IA 1986 S.214 · KNEW OR OUGHT TO KNOW · INCREASE IN NET DEFICIENCY · RE PRODUCE MARKETING · EVERY STEP TO MINIMISE LOSSES

Employment Status.front: What are the key tests used to determine whether someone is an employee (rather than self-employed)?
Employment Status.answer: Courts apply a multi-factor test — no single test is decisive. Key indicators of employee status include: (1) Mutuality of obligation — the employer is obliged to offer work and the worker is obliged to accept it; (2) Control — the employer controls how, when and where the work is done; (3) Personal service — the worker must perform the work personally (no right to send a substitute); (4) Integration — the worker is integrated into the employer's organisation. Indicators of self-employment include: the right to send a substitute; provision of own equipment; risk of financial loss; multiple clients. The label the parties apply is not conclusive.
Employment Status.summary_line: MULTI-FACTOR TEST · MUTUALITY · CONTROL · PERSONAL SERVICE · INTEGRATION · LABEL NOT CONCLUSIVE

Employment Status.front: Ready Mixed Concrete (South East) Ltd v Minister of Pensions and National Insurance [1968]
Employment Status.issue: Whether owner-drivers who delivered concrete under contracts labelled as 'contracts of carriage' were employees or independent contractors for the purposes of National Insurance contributions.
Employment Status.rule: A contract of service (employment) exists if three conditions are met: (1) the servant agrees to provide work and skill in return for a wage; (2) the servant agrees — expressly or impliedly — that in performing that service they will be subject to the employer's control to a sufficient degree; and (3) the other provisions of the contract are consistent with its being a contract of service. The label the parties attach is not conclusive.
Employment Status.application: The owner-drivers owned their vehicles, bore their own costs and risk, could substitute another driver, and were treated as self-employed in the contract. MacKenna J held they were not employees: despite some features of control, the freedom to provide a substitute and the economic risk they bore were inconsistent with employee status.
Employment Status.conclusion: The leading formulation of the test for employment status. Applied across employment and tax law. The three-part test (consideration, control, consistency) provides the structural framework, supplemented by a multi-factor analysis of the overall relationship. The label the parties use is not determinative.
Employment Status.summary_line: EMPLOYMENT STATUS · THREE-PART TEST · CONSIDERATION + CONTROL + CONSISTENCY · SUBSTITUTE CLAUSE · LABEL NOT DETERMINATIVE · MCKENNA J

Employment Status.front:
River Digital Ltd engages Stan as a 'freelance developer'. Stan works exclusively for River Digital, five days a week, at River's offices, using River's equipment. Stan's contract states he is self-employed and must invoice for his work. Stan has no right to send a substitute. River can direct how and when Stan does his work.

What is Stan's likely employment status and why does it matter?
Employment Status.answer:
Stan is almost certainly an employee, despite the 'self-employed' label.

Employment status is determined by the reality of the relationship, not the label the parties attach (Ready Mixed Concrete [1968]). The courts apply a multi-factor test. Here:

Control: River controls how, when and where Stan works. This strongly points to employment.

Personal service: Stan has no right to send a substitute — he must do the work himself. This is a strong indicator of employment (the right to substitute is inconsistent with a contract of service).

Mutuality of obligation: Stan works exclusively for River, five days a week. River provides work; Stan is obliged to perform it. This suggests employment.

Equipment / integration: River provides equipment and Stan works at River's offices. Stan is integrated into River's business.

The 'self-employed' label and invoice arrangements are not determinative.

Why it matters: If Stan is an employee, River must: deduct PAYE and NIC; provide employment rights (unfair dismissal protection after 2 years, statutory sick pay, holiday pay, minimum wage, pension auto-enrolment). River may also face HMRC back-tax and penalty exposure for treating Stan as self-employed. Stan can bring Employment Tribunal claims.
Employment Status.summary_line: EMPLOYMENT STATUS · CONTROL · PERSONAL SERVICE · MUTUAL OBLIGATION · LABEL NOT DETERMINATIVE · PAYE + NIC + EMPLOYMENT RIGHTS

Employment Status.front: Going deeper: Is the tripartite employment status framework (employee / worker / independent contractor) in English law fit for purpose in the modern gig economy, and what reforms have been proposed?
Employment Status.answer:
THE CURRENT FRAMEWORK
English employment law recognises three categories: (1) employees — full employment rights (unfair dismissal, redundancy pay, etc.); (2) workers — an intermediate category with rights to minimum wage, holiday pay, and pension auto-enrolment, but not unfair dismissal or redundancy pay; (3) independent contractors — minimal statutory protections. The categories are determined by case-law tests (Ready Mixed Concrete [1968]; Autoclenz v Belcher [2011]) rather than simple bright-line rules.

THE GIG ECONOMY CHALLENGE
Platform-based work (Uber, Deliveroo, Amazon Flex) has tested the tripartite framework severely. In Uber BV v Aslam [2021] (SC), the Supreme Court held Uber drivers were 'workers', not independent contractors — notwithstanding contractual terms describing them as self-employed. The Court used the purposive Autoclenz approach: the written terms are not determinative if they do not reflect the reality of the relationship. This extended the practical scope of worker status significantly, but did not grant full employment rights.

THE ACADEMIC CRITIQUE
Freedland and Kountouris ('The Legal Construction of Personal Work Relations' [2011]) argue the binary employee/non-employee divide is historically contingent and should be replaced by a 'personal work relation' concept that grants all personal service workers a baseline of rights, scaled by the degree of dependence. Davidov ('A Purposive Approach to Labour Law' [2016]) similarly argues that the purpose of labour law — protecting economically dependent workers — should drive status classification, not formalistic contractual analysis.

REFORM PROPOSALS
The Taylor Review ('Good Work' [2017]) recommended: (a) renaming 'workers' as 'dependent contractors'; (b) creating a clearer legislative definition of employment status; (c) reversing the burden of proof (presumption of employment unless employer proves otherwise). The Government accepted the Taylor Review's direction but has been slow to legislate. The Employment Rights Bill (⚠️ verify current legislative position) proposes reforms to employment status and worker rights. The EU's Platform Work Directive (2024) creates a rebuttable presumption of employment for platform workers — England post-Brexit is not bound but may face pressure to align.

OPEN QUESTION
Should England adopt a single employment status (all personal service workers as 'workers') or retain the tripartite distinction? The gig economy cases suggest courts can adapt existing law — but only at the cost of litigation and uncertainty. Legislative clarity is overdue.
Employment Status.summary_line: TRIPARTITE STATUS · UBER v ASLAM [2021] · AUTOCLENZ · TAYLOR REVIEW 2017 · FREEDLAND/KOUNTOURIS · GIG ECONOMY · EU PLATFORM DIRECTIVE · REFORM

Redundancy.front: How is a statutory redundancy payment calculated?
Redundancy.answer: Statutory redundancy pay (ERA 1996 s.162) is based on: age, length of service (up to a maximum of 20 years), and weekly pay (capped at a statutory weekly pay cap — ⚠️ verify the current cap). The calculation: for each year of qualifying service — 1.5 weeks' pay for each year aged 41 and over; 1 week's pay for each year aged 22–40; 0.5 week's pay for each year under 22. The employee must have at least 2 years' continuous employment to qualify for a statutory redundancy payment.
Redundancy.summary_line: ERA 1996 S.162 · AGE + SERVICE + WEEKLY PAY · 1.5/1/0.5 WEEKS · MAX 20 YEARS · ⚠️ VERIFY WEEKLY PAY CAP

Redundancy.front:
Valley Press Ltd decides to close its Manchester office and centralise all operations in London. It proposes to make 8 employees redundant. It gives them one week's notice each (as stated in their contracts) and pays them their statutory redundancy entitlement. Three of the employees have served 12, 15 and 22 years respectively.

Identify any issues with the redundancy process.
Redundancy.answer:
There are two significant issues:

1. Collective consultation: Where an employer proposes to make 20 or more redundancies within 90 days at one establishment, it must collectively consult with appropriate employee representatives for a minimum of 30 days (or 45 days if 100 or more redundancies are proposed) under the Trade Union and Labour Relations (Consolidation) Act 1992. Although only 8 employees are affected here (below the 20-person threshold), Valley Press should check whether it has made or plans to make other redundancies elsewhere that might aggregate to 20+.

2. Statutory redundancy pay — 20-year cap: Under ERA 1996 s.162, statutory redundancy pay is capped at 20 years' service. The employee with 22 years' service will receive the same statutory pay as the employee with 20 years — the extra 2 years counts for nothing.

3. Notice periods: One week's contractual notice may be shorter than the statutory minimum. ERA 1996 s.86 provides minimum statutory notice of one week per year of employment (up to 12 weeks). Employees with 12 and 15 years' service are entitled to at least 12 weeks' statutory notice each — one week is a significant shortfall. Valley Press is in breach of s.86 and those employees can claim wrongful dismissal for the notice shortfall.

Individual consultation and fair selection processes should also be confirmed.
Redundancy.summary_line: COLLECTIVE CONSULTATION ≥20 REDUNDANCIES · STATUTORY NOTICE S.86 · 1 WEEK PER YEAR · REDUNDANCY PAY CAPPED 20 YEARS · WRONGFUL DISMISSAL

TUPE.front:
Westgate Cleaning Ltd has a contract to clean a chain of hotels. The hotel chain decides to bring cleaning in-house, terminating Westgate's contract. Westgate employs 15 cleaners assigned to that contract. The hotel chain does not want to take on any of the cleaners.

Does TUPE apply and what are the consequences if the hotel chain dismisses the cleaners?
TUPE.answer:
TUPE is likely to apply under the 'service provision change' category.

TUPE 2006 applies not only to traditional business transfers but also to service provision changes — where activities carried out by a contractor are brought back in-house by the client. The hotel chain bringing cleaning in-house from Westgate is a paradigm service provision change, provided: the activities are essentially the same (cleaning — yes); and the employees are organised into a grouping assigned to the contract (yes — 15 cleaners assigned to this contract).

Effect of TUPE: If TUPE applies, the 15 cleaners automatically transfer to the hotel chain on their existing terms and conditions. The hotel chain cannot simply refuse to take them on.

Dismissal: If the hotel chain dismisses the cleaners, or if Westgate dismisses them in connection with the transfer, those dismissals are automatically unfair under TUPE reg. 7 — unless there is an 'economic, technical or organisational' (ETO) reason entailing changes in the workforce. The hotel chain's wish not to employ cleaners is not an ETO reason.

Liability: Claims for automatically unfair dismissal run against the hotel chain (as the transferee). Westgate may also face claims if it dismissed before transfer.

Advice: The hotel chain must accept the transferring employees or face significant Employment Tribunal liability.
TUPE.summary_line: TUPE 2006 · SERVICE PROVISION CHANGE · AUTO TRANSFER · EXISTING TERMS · AUTO UNFAIR DISMISSAL · ETO DEFENCE · TRANSFEREE LIABLE

Unfair Dismissal.front: What qualifying conditions must an employee meet to bring an unfair dismissal claim?
Unfair Dismissal.answer: To bring an unfair dismissal claim under ERA 1996: (1) the claimant must be an employee (not a worker or self-employed); (2) the employee must have at least 2 years' continuous employment (qualifying period); (3) the employee must have been dismissed (actual dismissal, constructive dismissal, or non-renewal of a fixed-term contract). No qualifying period applies for dismissals in automatically unfair categories (e.g. whistleblowing, pregnancy, assertion of statutory rights). Claims must be brought to the Employment Tribunal within 3 months of the effective date of termination (subject to ACAS early conciliation).
Unfair Dismissal.summary_line: ERA 1996 · EMPLOYEE · 2 YEARS' CONTINUOUS EMPLOYMENT · 3 MONTH LIMIT · NO QUALIFYING PERIOD FOR AUTO UNFAIR

Unfair Dismissal.front: What are the five potentially fair reasons for dismissal under the Employment Rights Act 1996?
Unfair Dismissal.answer: Under ERA 1996 s.98, a dismissal may be fair if the employer can show one of five potentially fair reasons: (1) Capability — the employee lacks the skill, aptitude, health or qualifications to do the job; (2) Conduct — misconduct (e.g. dishonesty, repeated lateness); (3) Redundancy — genuine redundancy situation; (4) Statutory bar — continuing employment would contravene a statutory requirement (e.g. loss of driving licence for a driver); (5) Some other substantial reason (SOSR) — a residual category for other genuinely compelling reasons (e.g. protecting confidential business information). Even with a fair reason, the employer must follow a fair procedure.
Unfair Dismissal.summary_line: ERA 1996 S.98 · CAPABILITY · CONDUCT · REDUNDANCY · STATUTORY BAR · SOSR · FAIR PROCEDURE ALSO REQUIRED

Unfair Dismissal.front:
Tara has worked for Upland Ltd for 3 years as a sales manager. She is dismissed after one incident in which she shouted at a client during a phone call. Her employer did not carry out any investigation, did not warn Tara before dismissal, and did not offer her the right to appeal.

Advise Tara on an unfair dismissal claim.
Unfair Dismissal.answer:
Tara has strong grounds for an unfair dismissal claim under ERA 1996.

Qualifying conditions: Tara has 3 years' continuous employment — well above the 2-year qualifying threshold. She has been dismissed.

Fair reason: The employer may argue the dismissal was for conduct — a potentially fair reason under ERA 1996 s.98. Shouting at a client could constitute misconduct.

Fair procedure: Even with a fair reason, the employer must follow a fair procedure. The ACAS Code of Practice on Disciplinary and Grievance Procedures requires: a proper investigation of the alleged misconduct; notification to the employee of the allegations; a disciplinary hearing at which the employee can put their case (and be accompanied); and a right of appeal. None of these steps were followed.

Was summary dismissal reasonable?: A single incident of shouting, with no prior warnings, is unlikely to justify summary (instant) dismissal without at least a hearing.
