The employer must act within the range of reasonable responses — dismissal for a first misconduct incident of this nature without process will rarely satisfy that standard.

Likely outcome: The dismissal is likely to be unfair both on substance (disproportionate for a first incident) and procedure. Compensation will be reduced by any contributory fault and by Tara's failure to mitigate. A 25% uplift to any award can be applied for unreasonable failure to follow the ACAS Code.

Time limit: Tara must submit her ET1 claim form within 3 months of the effective date of termination (subject to ACAS early conciliation).
Unfair Dismissal.summary_line: ERA 1996 · UNFAIR DISMISSAL · 2 YEARS' SERVICE · CONDUCT REASON · ACAS CODE · NO INVESTIGATION OR HEARING · UNFAIR PROCEDURE · 3-MONTH LIMIT

Asset Purchase vs Share Purchase.topic: Sale and Purchase of a Business
Asset Purchase vs Share Purchase.front: What are the key differences between an asset purchase and a share purchase from a buyer's perspective?
Asset Purchase vs Share Purchase.answer: Asset purchase — buyer acquires specific identified assets and liabilities; can leave behind unwanted liabilities; TUPE applies to employees (they transfer automatically); VAT implications on transfer of assets (though transfer of a going concern may be VAT-exempt); SDLT on land included; stamp duty on shares if shares form part of assets acquired is not applicable (stamp duty on the share transfer itself if shares are being purchased). Share purchase — buyer acquires the target company with all its assets AND liabilities (known and unknown); buyer inherits all historical liabilities; employees do not transfer under TUPE (they stay with the company); stamp duty payable at 0.5% of consideration on share transfers. Buyers generally prefer asset purchases to limit liability exposure; sellers often prefer share sales for tax reasons (BADR).
Asset Purchase vs Share Purchase.summary_line: ASSET = CHOSEN ASSETS/LIABILITIES · TUPE APPLIES · SHARE = ALL LIABILITIES · NO TUPE · STAMP DUTY 0.5% ON SHARES

Asset Purchase vs Share Purchase.topic: Sale and Purchase of a Business
Asset Purchase vs Share Purchase.front: What is the difference between a warranty and an indemnity in a business sale agreement?
Asset Purchase vs Share Purchase.answer: A warranty is a contractual statement of fact about the business (e.g. 'there are no pending claims against the company'). If a warranty is breached, the buyer's remedy is damages — the buyer must prove loss and the normal rules of mitigation apply. An indemnity is a promise to pay a specific sum if a specified event occurs (e.g. 'the seller will indemnify the buyer for any liability arising from the pre-completion tax period'). The buyer does not need to prove loss — payment is triggered by the event. Indemnities are often used for known (or suspected) specific risks; warranties provide general assurance about the state of the business. A disclosure letter from the seller qualifies the warranties and limits liability.
Asset Purchase vs Share Purchase.summary_line: WARRANTY = STATEMENT + DAMAGES REMEDY · INDEMNITY = PROMISE TO PAY ON EVENT · DISCLOSURE LETTER QUALIFIES WARRANTIES

Asset Purchase vs Share Purchase.topic: Sale and Purchase of a Business
Asset Purchase vs Share Purchase.front: What does TUPE stand for and when does it apply on a business transfer?
Asset Purchase vs Share Purchase.answer: TUPE stands for the Transfer of Undertakings (Protection of Employment) Regulations 2006. TUPE applies where there is a 'relevant transfer' — a transfer of a business or undertaking (or part) from one employer to another where the business retains its identity. Effect: employees who are assigned to the transferring part of the business automatically transfer to the new employer on their existing terms and conditions. Dismissal connected to the transfer is automatically unfair unless there is an 'economic, technical or organisational' (ETO) reason entailing changes in the workforce. TUPE applies to asset purchases; it does not apply to share purchases (employees remain employed by the same company).
Asset Purchase vs Share Purchase.summary_line: TUPE 2006 · ASSET PURCHASES · AUTO TRANSFER ON EXISTING TERMS · AUTO UNFAIR DISMISSAL · ETO DEFENCE · NOT SHARE PURCHASES

Asset Purchase vs Share Purchase.topic: Sale and Purchase of a Business
Asset Purchase vs Share Purchase.front:
Xavier wants to buy a catering business from its owner, Yasmin. The business is run through a company, Zest Catering Ltd. Xavier has discovered that Zest has an outstanding employment tribunal claim from a former employee and a disputed tax liability. Xavier wants to acquire the business and its assets but not these historic liabilities.

Advise Xavier on how he might structure the acquisition.
Asset Purchase vs Share Purchase.answer:
Xavier should structure the acquisition as an asset purchase rather than a share purchase.

Share purchase: If Xavier bought Yasmin's shares in Zest Catering Ltd, he would acquire the company with all its liabilities — both known (the ET claim and tax dispute) and unknown. He cannot 'cherry-pick' which liabilities to take on. The company simply changes hands with its full legal history.

Asset purchase: By purchasing specific assets (equipment, goodwill, contracts, stock, IP) directly from Zest Ltd, Xavier can choose which assets and which liabilities (if any) to assume. He can exclude the ET claim and tax dispute — those remain with Zest Ltd (Yasmin's company). Xavier's new vehicle (a new company or himself personally) takes on a clean slate.

TUPE: If employees are assigned to the catering business, TUPE will apply on an asset purchase — the employees transfer automatically to Xavier on existing terms. Xavier cannot avoid TUPE by choosing an asset structure.

Tax: An asset purchase triggers SDLT on any land included and VAT on non-TOGC asset transfers (though a transfer of a going concern (TOGC) may be VAT-exempt if conditions are met). Yasmin's tax position on a share sale (potentially qualifying for BADR) may mean she pushes for a higher price on an asset sale.

Recommendation: Asset purchase, with full due diligence warranties on the transferred assets and indemnities for any hidden liabilities.
Asset Purchase vs Share Purchase.summary_line: ASSET PURCHASE · CHOSEN ASSETS/LIABILITIES · HISTORIC LIABILITIES STAY WITH COMPANY · TUPE APPLIES · TOGC VAT EXEMPTION · SHARE SALE TAX POSITION

TUPE and Business Transfers.topic: Sale and Purchase of a Business
TUPE and Business Transfers.front:
Dex Ltd is acquiring the catering division of Emu Group plc by way of asset purchase. The catering division has 40 employees. Dex wants to renegotiate all employment terms post-transfer (higher hours, lower pay) as a condition of the acquisition.

Advise Dex on the TUPE implications.
TUPE and Business Transfers.answer:
TUPE 2006 applies to this asset purchase as a relevant transfer — the catering division is an organised grouping of employees dedicated to catering activities, and the business retains its identity.

Automatic transfer: All 40 employees transfer automatically to Dex on their existing terms and conditions. Dex cannot select which employees to take.

Variation of terms: TUPE reg. 4(4) provides that any purported variation of the terms of employment is void if the sole or principal reason for the variation is the transfer itself. Dex's proposed changes — lower pay, higher hours — are clearly transfer-connected. They are therefore void under TUPE, and Dex cannot lawfully implement them simply because it acquired the business.

Exception — ETO reason: Dex can vary terms if there is an economic, technical or organisational (ETO) reason entailing changes in the workforce — but this requires genuine workforce changes (e.g. redundancies, changes to job functions), not just a desire to cut employment costs. Cost-saving alone is not an ETO reason.

If Dex dismisses employees who refuse the new terms, those dismissals will be automatically unfair (TUPE reg. 7) unless an ETO reason exists.

Practical advice: Dex should negotiate the price to reflect the employment terms it is inheriting, and consider whether any genuine restructuring (which might qualify as an ETO reason) is planned. Changes should only be made after proper legal advice and, ideally, through collective agreement with employee representatives.
TUPE and Business Transfers.summary_line: TUPE 2006 · RELEVANT TRANSFER · AUTO TRANSFER ON EXISTING TERMS · VARIATION VOID REG.4(4) · ETO REASON EXCEPTION · AUTO UNFAIR DISMISSAL

TUPE and Business Transfers.topic: Sale and Purchase of a Business
TUPE and Business Transfers.front: Going deeper: Does TUPE 2006 strike the right balance between protecting employees on business transfers and facilitating efficient commercial restructuring? Is the prohibition on harmonising terms post-transfer too restrictive?
TUPE and Business Transfers.answer:
THE TUPE FRAMEWORK
TUPE 2006 implements the EU Acquired Rights Directive (2001/23/EC). It provides that on a relevant transfer, employees automatically transfer on their existing terms and conditions, and dismissals connected to the transfer are automatically unfair (reg. 7) unless there is an ETO reason. Reg. 4(4) voids any variation of terms connected to the transfer, even if agreed by the employee.

THE HARMONISATION PROBLEM
A persistent commercial problem is the prohibition on post-transfer harmonisation. Transferee employers frequently wish to integrate transferred employees into a common employment framework — equalising terms across a workforce is commercially rational and often in employees' interests (where existing terms are inferior). However, reg. 4(4) makes any variation void if the reason is 'the transfer'. In Capita Hartshead [2012], the EAT held that even variations agreed years after a transfer can be void if the transfer was still a reason for the change. This creates a potentially indefinite constraint on employer flexibility.

THE ACADEMIC AND PRACTITIONER DEBATE
McMullen ('TUPE 2006: The New Regime' [2006]) and Millward argue that the prohibition on harmonisation frustrates sensible workforce integration and increases the cost of acquisitions — potentially deterring beneficial business transfers. Hepple and others counter that the prohibition is essential: without it, employers would simply harmonise down to the lowest common denominator, eliminating the protection the Directive was designed to provide.

POST-BREXIT POSITION
Post-Brexit, the UK is no longer bound to implement the Acquired Rights Directive. The Government's 2023 consultation on TUPE proposed allowing smaller employers to consult directly with employees rather than through elected representatives. ⚠️ Verify whether any amendments to TUPE have been made since 2023. The Employment Rights Bill (⚠️ verify current status) may further modify the regime.

COMPARATIVE NOTE
German law (Betriebsübergang — s.613a BGB) has similar protections but allows harmonisation after one year where a collective agreement applies. French law goes further, treating transferred employees as if they were employed under the transferee's collective agreement from day one. The English absolute prohibition on connected variations may be uniquely strict.

OPEN QUESTION
Should English law introduce a 'sunset clause' — allowing harmonisation of transferred terms after a defined period (e.g. two or three years), subject to individual employee consent? This would balance continuity of protection with commercial flexibility.
TUPE and Business Transfers.summary_line: TUPE 2006 · REG.4(4) VARIATION VOID · HARMONISATION PROBLEM · CAPITA HARTSHEAD · POST-BREXIT · MCMULLEN · GERMAN BGB · SUNSET CLAUSE PROPOSAL

Warranties and Indemnities.topic: Sale and Purchase of a Business
Warranties and Indemnities.front:
Aaron is buying all the shares in Baker Widgets Ltd from Carol. The share purchase agreement contains warranties that there are no outstanding litigation claims and that all tax has been properly paid. After completion, Aaron discovers an undisclosed HMRC tax investigation and a supplier claim, neither of which was disclosed by Carol.

Advise Aaron on his remedies.
Warranties and Indemnities.answer:
Aaron has a breach of warranty claim against Carol for both matters.

The warranty that all tax has been properly paid, and the warranty of no outstanding litigation claims, were both false at the date of the agreement. Carol should have either disclosed these matters against the warranties in a disclosure letter (which would have qualified the warranties and limited her liability), or ensured they were resolved before completion.

Breach of warranty — remedy: Damages. Aaron must prove his loss — the diminution in value of the shares caused by the undisclosed matters. He must also mitigate his loss. The amount recoverable will reflect what Aaron would have paid had he known the true position (likely a lower price, or he might have walked away). Normal contractual principles of remoteness apply.

Limitation: The SPA will typically contain a cap on Carol's liability (e.g. limited to the purchase price) and a time limit for bringing warranty claims (often 18 months to 2 years for general warranties, 4–7 years for tax). Aaron should check these provisions urgently.

Indemnity: If the SPA included a specific tax indemnity (covering pre-completion tax liabilities), Aaron has a more direct route — he can claim the full cost of the HMRC liability from Carol without needing to prove loss. Aaron should check whether the SPA contains a tax covenant or indemnity.
Warranties and Indemnities.summary_line: BREACH OF WARRANTY · DAMAGES REMEDY · PROVE LOSS · DISCLOSURE LETTER DEFENCE · INDEMNITY CLAIM · CAP AND TIME LIMITS IN SPA

Filing and Disclosure Obligations.front: What is the confirmation statement and when must it be filed?
Filing and Disclosure Obligations.answer: The confirmation statement (Form CS01) replaced the annual return in 2016. Every company must file a confirmation statement at Companies House at least once every 12 months, confirming that the information held at Companies House about the company is up to date (CA 2006 s.853A). The confirmation statement confirms details including: registered office; directors and secretary; shareholders and share capital; SIC codes; PSC (persons with significant control) information. It must be filed within 14 days of the end of the review period. Failure to file is a criminal offence and can lead to the company being struck off.
Filing and Disclosure Obligations.summary_line: CONFIRMATION STATEMENT · ANNUAL · CA 2006 S.853A · 14 DAYS AFTER REVIEW PERIOD · PSC · FAILURE = CRIMINAL OFFENCE

Filing and Disclosure Obligations.front: What is the PSC register and who qualifies as a person with significant control?
Filing and Disclosure Obligations.answer: Companies are required to identify, record, and disclose persons with significant control (PSC) over the company (CA 2006 Part 21A). A PSC is an individual (or registrable relevant legal entity) who: holds more than 25% of shares; holds more than 25% of voting rights; has the right to appoint or remove a majority of directors; has the right to exercise, or actually exercises, significant influence or control over the company; exercises significant influence or control through a trust or firm. The PSC register must be maintained by the company and information is filed at Companies House (publicly available).
Filing and Disclosure Obligations.summary_line: CA 2006 PART 21A · PSC · >25% SHARES OR VOTES · APPOINT MAJORITY DIRECTORS · SIGNIFICANT CONTROL · PUBLIC REGISTER

Filing and Disclosure Obligations.front: What are the accounting and audit requirements for a private company and when does a small company exemption apply?
Filing and Disclosure Obligations.answer: All companies must prepare annual accounts and file them at Companies House. A company qualifies as 'small' if it meets at least two of: turnover ≤ £10.2m; balance sheet total ≤ £5.1m; employees ≤ 50. Small companies can file abbreviated accounts (only a balance sheet). A company is exempt from statutory audit if it qualifies as small — unless it is a subsidiary of a group that is not small, a public company, or in certain regulated sectors. Micro-entities (turnover ≤ £632k, balance sheet ≤ £316k, employees ≤ 10) have even simpler reporting. ⚠️ Verify the current thresholds — these are periodically updated.
Filing and Disclosure Obligations.summary_line: SMALL CO THRESHOLDS · ⚠️ VERIFY FIGURES · ABBREVIATED ACCOUNTS · AUDIT EXEMPTION · MICRO-ENTITY REGIME

Business Law & Practice Traps.front:
⚠️ TRAP CARD

Aaron is the sole director and sole shareholder of Bright Ltd. The company enters into a contract and then defaults. Aaron's creditor sues Aaron personally, arguing that because Aaron controls every aspect of the company and the company was set up purely to run his business, the corporate veil should be pierced and Aaron should be personally liable.
Business Law & Practice Traps.issue: The Trap: A sole director/shareholder who controls every aspect of a company can have the corporate veil pierced — control and sole ownership demonstrate the company is merely Aaron's alter ego.
Business Law & Practice Traps.rule: The Reality: Salomon v Salomon & Co Ltd [1897] established that a company is a separate legal entity from its members and directors, regardless of how complete the individual's control is. The fact that Aaron is the sole director and sole shareholder — precisely the situation in Salomon itself — does not justify piercing the veil. The veil is pierced only in exceptional circumstances: fraud, sham, or specific statutory provision. Control alone is never sufficient.
Business Law & Practice Traps.application: Why the Distractor Fails: The scenario is constructed to mirror Salomon's own facts (sole controller, company as vehicle for personal business) and then suggest those facts justify piercing. This is the exact opposite of Salomon's holding. The House of Lords in Salomon upheld the veil precisely on facts where the company was effectively one man's business. The 'alter ego' argument without fraud or sham fails.
Business Law & Practice Traps.conclusion: The Key Rule: Salomon — a company is a separate legal entity even where one person controls it entirely. Control alone never pierces the veil. Piercing requires fraud, sham, or specific statutory authority.
Business Law & Practice Traps.summary_line: SALOMON · SEPARATE LEGAL PERSONALITY · VEIL NOT PIERCED BY CONTROL ALONE · FRAUD OR SHAM REQUIRED · SOLE DIRECTOR SHAREHOLDER · ALTER EGO INSUFFICIENT

Business Law & Practice Traps.front:
⚠️ TRAP CARD

Brenda and Colin run a catering business together, sharing profits equally and making decisions jointly. They have never signed any written agreement and have never registered a partnership. Brenda argues they cannot be partners because there is no written partnership agreement and the partnership has not been formally registered.
Business Law & Practice Traps.issue: The Trap: A partnership requires a formal written agreement and registration to exist — without these, Brenda and Colin are merely co-workers sharing profits, not partners.
Business Law & Practice Traps.rule: The Reality: Under s.1(1) Partnership Act 1890, a partnership is the relation which subsists between persons carrying on a business in common with a view of profit. No written agreement is required — a partnership can arise from conduct alone. Registration is not a requirement for a general partnership (unlike an LLP). Brenda and Colin are almost certainly partners under the PA 1890 by virtue of their conduct.
Business Law & Practice Traps.application: Why the Distractor Fails: The scenario exploits the assumption that partnerships, like companies, require formal registration or a written document to exist. This is wrong for general partnerships. The PA 1890 test is purely functional — if the parties carry on business in common with a view to profit, a partnership exists regardless of what they call themselves or whether they have any paperwork.
Business Law & Practice Traps.conclusion: The Key Rule: A partnership under PA 1890 s.1 requires no written agreement and no registration. It arises from conduct. Profit-sharing is prima facie evidence of partnership (s.2 PA 1890).
Business Law & Practice Traps.summary_line: PARTNERSHIP · PA 1890 S.1 · NO WRITING REQUIRED · NO REGISTRATION REQUIRED · CONDUCT SUFFICIENT · CARRYING ON BUSINESS IN COMMON · PROFIT SHARING PRIMA FACIE EVIDENCE

Business Law & Practice Traps.front:
⚠️ TRAP CARD

Delta Ltd has three shareholders. Under s.168 Companies Act 2006, an ordinary resolution is required to remove a director. The company's articles give one director, Edward, three votes per share he holds on any resolution to remove him as director. The other shareholders together hold enough shares to pass an ordinary resolution. They argue s.168 guarantees them the power to remove Edward by ordinary resolution, overriding the weighted voting rights in the articles.
Business Law & Practice Traps.issue: The Trap: Section 168 CA 2006 gives shareholders the statutory right to remove a director by ordinary resolution — a provision of the Companies Act cannot be overridden by the articles, so the weighted voting rights are ineffective.
Business Law & Practice Traps.rule: The Reality: The articles can validly confer weighted voting rights on a director in relation to a resolution to remove them, and such rights are effective even against s.168. This was established in Bushell v Faith [1970], where the House of Lords upheld a provision giving a director three votes per share on a removal resolution. Section 168 requires only an ordinary majority — it does not prevent the articles from adjusting the voting weight of shares to make that majority harder to achieve.
Business Law & Practice Traps.application: Why the Distractor Fails: The scenario relies on the intuition that a statutory right (s.168) must override articles. But s.168 operates on the number of votes cast, not on the weight of individual votes. Bushell v Faith confirmed that adjusting vote weight in the articles is a legitimate way to entrench a director against removal — it does not violate s.168, it simply makes the required majority arithmetically harder to achieve with the existing shareholding.
Business Law & Practice Traps.conclusion: The Key Rule: Bushell v Faith — weighted voting rights in the articles are valid and effective against a s.168 removal resolution. Section 168 CA 2006 does not prevent articles from allocating enhanced voting rights to a director on their own removal.
Business Law & Practice Traps.summary_line: DIRECTOR REMOVAL · S.168 CA 2006 · BUSHELL V FAITH · WEIGHTED VOTING RIGHTS VALID · ARTICLES CAN ENTRENCH DIRECTOR · ORDINARY RESOLUTION ON ADJUSTED VOTES

Business Law & Practice Traps.front:
⚠️ TRAP CARD

Felicity assigns her lease to George in 2023. The lease was granted in 1998. The landlord requires George to enter into an authorised guarantee agreement (AGA) guaranteeing Felicity's obligations. George's solicitor advises that AGAs only apply to leases granted after 1 January 1996 and therefore Felicity cannot be required to provide one.
Business Law & Practice Traps.issue: The Trap: AGAs apply to post-1995 leases and the lease was granted in 1998 — so an AGA can be required, and the solicitor's advice about post-1996 leases is correct.
Business Law & Practice Traps.rule: The Reality: The solicitor's advice contains a critical error in identifying which party provides the AGA. Under the Landlord and Tenant (Covenants) Act 1995, on assignment of a post-1995 lease, the outgoing tenant (Felicity, the assignor) may be required to enter into an AGA guaranteeing the performance of the incoming tenant (George, the assignee). George himself cannot be required to provide an AGA — he is the assignee, not the assignor. The AGA obligation falls on Felicity, not George.
Business Law & Practice Traps.application: Why the Distractor Fails: The scenario correctly identifies that the 1995 Act applies (post-1995 lease) but then confuses which party provides the AGA. Students commonly assume the incoming tenant (George) gives the guarantee. Under LT(C)A 1995, the AGA is provided by the outgoing tenant (Felicity) guaranteeing the assignee. George cannot be compelled to guarantee Felicity's prior obligations — he has no ongoing liability for them under the 1995 Act regime.
Business Law & Practice Traps.conclusion: The Key Rule: On assignment of a post-1995 lease, it is the outgoing tenant (assignor) who may be required to provide an AGA guaranteeing the assignee's performance — not the incoming tenant (assignee).
Business Law & Practice Traps.summary_line: AGA · LT(C)A 1995 · POST-1995 LEASE · OUTGOING TENANT PROVIDES AGA · NOT INCOMING TENANT · ASSIGNOR GUARANTEES ASSIGNEE · PRE-1996 LEASES DIFFERENT REGIME

Business Law & Practice Traps.front:
⚠️ TRAP CARD

Haven Ltd has 100 shares in issue held by five shareholders. A written resolution is proposed. Three shareholders holding 60 shares return their votes in favour. Two shareholders holding 40 shares do not respond at all. The company secretary announces the resolution has passed with 60% in favour, noting that the non-respondents' shares drop out of the denominator since they did not participate.
Business Law & Practice Traps.issue: The Trap: Non-responding shareholders drop out of the denominator for written resolution purposes — the majority is calculated only among those who actually vote, so 60 out of 60 responding shares (100%) means the resolution passes.
Business Law & Practice Traps.rule: The Reality: For written resolutions under CA 2006, the required majority is calculated by reference to the total eligible voting rights of all members entitled to vote — not just those who respond. Non-respondents do not drop out of the denominator. An ordinary resolution requires a simple majority of all eligible voting rights; a special resolution requires 75% of all eligible voting rights. Here, 60 out of 100 = 60% — which passes an ordinary resolution threshold but not a special resolution threshold. The company secretary's reasoning about dropping non-respondents out is wrong.
Business Law & Practice Traps.application: Why the Distractor Fails: The scenario triggers the assumption that written resolutions work like a show of hands — where only those present vote. But written resolutions are assessed against the total eligible voting rights. The denominator is fixed at the total issued voting shares. This is a common precision trap: students know written resolutions exist but misapply the majority calculation.
Business Law & Practice Traps.conclusion: The Key Rule: Written resolution majorities are calculated against total eligible voting rights — non-respondents do not reduce the denominator. An ordinary written resolution requires more than 50% of total eligible voting rights.
Business Law & Practice Traps.summary_line: WRITTEN RESOLUTION · CA 2006 · DENOMINATOR IS ALL ELIGIBLE MEMBERS · NON-RESPONDENTS DO NOT DROP OUT · ORDINARY MAJORITY OF TOTAL VOTING RIGHTS · SPECIAL RESOLUTION 75%

Business Law & Practice Traps.front:
⚠️ TRAP CARD

Isla is a director of Junction Ltd. She causes the company to enter into a transaction that she personally believes will benefit the company's shareholders by increasing the value of their shares. She is later accused of breaching her s.172 CA 2006 duty. She argues she acted in good faith for the benefit of those who own the company — the shareholders — so she cannot have breached s.172.
Business Law & Practice Traps.issue: The Trap: Section 172 requires directors to act in good faith in the way they consider most likely to promote the success of the company for the benefit of its members — Isla acted to benefit shareholders, so she complied with s.172.
Business Law & Practice Traps.rule: The Reality: Section 172 CA 2006 requires a director to act in the way they consider, in good faith, would be most likely to promote the success of the company — not to promote the personal interests of shareholders as individuals. The company is a separate legal entity. A director who prefers shareholder personal gain over the long-term success of the company may breach s.172, even acting in good faith as to shareholders' wishes. The duty is owed to the company, not to individual members.
Business Law & Practice Traps.application: Why the Distractor Fails: The scenario exploits the conflation of 'the company' with 'its shareholders.' Section 172 uses the language 'for the benefit of its members as a whole' — which refers to the membership collectively as beneficiaries of the company's success, not to advancing individual members' personal financial interests at the expense of the company. Acting for shareholder benefit in a way that harms the company's long-term success breaches s.172.
Business Law & Practice Traps.conclusion: The Key Rule: Section 172 duty is to promote the success of the company — a separate legal entity — not to serve shareholders' personal interests. The duty is owed to the company, not to individual members.
Business Law & Practice Traps.summary_line: S.172 CA 2006 · PROMOTE SUCCESS OF COMPANY · NOT SHAREHOLDERS PERSONALLY · COMPANY IS SEPARATE ENTITY · GOOD FAITH · LONG-TERM SUCCESS · DUTY OWED TO COMPANY

Business Law & Practice Traps.front:
⚠️ TRAP CARD

Kappa Ltd is in financial difficulty. Its directors, knowing the company is insolvent, cause it to repay a loan to a friendly creditor, Lena, six months before the company goes into liquidation. The liquidator challenges the payment as a preference. The directors argue the payment was made under commercial pressure from Lena, who had threatened to wind up the company if not repaid, so there was no desire to prefer.
Business Law & Practice Traps.issue: The Trap: Commercial pressure from a creditor negates the 'desire to prefer' element required for a preference — because the directors were pressured, they lacked the necessary desire and the preference claim fails.
Business Law & Practice Traps.rule: The Reality: Commercial pressure can negate the desire to prefer, but only where the pressure is the genuine reason for the payment. Under s.239 Insolvency Act 1986, a preference requires that the company was influenced by a desire to put the creditor in a better position than they would be in on insolvency. The courts have accepted that genuine commercial pressure — where the payment was made to avoid immediate consequences, not out of a desire to benefit the creditor — can rebut the desire element (Re MC Bacon Ltd [1990]). However, this is a factual question: the pressure must be real and the dominant reason for the payment.
Business Law & Practice Traps.application: Why the Distractor Fails: The scenario is not straightforwardly a trap — commercial pressure is a genuine defence. The trap lies in assuming it automatically succeeds. Re MC Bacon confirms that the desire element can be rebutted by genuine commercial pressure, but courts scrutinise whether the pressure was real or manufactured. A friendly creditor threatening winding up in a way that appears coordinated with the directors to enable repayment before insolvency may not constitute genuine arm's length pressure. The liquidator will investigate the circumstances carefully.
Business Law & Practice Traps.conclusion: The Key Rule: Commercial pressure can negate the desire to prefer under s.239 IA 1986 (Re MC Bacon), but it must be genuine arm's length pressure — not manufactured or coordinated. The court examines the dominant reason for the payment.
Business Law & Practice Traps.summary_line: PREFERENCE · S.239 IA 1986 · DESIRE TO PREFER · COMMERCIAL PRESSURE DEFENCE · RE MC BACON · GENUINE PRESSURE NEGATES DESIRE · FACTUAL QUESTION · NOT AUTOMATIC

Business Law & Practice Traps.front:
⚠️ TRAP CARD

Mega Corp creates a floating charge over its undertaking in favour of its bank on 1 March. On 15 April, Mega Corp goes into administration. The bank argues its floating charge crystallised when the company defaulted on a loan payment in February, before administration — and therefore it became a fixed charge before administration commenced and is not affected by the administration moratorium.
Business Law & Practice Traps.issue: The Trap: A floating charge that crystallises before administration begins converts to a fixed charge — administration of a fixed charge is different from administration of a floating charge, and the bank's security has priority.
Business Law & Practice Traps.rule: The Reality: A floating charge crystallises automatically on the appointment of an administrator — it does not need to have crystallised beforehand to be affected by the administration. Under the Insolvency Act 1986 and the administration regime, the appointment of an administrator itself causes crystallisation of any floating charge. Whether the charge crystallised earlier (on default) or only upon appointment is relevant to other issues but does not affect the fundamental position that administration engages the floating charge regime.
Business Law & Practice Traps.application: Why the Distractor Fails: The scenario introduces a pre-administration default to suggest the charge was already fixed before administration. But the key trap is the appointment of an administrator: this itself crystallises floating charges (Schedule B1 IA 1986). The bank cannot avoid the administration regime simply by arguing prior crystallisation — the administrator's powers apply regardless, and the prescribed part rules and priority regime still operate. The bank's correct protection is a qualifying floating charge holder's right to appoint an administrator.
Business Law & Practice Traps.conclusion: The Key Rule: Appointment of an administrator crystallises any floating charge over the company's assets. A floating charge holder's correct protection is the right to appoint an administrator as a qualifying floating charge holder — not to argue pre-crystallisation.
Business Law & Practice Traps.summary_line: FLOATING CHARGE CRYSTALLISATION · ADMINISTRATION · APPOINTMENT OF ADMINISTRATOR · SCHEDULE B1 IA 1986 · QUALIFYING FLOATING CHARGE HOLDER · PRESCRIBED PART · PRIOR DEFAULT

Business Law & Practice Traps.front:
⚠️ TRAP CARD

Nova Ltd has two equal shareholders who are also its only directors — Owen and Petra. They have fallen out completely and cannot agree on any decisions. The company is deadlocked. Owen petitions for just and equitable winding up under s.122(1)(g) Insolvency Act 1986, arguing that the complete deadlock makes winding up inevitable.
Business Law & Practice Traps.issue: The Trap: A complete deadlock between equal shareholders/directors is sufficient to justify just and equitable winding up — the company simply cannot function, so the court must wind it up.
Business Law & Practice Traps.rule: The Reality: Deadlock alone is not sufficient for just and equitable winding up. The court requires that the petitioner had a legitimate expectation in relation to the company's affairs that has been violated — mere deadlock without a legitimate expectation being defeated does not justify the remedy (Re Westbourne Galleries [1973]; Ebrahimi v Westbourne Galleries [1973]). The concept of legitimate expectation arises from a quasi-partnership relationship — typically where the company was formed on the basis of mutual trust and the petitioner's participation was predicated on that basis.
Business Law & Practice Traps.application: Why the Distractor Fails: The scenario presents deadlock as self-evidently justifying winding up. But Re Westbourne Galleries requires more — the petitioner must show a legitimate expectation (typically from a quasi-partnership relationship) that has been defeated. Equal shareholding and deadlock are relevant context, but Owen must also show that his participation in Nova Ltd was based on mutual understanding and good faith that has broken down. Without this additional element, deadlock is just a management dispute, not grounds for equitable winding up.
Business Law & Practice Traps.conclusion: The Key Rule: Just and equitable winding up under s.122(1)(g) IA 1986 requires more than deadlock — the petitioner must establish a legitimate expectation (typically from a quasi-partnership) that has been defeated (Ebrahimi v Westbourne Galleries).
Business Law & Practice Traps.summary_line: JUST AND EQUITABLE WINDING UP · S.122(1)(G) IA 1986 · DEADLOCK INSUFFICIENT ALONE · LEGITIMATE EXPECTATION · QUASI-PARTNERSHIP · EBRAHIMI V WESTBOURNE GALLERIES · RE WESTBOURNE GALLERIES

Business Law & Practice Traps.front:
⚠️ TRAP CARD

Quinn is a director of Radius Ltd. In January, she becomes aware that the company is heading toward insolvency and cannot avoid it. She immediately resigns as a director. In the subsequent liquidation, the liquidator brings a wrongful trading claim against Quinn under s.214 Insolvency Act 1986 in respect of debts incurred between January and the date she resigned. Quinn argues her resignation absolves her of wrongful trading liability.
Business Law & Practice Traps.issue: The Trap: Resignation as a director severs Quinn's legal responsibilities — she is no longer a director after January and cannot be liable for wrongful trading in respect of events after her resignation.
Business Law & Practice Traps.rule: The Reality: Resignation does not extinguish wrongful trading liability that has already accrued, nor does it necessarily prevent liability continuing to attach for the period before insolvency proceedings. Under s.214 IA 1986, liability attaches to a person who was a director at the time when they knew or ought to have concluded there was no reasonable prospect of avoiding insolvent liquidation. The question is whether Quinn took every step to minimise loss to creditors once she had that knowledge — resignation alone is not a step that minimises creditor loss and does not satisfy the s.214(3) defence.
Business Law & Practice Traps.application: Why the Distractor Fails: The scenario exploits the intuition that resignation = no longer responsible. But wrongful trading liability is assessed by reference to what D knew or ought to have known and what steps were taken — not merely by whether D held office at the date of liquidation. Quinn resigned knowing insolvency was inevitable without taking steps to minimise losses. Resignation to avoid liability is not the same as taking every step to minimise loss to creditors. The liquidator can pursue her for the period when she was a director and had the relevant knowledge.
Business Law & Practice Traps.conclusion: The Key Rule: Resignation does not extinguish wrongful trading liability under s.214 IA 1986. The defence requires every step to minimise loss to creditors — resignation alone does not satisfy this. Liability is assessed from the point of knowledge, not the point of resignation.
Business Law & Practice Traps.summary_line: WRONGFUL TRADING · S.214 IA 1986 · RESIGNATION NO DEFENCE · KNOWLEDGE OF INEVITABLE INSOLVENCY · EVERY STEP TO MINIMISE LOSS · LIQUIDATOR CAN PURSUE · PERIOD OF KNOWLEDGE
